question_text: Whenever a magnet is moved either towards or away from a conducting coil, an emf is induced, the magnitude of which is independent of:

options.text: the strength of the magnetic field
options.html: the strength of the magnetic field

options.text: the speed with which the magnet is moved
options.html: the speed with which the magnet is moved

options.text: the number of turns in the coil
options.html: the number of turns in the coil

options.explanation: Induced emf depends on the total flux {tex}\times{/tex} current per second, i.e., on the strength of the magnet, speed of moving the magnet, number of turns of the coil and area of cross-section of the coil. Only the current is decided by the emf and the resistance. {tex}\therefore{/tex} emf is independent of the resistance of the coil.

explanation: Induced emf depends on the total flux {tex}\times{/tex} current per second, i.e., on the strength of the magnet, speed of moving the magnet, number of turns of the coil and area of cross-section of the coil. Only the current is decided by the emf and the resistance. {tex}\therefore{/tex} emf is independent of the resistance of the coil.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Define the term mutual inductance. Deduce the expression for the mutual inductance of two long coaxial solenoids of the same length having different radii and different number of turns.
  2. The current through an inductor is uniformly increased from zero to 2 A in 40 s. An emf of 5 mV is induced during this period. Find the flux linked with the inductor at {tex}{t}=10 {~s}{/tex}.
question_text: Define the term mutual inductance. Deduce the expression for the mutual inductance of two long coaxial solenoids of the same length having different radii and different number of turns. The current through an inductor is uniformly increased from zero to 2 A in 40 s. An emf of 5 mV is induced during this period. Find the flux linked with the inductor at {tex}{t}=10 {~s}{/tex} .
explanation: Mutual inductance is defined as the induced emf in primary coil when the current in secondary coil changes at the unit rate. Consider two long co-axial solenoids each of length 1. Radius of inner solenoid {tex}S_1{/tex} is {tex}r_1{/tex} and number of turns per unit length is {tex}n_1{/tex} . The corresponding quantities for outer solenoid {tex}S_2{/tex} are {tex}r_2{/tex} and {tex}n_2{/tex} respectively. Let {tex}{N}_1{/tex} and {tex}{N}_2{/tex} be the total number of turns of coils {tex}{S}_1{/tex} and {tex}{S}_2{/tex} respectively. When a current {tex}I_2{/tex} is set up through {tex}S_2{/tex} , it sets up magnetic flux through {tex}{S}_1{/tex} . {tex} {N}_1 \phi_1 ={M}_{12} {I}_2 {/tex} {tex} =\left({n}_1 l\right) \times\left(\pi {r}_1^2\right) \times\left(\mu_0 {n}_2 {I}_2\right) {/tex} {tex} =\mu_0 {n}_1 {n}_2 \pi {r}_1^2 l \ {I}_2 {/tex} {tex} {M}_{12}= \mu_0 {n}_1 {n}_2 \pi {r}_1^2 l={M}_{21} {/tex} {tex} |{e}| ={L} \frac{{dI}}{{dt}} {/tex} {tex} L =\frac{e}{d I / d t} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{2 / 40} {/tex} {tex} =0.1 {H} {/tex} {tex} \phi ={LI} {/tex} {tex} =0.1 \times \frac{2}{40} \times 10 {/tex} {tex} =0.05 {~Wb} {/tex}
solution: Mutual inductance is defined as the induced emf in primary coil when the current in secondary coil changes at the unit rate. Consider two long co-axial solenoids each of length 1. Radius of inner solenoid {tex}S_1{/tex} is {tex}r_1{/tex} and number of turns per unit length is {tex}n_1{/tex} . The corresponding quantities for outer solenoid {tex}S_2{/tex} are {tex}r_2{/tex} and {tex}n_2{/tex} respectively. Let {tex}{N}_1{/tex} and {tex}{N}_2{/tex} be the total number of turns of coils {tex}{S}_1{/tex} and {tex}{S}_2{/tex} respectively. When a current {tex}I_2{/tex} is set up through {tex}S_2{/tex} , it sets up magnetic flux through {tex}{S}_1{/tex} . {tex} {N}_1 \phi_1 ={M}_{12} {I}_2 {/tex} {tex} =\left({n}_1 l\right) \times\left(\pi {r}_1^2\right) \times\left(\mu_0 {n}_2 {I}_2\right) {/tex} {tex} =\mu_0 {n}_1 {n}_2 \pi {r}_1^2 l \ {I}_2 {/tex} {tex} {M}_{12}= \mu_0 {n}_1 {n}_2 \pi {r}_1^2 l={M}_{21} {/tex} {tex} |{e}| ={L} \frac{{dI}}{{dt}} {/tex} {tex} L =\frac{e}{d I / d t} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{2 / 40} {/tex} {tex} =0.1 {H} {/tex} {tex} \phi ={LI} {/tex} {tex} =0.1 \times \frac{2}{40} \times 10 {/tex} {tex} =0.05 {~Wb} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Mutual inductance is defined as the induced emf in primary coil when the current in secondary coil changes at the unit rate.

    Consider two long co-axial solenoids each of length 1. Radius of inner solenoid {tex}S_1{/tex} is {tex}r_1{/tex} and number of turns per unit length is {tex}n_1{/tex}.
    The corresponding quantities for outer solenoid {tex}S_2{/tex} are {tex}r_2{/tex} and {tex}n_2{/tex} respectively. Let {tex}{N}_1{/tex} and {tex}{N}_2{/tex} be the total number of turns of coils {tex}{S}_1{/tex} and {tex}{S}_2{/tex} respectively.
    When a current {tex}I_2{/tex} is set up through {tex}S_2{/tex}, it sets up magnetic flux through {tex}{S}_1{/tex}.
    {tex} {N}_1 \phi_1 ={M}_{12} {I}_2 {/tex}
    {tex} =\left({n}_1 l\right) \times\left(\pi {r}_1^2\right) \times\left(\mu_0 {n}_2 {I}_2\right) {/tex}
    {tex} =\mu_0 {n}_1 {n}_2 \pi {r}_1^2 l \ {I}_2 {/tex}
    {tex} {M}_{12}= \mu_0 {n}_1 {n}_2 \pi {r}_1^2 l={M}_{21} {/tex}
  2. {tex} |{e}| ={L} \frac{{dI}}{{dt}} {/tex}
    {tex} L =\frac{e}{d I / d t} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{2 / 40} {/tex}
    {tex} =0.1 {H} {/tex}
    {tex} \phi ={LI} {/tex}
    {tex} =0.1 \times \frac{2}{40} \times 10 {/tex}
    {tex} =0.05 {~Wb} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Define self-inductance of a coil. Derive the expression for the energy required to build up a current I in a coil of self-inductance L.
  2. The currents passing through two inductors of self-inductances 10 mH and 20 mH increase with time at the same rate.
    Draw graphs showing the variation of:
    1. the magnitude of emf induced with the rate of change of current in each inductor.
    2. the energy stored in each inductor with the current flowing through it.
question_text: Define self-inductance of a coil. Derive the expression for the energy required to build up a current I in a coil of self-inductance L. The currents passing through two inductors of self-inductances 10 mH and 20 mH increase with time at the same rate. Draw graphs showing the variation of: the magnitude of emf induced with the rate of change of current in each inductor. the energy stored in each inductor with the current flowing through it.
explanation: Self Inductance is magnetic flux linked with a coil when the current through the coil is unity. To maintain growth of current, power has to be supplied from external source. {tex} {P} =|e \| I| {/tex} {tex} = \frac{{d W}}{{d t}}={L I} \frac{{d I}}{{d t}} {/tex} {tex} {dW} ={LIdI} {/tex} {tex} W =\int {LIdI} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} {LI}^2 {/tex} {tex}{E}=-{L} \frac{{dI}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}{U}=\frac{1}{2} {LI}^2{/tex} Parabolic graph obtained. (1 indicates 10mH) & (2 indicates 20mH)
solution: Self Inductance is magnetic flux linked with a coil when the current through the coil is unity. To maintain growth of current, power has to be supplied from external source. {tex} {P} =|e \| I| {/tex} {tex} = \frac{{d W}}{{d t}}={L I} \frac{{d I}}{{d t}} {/tex} {tex} {dW} ={LIdI} {/tex} {tex} W =\int {LIdI} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2} {LI}^2 {/tex} {tex}{E}=-{L} \frac{{dI}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}{U}=\frac{1}{2} {LI}^2{/tex} Parabolic graph obtained. (1 indicates 10mH) & (2 indicates 20mH)
solution_html:
1. Self Inductance is magnetic flux linked with a coil when the current through the coil is unity.
    To maintain growth of current, power has to be supplied from external source.
    {tex} {P} =|e \| I| {/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{{d W}}{{d t}}={L I} \frac{{d I}}{{d t}} {/tex}
    {tex} {dW} ={LIdI} {/tex}
    {tex} W =\int {LIdI} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{1}{2} {LI}^2 {/tex}
    1. {tex}{E}=-{L} \frac{{dI}}{{dt}}{/tex}
    2. {tex}{U}=\frac{1}{2} {LI}^2{/tex} Parabolic graph obtained.

      (1 indicates 10mH) & (2 indicates 20mH)

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction and explain the role of negative sign in its expression.
  2. Explain, with an example, that Lenz's law is consistent with the law of conservation of energy.
question_text: State Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction and explain the role of negative sign in its expression. Explain, with an example, that Lenz's law is consistent with the law of conservation of energy.
explanation: The magnitude of induced emf in a circuit is equal to the time rate of change of magnetic flux. Mathematically, {tex} {e}=-{d} \Phi / {dt}{/tex} Negative sign indicates that the direction of induced emf and hence induced current in closed loop opposes its cause. When magnet is moved closer/away from the loop, same/ opposite pole is developed on the approaching face of the loop. So, mechanical work is required to move a magnet which gets converted into electrical energy which is consistent with law of conservation of energy.
solution: The magnitude of induced emf in a circuit is equal to the time rate of change of magnetic flux. Mathematically, {tex} {e}=-{d} \Phi / {dt}{/tex} Negative sign indicates that the direction of induced emf and hence induced current in closed loop opposes its cause. When magnet is moved closer/away from the loop, same/ opposite pole is developed on the approaching face of the loop. So, mechanical work is required to move a magnet which gets converted into electrical energy which is consistent with law of conservation of energy.
solution_html:
1. The magnitude of induced emf in a circuit is equal to the time rate of change of magnetic flux.
    Mathematically, {tex} {e}=-{d} \Phi / {dt}{/tex}
    Negative sign indicates that the direction of induced emf and hence induced current in closed loop opposes its cause.
  2. When magnet is moved closer/away from the loop, same/ opposite pole is developed on the approaching face of the loop. So, mechanical work is required to move a magnet which gets converted into electrical energy which is consistent with law of conservation of energy.

question_html:
In the given figure, three identical bulbs {tex}{P}, {Q}{/tex} and S are connected to a battery.

  1. Compare the brightness of bulbs P and Q with that of bulb S when key K is closed.
  2. Compare the brightness of the bulbs S and Q when the key K is opened.
    Justify your answer in both cases.
question_text: In the given figure, three identical bulbs {tex}{P}, {Q}{/tex} and S are connected to a battery. Compare the brightness of bulbs P and Q with that of bulb S when key K is closed. Compare the brightness of the bulbs S and Q when the key K is opened. Justify your answer in both cases.
explanation: Brightness of the bulb 'S' will be more than bulbs 'P' and 'Q' The current flowing through the bulb 'S' is twice of the current in bulbs 'P' and 'Q'. Brightness of the bulb 'S' and 'Q' will be same The current flowing through both bulbs is same.
solution: Brightness of the bulb 'S' will be more than bulbs 'P' and 'Q' The current flowing through the bulb 'S' is twice of the current in bulbs 'P' and 'Q'. Brightness of the bulb 'S' and 'Q' will be same The current flowing through both bulbs is same.
solution_html:
1. Brightness of the bulb 'S' will be more than bulbs 'P' and 'Q'
    The current flowing through the bulb 'S' is twice of the current in bulbs 'P' and 'Q'.
  2. Brightness of the bulb 'S' and 'Q' will be same
    The current flowing through both bulbs is same.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Differentiate between magnetic flux through an area and magnetic field at a point.
  2. A bar magnet is held with its length along the axis of a closed coil. Initially the south pole of the magnet faces the coil. If the magnet is moved towards the coil, explain how a current is induced in the coil and in what direction.
question_text: Differentiate between magnetic flux through an area and magnetic field at a point. A bar magnet is held with its length along the axis of a closed coil. Initially the south pole of the magnet faces the coil. If the magnet is moved towards the coil, explain how a current is induced in the coil and in what direction.
explanation: Magnetic flux is equal to total number of magnetic field lines passing normal to given area. Magnetic field at a point in the space around the magnet or moving charge where magnetic force can be experienced. When south pole of the bar magnet moves closer to coil, the magnetic flux through the coil increases. Hence according to Faraday's law induced emf/current generate in the coil. According to Lenz's law near end of the coil become south pole and as a consequence, current flows in the coil is clockwise direction.
solution: Magnetic flux is equal to total number of magnetic field lines passing normal to given area. Magnetic field at a point in the space around the magnet or moving charge where magnetic force can be experienced. When south pole of the bar magnet moves closer to coil, the magnetic flux through the coil increases. Hence according to Faraday's law induced emf/current generate in the coil. According to Lenz's law near end of the coil become south pole and as a consequence, current flows in the coil is clockwise direction.
solution_html:
1. Magnetic flux is equal to total number of magnetic field lines passing normal to given area.
    Magnetic field at a point in the space around the magnet or moving charge where magnetic force can be experienced.
  2. When south pole of the bar magnet moves closer to coil, the magnetic flux through the coil increases. Hence according to Faraday's law induced emf/current generate in the coil.
    According to Lenz's law near end of the coil become south pole and as a consequence, current flows in the coil is clockwise direction.

subject: Physics
question_html: Define 'self-inductance' of a coil. Derive an expression for self-inductance of a long solenoid of cross-sectional area A and length {tex}l{/tex}, having n turns per unit length.
question_text: Define 'self-inductance' of a coil. Derive an expression for self-inductance of a long solenoid of cross-sectional area A and length {tex}l{/tex} , having n turns per unit length.
explanation: Self inductance of a coil is the ratio of the flux linkage to the current flowing in the coil. Expression for self inductance of a long solenoid: The magnetic field due to current flowing in the solenoid, {tex}{B}=\mu_{0} n I{/tex} Total flux linked with the given solenoid {tex}N \phi_{B}=(n l)\left(\mu_{0} n I\right) {A}{/tex} {tex}N \phi_{B}=\mu_{0} n^{2} {~A} l I{/tex} Self inductance {tex}L=\frac{N \phi_{B}}{I}{/tex} {tex}L=\mu_{0} n^{2} {~A} l{/tex}
solution: Self inductance of a coil is the ratio of the flux linkage to the current flowing in the coil. Expression for self inductance of a long solenoid: The magnetic field due to current flowing in the solenoid, {tex}{B}=\mu_{0} n I{/tex} Total flux linked with the given solenoid {tex}N \phi_{B}=(n l)\left(\mu_{0} n I\right) {A}{/tex} {tex}N \phi_{B}=\mu_{0} n^{2} {~A} l I{/tex} Self inductance {tex}L=\frac{N \phi_{B}}{I}{/tex} {tex}L=\mu_{0} n^{2} {~A} l{/tex}
solution_html:
Self inductance of a coil is the ratio of the flux linkage to the current flowing in the coil.
Expression for self inductance of a long solenoid:
The magnetic field due to current flowing in the solenoid, {tex}{B}=\mu_{0} n I{/tex}
Total flux linked with the given solenoid
{tex}N \phi_{B}=(n l)\left(\mu_{0} n I\right) {A}{/tex}
{tex}N \phi_{B}=\mu_{0} n^{2} {~A} l I{/tex}
Self inductance
{tex}L=\frac{N \phi_{B}}{I}{/tex}
{tex}L=\mu_{0} n^{2} {~A} l{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: State Lenz's law. A rod MN of length L is rotated about an axis passing through its end M perpendicular to its length, with a constant angular velocity {tex}\omega{/tex} in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} parallel to the axis. Obtain an expression for emf induced between its ends.
question_text: State Lenz's law. A rod MN of length L is rotated about an axis passing through its end M perpendicular to its length, with a constant angular velocity {tex}\omega{/tex} in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} parallel to the axis. Obtain an expression for emf induced between its ends.
explanation: Lenz's law The polarity of induced emf is such that it tends to produce a current which opposes the change in magnetic flux that produced it. Expression of induced emf The magnitude of the emf generated across the length dr of the rod as it moves at right angles to the magnetic field is given by {tex}d \varepsilon =B {v} d r{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon =\int d \varepsilon{/tex} {tex}= \int_{0}^{L} B {v} d r{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon =\int_{0}^{L} B \omega r d r{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon =\frac{1}{2} B L^{2} \omega{/tex}
solution: Lenz's law The polarity of induced emf is such that it tends to produce a current which opposes the change in magnetic flux that produced it. Expression of induced emf The magnitude of the emf generated across the length dr of the rod as it moves at right angles to the magnetic field is given by {tex}d \varepsilon =B {v} d r{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon =\int d \varepsilon{/tex} {tex}= \int_{0}^{L} B {v} d r{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon =\int_{0}^{L} B \omega r d r{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon =\frac{1}{2} B L^{2} \omega{/tex}
solution_html:
Lenz's law
The polarity of induced emf is such that it tends to produce a current which opposes the change in magnetic flux that produced it.
Expression of induced emf

The magnitude of the emf generated across the length dr of the rod as it moves at right angles to the magnetic field is given by
{tex}d \varepsilon =B {v} d r{/tex}
{tex}\varepsilon =\int d \varepsilon{/tex}
{tex}= \int_{0}^{L} B {v} d r{/tex}
{tex}\varepsilon =\int_{0}^{L} B \omega r d r{/tex}
{tex}\varepsilon =\frac{1}{2} B L^{2} \omega{/tex}

1. State Lenz's law.
  2. In the given figure:
    1. Identify the machine.
    2. Name the parts P and Q and R of the machine.
    3. Give the polarities of the magnetic poles.
    4. Write the two ways of increasing the output voltage.
question_text: State Lenz's law. In the given figure: Identify the machine. Name the parts P and Q and R of the machine. Give the polarities of the magnetic poles. Write the two ways of increasing the output voltage.
explanation: Lenz’s law- The polarity of induced emf is such that it tends to produce a current which opposes the change in magnetic flux that produced it. AC generator P - Slip rings Q - Carbon brushes R - Armature coil Left side of the magnet is North & right side is South or vice-versa. By increasing the number of turns in the armature coil. By increasing the speed of rotation of the armature coil. By increasing the strength of the magnetic field B.
solution: Lenz’s law- The polarity of induced emf is such that it tends to produce a current which opposes the change in magnetic flux that produced it. AC generator P - Slip rings Q - Carbon brushes R - Armature coil Left side of the magnet is North & right side is South or vice-versa. By increasing the number of turns in the armature coil. By increasing the speed of rotation of the armature coil. By increasing the strength of the magnetic field B.
solution_html:
1. Lenz’s law- The polarity of induced emf is such that it tends to produce a current which opposes the change in magnetic flux that produced it.
    1. AC generator
    2. P - Slip rings
      Q - Carbon brushes
      R -  Armature coil
    3. Left side of the magnet is North & right side is South or vice-versa.
      • By increasing the number of turns in the armature coil.
      • By increasing the speed of rotation of the armature coil.
      • By increasing the strength of the magnetic field B.

question_text: State Lenz's law and explain how this law is a consequence of conservation of energy principle. A square shaped loop of side {tex}\frac{l}{2}{/tex} is initially lying outside a region of uniform magnetic field {tex}\overrightarrow{{B}}{/tex} as shown in the figure. The loop is moved towards right with a constant velocity {tex}\vec{v}{/tex} till it goes out of the region of magnetic field. What will be the directions of induced current when the loop enters the field and when it leaves the field? explanation: Lenz's law- Polarity of the induced emf is such that it tends to produce a current, which opposes the change in magnetic flux that produces it. When magnet is moved closer/away from the loop, same/opposite pole is developed on the approaching face of the loop. So mechanical work is required to move a magnet which gets converted into electrical energy which is consistent with the law of conservation of energy. Anticlockwise Clockwise

question_html: Consider two long co-axial solenoids {tex}{S}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{S}_{2}{/tex}, each of length {tex}l\left(\gg {r}_{2}\right){/tex} and of radius {tex}r_{1}{/tex} and {tex}r_{2}\left(r_{2} \gt r_{1}\right){/tex}. The number of turns per unit length are {tex}n_{1}{/tex} and {tex}n_{2}{/tex} respectively. Derive an expression for mutual inductance {tex}M_{12}{/tex} of solenoid {tex}{S}_{1}{/tex} with respect to solenoid {tex}{S}_{2}{/tex}. Show that {tex}{M}_{21}={M}_{12}{/tex}.
question_text: Consider two long co-axial solenoids {tex}{S}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{S}_{2}{/tex} , each of length {tex}l\left(\gg {r}_{2}\right){/tex} and of radius {tex}r_{1}{/tex} and {tex}r_{2}\left(r_{2} \gt r_{1}\right){/tex} . The number of turns per unit length are {tex}n_{1}{/tex} and {tex}n_{2}{/tex} respectively. Derive an expression for mutual inductance {tex}M_{12}{/tex} of solenoid {tex}{S}_{1}{/tex} with respect to solenoid {tex}{S}_{2}{/tex} . Show that {tex}{M}_{21}={M}_{12}{/tex} .
explanation: Let {tex}{N}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{N}_{2}{/tex} be the total number of turns of coils {tex}{S}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{S}_{2}{/tex} respectively. When current {tex}I_{2}{/tex} is set up in {tex}S_{2}{/tex} , flux linkage with solenoid {tex}S_{1}{/tex} is- {tex}{N}_{1} \phi_{1}={M}_{12} {I}_{2} {/tex} ...(i) {tex}{~N}_{1} \phi_{1}=\left({n}_{1} l\right)\left(\pi {r}_{1}^{2}\right)\left(\mu_{0} {n}_{2} {I}_{2}\right){/tex} {tex}{N}_{1} \phi_{1}=\mu_{0} {n}_{1} {n}_{2} \pi {r}_{1}^{2} l {I}_{2}{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\text { From (i) and (ii) }{/tex} {tex}{M}_{12}=\mu_{0} {n}_{1} {n}_{2} \pi {r}_{1}^{2} l{/tex} Considering Reverse case, when {tex}{I}_{1}{/tex} current is set up in {tex}{S}_{1}{/tex} , flux linkage with {tex}{S}_{2}{/tex} is - {tex}{N}_{2} \phi_{2}={M}_{21} {I}_{1}{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}{~N}_{2} \phi_{2}=\left({n}_{2} {l}\right)\left(\pi {r}_{1}^{2}\right)\left(\mu_{0} {n}_{1} {I}_{1}\right){/tex} ...(iv) From (iii) and (iv) {tex}{M}_{21}={n}_{1} {n}_{2} \pi {r}_{1}^{2} l{/tex} {tex}\therefore M_{12}=M_{21}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}{N}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{N}_{2}{/tex} be the total number of turns of coils {tex}{S}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{S}_{2}{/tex} respectively. When current {tex}I_{2}{/tex} is set up in {tex}S_{2}{/tex} , flux linkage with solenoid {tex}S_{1}{/tex} is- {tex}{N}_{1} \phi_{1}={M}_{12} {I}_{2} {/tex} ...(i) {tex}{~N}_{1} \phi_{1}=\left({n}_{1} l\right)\left(\pi {r}_{1}^{2}\right)\left(\mu_{0} {n}_{2} {I}_{2}\right){/tex} {tex}{N}_{1} \phi_{1}=\mu_{0} {n}_{1} {n}_{2} \pi {r}_{1}^{2} l {I}_{2}{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\text { From (i) and (ii) }{/tex} {tex}{M}_{12}=\mu_{0} {n}_{1} {n}_{2} \pi {r}_{1}^{2} l{/tex} Considering Reverse case, when {tex}{I}_{1}{/tex} current is set up in {tex}{S}_{1}{/tex} , flux linkage with {tex}{S}_{2}{/tex} is - {tex}{N}_{2} \phi_{2}={M}_{21} {I}_{1}{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}{~N}_{2} \phi_{2}=\left({n}_{2} {l}\right)\left(\pi {r}_{1}^{2}\right)\left(\mu_{0} {n}_{1} {I}_{1}\right){/tex} ...(iv) From (iii) and (iv) {tex}{M}_{21}={n}_{1} {n}_{2} \pi {r}_{1}^{2} l{/tex} {tex}\therefore M_{12}=M_{21}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}{N}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{N}_{2}{/tex} be the total number of turns of coils {tex}{S}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{S}_{2}{/tex} respectively.
When current {tex}I_{2}{/tex} is set up in {tex}S_{2}{/tex}, flux linkage with solenoid {tex}S_{1}{/tex} is-
{tex}{N}_{1} \phi_{1}={M}_{12} {I}_{2} {/tex} ...(i)
{tex}{~N}_{1} \phi_{1}=\left({n}_{1} l\right)\left(\pi {r}_{1}^{2}\right)\left(\mu_{0} {n}_{2} {I}_{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}{N}_{1} \phi_{1}=\mu_{0} {n}_{1} {n}_{2} \pi {r}_{1}^{2} l {I}_{2}{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}\text { From (i) and (ii) }{/tex}
{tex}{M}_{12}=\mu_{0} {n}_{1} {n}_{2} \pi {r}_{1}^{2} l{/tex}
Considering Reverse case, when {tex}{I}_{1}{/tex} current is set up in {tex}{S}_{1}{/tex}, flux linkage with {tex}{S}_{2}{/tex} is -
{tex}{N}_{2} \phi_{2}={M}_{21} {I}_{1}{/tex} ...(iii)
{tex}{~N}_{2} \phi_{2}=\left({n}_{2} {l}\right)\left(\pi {r}_{1}^{2}\right)\left(\mu_{0} {n}_{1} {I}_{1}\right){/tex} ...(iv)
From (iii) and (iv)
{tex}{M}_{21}={n}_{1} {n}_{2} \pi {r}_{1}^{2} l{/tex}
{tex}\therefore M_{12}=M_{21}{/tex}

question_html:
1. Obtain an expression for the self-inductance of a long solenoid of length '{tex}l{/tex}', cross-sectional area 'A' and having 'N' turns.
  2. The figure shows the plot of magnitude of induced emf ({tex}\varepsilon{/tex}) versus the rate of change of current in two coils '1' and '2'. Which coil has greater value of self-inductance and why?
question_text: Obtain an expression for the self-inductance of a long solenoid of length ' {tex}l{/tex} ', cross-sectional area 'A' and having 'N' turns. The figure shows the plot of magnitude of induced emf ( {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} ) versus the rate of change of current in two coils '1' and '2'. Which coil has greater value of self-inductance and why?
explanation: Magnetic field due to a current I flowing in the solenoid {tex}{B}=\mu_{0} {nI}{/tex} Total flux linked with solenoid is {tex} {N} \phi_B=({n} l)\left(\mu_0 {nI}\right) {A} {/tex} {tex} =\mu_0 {n}^2 {~A} / {I} {/tex} {tex} {~L}=\frac{{N} \phi_B}{{I}} {/tex} {tex} {~L}=\mu_0 {n}^2 {~A} l {/tex} Coil 1 Slope of line {tex}1, \frac{|\varepsilon|}{{dI} / {dt}}={L}{/tex} is more for coil 1 than coil 2
solution: Magnetic field due to a current I flowing in the solenoid {tex}{B}=\mu_{0} {nI}{/tex} Total flux linked with solenoid is {tex} {N} \phi_B=({n} l)\left(\mu_0 {nI}\right) {A} {/tex} {tex} =\mu_0 {n}^2 {~A} / {I} {/tex} {tex} {~L}=\frac{{N} \phi_B}{{I}} {/tex} {tex} {~L}=\mu_0 {n}^2 {~A} l {/tex} Coil 1 Slope of line {tex}1, \frac{|\varepsilon|}{{dI} / {dt}}={L}{/tex} is more for coil 1 than coil 2
solution_html:
1. Magnetic field due to a current I flowing in the solenoid {tex}{B}=\mu_{0} {nI}{/tex}
    Total flux linked with solenoid is
    {tex} {N} \phi_B=({n} l)\left(\mu_0 {nI}\right) {A} {/tex}
    {tex} =\mu_0 {n}^2 {~A} / {I} {/tex}
    {tex} {~L}=\frac{{N} \phi_B}{{I}} {/tex}
    {tex} {~L}=\mu_0 {n}^2 {~A} l {/tex}
  2. Coil 1
    Slope of line {tex}1, \frac{|\varepsilon|}{{dI} / {dt}}={L}{/tex} is more for coil 1 than coil 2

question_html:
Two coils '1' and '2' are placed close to each other as shown in the figure. Find the direction of induced current in coil '1' in each of the following situations, justifying your answers:

  1. Coil '2' is moving towards coil '1'.
  2. Coil '2' is moving away from coil '1'.
  3. The resistance connected with coil '2' is increased keeping both the coils stationary.
question_text: Two coils '1' and '2' are placed close to each other as shown in the figure. Find the direction of induced current in coil '1' in each of the following situations, justifying your answers: Coil '2' is moving towards coil '1'. Coil '2' is moving away from coil '1'. The resistance connected with coil '2' is increased keeping both the coils stationary.
explanation: Induced current will be clockwise. Due to the direction flow of current in coil 2, the face approaching to coil 1 behaves like south pole. Induced polarity on coil 1 viewed from the side of coil 2 will be south pole Induced current will be Anticlockwise Due to direction of flow of current in coil 2, face going away from coil 1 behaves like south pole. Induced polarity on coil 1 viewed from the side of coil 2, will be north pole. Induced current will be Anticlockwise Till the resistance in coil 2 is increasing, the magnetic flux associated with coil 1 is decreasing. Hence according to Lenz's law current will induce in coil 1, momentarily and after that no induced current in coil
solution: Induced current will be clockwise. Due to the direction flow of current in coil 2, the face approaching to coil 1 behaves like south pole. Induced polarity on coil 1 viewed from the side of coil 2 will be south pole Induced current will be Anticlockwise Due to direction of flow of current in coil 2, face going away from coil 1 behaves like south pole. Induced polarity on coil 1 viewed from the side of coil 2, will be north pole. Induced current will be Anticlockwise Till the resistance in coil 2 is increasing, the magnetic flux associated with coil 1 is decreasing. Hence according to Lenz's law current will induce in coil 1, momentarily and after that no induced current in coil
solution_html:
1. Induced current will be clockwise.
    Due to the direction flow of current in coil 2, the face approaching to coil 1 behaves like south pole. Induced polarity on coil 1 viewed from the side of coil 2 will be south pole
  2. Induced current will be Anticlockwise
    Due to direction of flow of current in coil 2, face going away from coil 1 behaves like south pole. Induced polarity on coil 1 viewed from the side of coil 2, will be north pole.
  3. Induced current will be Anticlockwise
    Till the resistance in coil 2 is increasing, the magnetic flux associated with coil 1 is decreasing. Hence according to Lenz's law current will induce in coil 1, momentarily and after that no induced current in coil

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Show that the energy required to build up the current I in a coil of inductance L is {tex}\frac{1}{2} {LI}^{2}{/tex}.
  2. Considering the case of magnetic field produced by air-filled current carrying solenoid, show that the magnetic energy density of a magnetic field {tex}B{/tex} is {tex}\frac{B^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}}{/tex}.
question_text: Show that the energy required to build up the current I in a coil of inductance L is {tex}\frac{1}{2} {LI}^{2}{/tex} . Considering the case of magnetic field produced by air-filled current carrying solenoid, show that the magnetic energy density of a magnetic field {tex}B{/tex} is {tex}\frac{B^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}}{/tex} .
explanation: Induced emf in an inductor {tex}|\varepsilon|=L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} Rate of work done at any instant {tex}\frac{d W}{d t}=|\varepsilon| I{/tex} Total Amount of work done in establishing current I {tex}{W}=\int d W=\int_{0}^{I} L I d I{/tex} Energy required to build up current I is {tex}{W}=1 / 2 \ {LI}^{2}{/tex} The Magnetic Energy is {tex}{W} ={U}_{{B}}=1 / 2 \ {LI}^{2}{/tex} {tex}=1 / 2 {~L}\left(\frac{B}{n \mu_{0}}\right)^{2} \text { as } {B}={n} \mu_{0} {I}{/tex} Using {tex}{L}=\mu_{0} n^{2} A l{/tex} {tex}{U}_{{B}}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\mu_{0} n^{2} A l\right)\left(\frac{B^{2}}{\mu_{0}^{2} n^{2}}\right){/tex} Energy density {tex}=\frac{{U}_{{B}}}{\text { volume }}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{U}_{{B}}}{\text { volume }} =\frac{1}{2} \times \mu_{0} {n}^{2} {~A} l \times \frac{{B}^{2}}{\mu_{0}{ }^{2} {n}^{2}} \times \frac{1}{{~A} l}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{{~B}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex}
solution: Induced emf in an inductor {tex}|\varepsilon|=L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} Rate of work done at any instant {tex}\frac{d W}{d t}=|\varepsilon| I{/tex} Total Amount of work done in establishing current I {tex}{W}=\int d W=\int_{0}^{I} L I d I{/tex} Energy required to build up current I is {tex}{W}=1 / 2 \ {LI}^{2}{/tex} The Magnetic Energy is {tex}{W} ={U}_{{B}}=1 / 2 \ {LI}^{2}{/tex} {tex}=1 / 2 {~L}\left(\frac{B}{n \mu_{0}}\right)^{2} \text { as } {B}={n} \mu_{0} {I}{/tex} Using {tex}{L}=\mu_{0} n^{2} A l{/tex} {tex}{U}_{{B}}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\mu_{0} n^{2} A l\right)\left(\frac{B^{2}}{\mu_{0}^{2} n^{2}}\right){/tex} Energy density {tex}=\frac{{U}_{{B}}}{\text { volume }}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{U}_{{B}}}{\text { volume }} =\frac{1}{2} \times \mu_{0} {n}^{2} {~A} l \times \frac{{B}^{2}}{\mu_{0}{ }^{2} {n}^{2}} \times \frac{1}{{~A} l}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{{~B}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Induced emf in an inductor
    {tex}|\varepsilon|=L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex}
    Rate of work done at any instant
    {tex}\frac{d W}{d t}=|\varepsilon| I{/tex}
    Total Amount of work done in establishing current I
    {tex}{W}=\int d W=\int_{0}^{I} L I d I{/tex}
    Energy required to build up current I is
    {tex}{W}=1 / 2 \ {LI}^{2}{/tex}
  2. The Magnetic Energy is
    {tex}{W} ={U}_{{B}}=1 / 2 \ {LI}^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=1 / 2 {~L}\left(\frac{B}{n \mu_{0}}\right)^{2} \text { as } {B}={n} \mu_{0} {I}{/tex}
    Using {tex}{L}=\mu_{0} n^{2} A l{/tex}
    {tex}{U}_{{B}}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\mu_{0} n^{2} A l\right)\left(\frac{B^{2}}{\mu_{0}^{2} n^{2}}\right){/tex}
    Energy density {tex}=\frac{{U}_{{B}}}{\text { volume }}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{{U}_{{B}}}{\text { volume }} =\frac{1}{2} \times \mu_{0} {n}^{2} {~A} l \times \frac{{B}^{2}}{\mu_{0}{ }^{2} {n}^{2}} \times \frac{1}{{~A} l}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{{~B}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Prove that the voltage is ahead of the current in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} rad in an ac circuit containing an ideal inductor.
  2. The currents through two inductors of self-inductance 12 mH and 24 mH are increasing with time at the same rate. Draw graphs showing the variation of the:
    1. magnitude of emf induced with the rate of change of current in each inductor.
    2. energy stored in each inductor with the current flowing through it.
question_text: Prove that the voltage is ahead of the current in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} rad in an ac circuit containing an ideal inductor. The currents through two inductors of self-inductance 12 mH and 24 mH are increasing with time at the same rate. Draw graphs showing the variation of the: magnitude of emf induced with the rate of change of current in each inductor. energy stored in each inductor with the current flowing through it.
explanation: Let the voltage across the source be {tex} v=v_m \sin \omega t {/tex} Using Kirchhoff's loop rule {tex} v-L \frac{d i}{d t}=0 {/tex} {tex} \frac{d i}{d t}=\frac{v}{L}=\frac{v_m}{L} \sin \omega t {/tex} {tex} d i=\frac{v_m}{L} \sin \omega {t} d t {/tex} Integrating {tex} i=\frac{v_m}{\omega L} \cos \omega t {/tex} {tex} i=i_m \sin \left(\omega t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right) {/tex} This show that current lags behind the voltage by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2} {rad}{/tex} . {tex}\therefore{/tex} Voltage is ahead of current in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2} {rad}{/tex} Graph for induced e.m.f. Graph for energy stored
solution: Let the voltage across the source be {tex} v=v_m \sin \omega t {/tex} Using Kirchhoff's loop rule {tex} v-L \frac{d i}{d t}=0 {/tex} {tex} \frac{d i}{d t}=\frac{v}{L}=\frac{v_m}{L} \sin \omega t {/tex} {tex} d i=\frac{v_m}{L} \sin \omega {t} d t {/tex} Integrating {tex} i=\frac{v_m}{\omega L} \cos \omega t {/tex} {tex} i=i_m \sin \left(\omega t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right) {/tex} This show that current lags behind the voltage by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2} {rad}{/tex} . {tex}\therefore{/tex} Voltage is ahead of current in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2} {rad}{/tex} Graph for induced e.m.f. Graph for energy stored
solution_html:
1. Let the voltage across the source be
    {tex} v=v_m \sin \omega t {/tex}

    Using Kirchhoff's loop rule
    {tex} v-L \frac{d i}{d t}=0 {/tex}
    {tex} \frac{d i}{d t}=\frac{v}{L}=\frac{v_m}{L} \sin \omega t {/tex}
    {tex} d i=\frac{v_m}{L} \sin \omega {t} d t {/tex}
    Integrating
    {tex} i=\frac{v_m}{\omega L} \cos \omega t {/tex}
    {tex} i=i_m \sin \left(\omega t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right) {/tex}
    This show that current lags behind the voltage by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2} {rad}{/tex}.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Voltage is ahead of current in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2} {rad}{/tex}
    1. Graph for induced e.m.f.
    2. Graph for energy stored

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Two concentric circular coils of radii {tex}r_{1}{/tex} and {tex}r_{2}\left(r_{2} \gg r\right){/tex} are placed coaxially with their centres coinciding. If a current I is passed through the outer coil, obtain the expression for mutual inductance of the arrangement.
  2. The current in a solenoid decreases steadily from 6 mA to 2 mA in 50 ms. If an average emf of 0.4 V is induced, find the self-inductance of the solenoid.
question_text: Two concentric circular coils of radii {tex}r_{1}{/tex} and {tex}r_{2}\left(r_{2} \gg r\right){/tex} are placed coaxially with their centres coinciding. If a current I is passed through the outer coil, obtain the expression for mutual inductance of the arrangement. The current in a solenoid decreases steadily from 6 mA to 2 mA in 50 ms. If an average emf of 0.4 V is induced, find the self-inductance of the solenoid.
explanation: Magnetic field at centre of outer coil {tex}{S}_2{/tex} {tex} B_2=\frac{\mu_0 {I}_2}{2 r_2} {/tex} Flux linked with inner coil {tex}{S}_1{/tex} is {tex} \phi_1 =B_2 A_1 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\mu_0 {I}_2}{2 r_2} \cdot \pi r_1^2 {/tex} Also, {tex}\phi_1=M_{12} I_2{/tex} {tex} \therefore M_{12}=\frac{\mu_0}{2 r_2} \cdot \pi r_1^2 {/tex} {tex} \varepsilon =-L \frac{d I}{d t} {/tex} {tex} L =\frac{0.4 \times 50 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{-3}} {/tex} {tex} =5 {H} {/tex}
solution: Magnetic field at centre of outer coil {tex}{S}_2{/tex} {tex} B_2=\frac{\mu_0 {I}_2}{2 r_2} {/tex} Flux linked with inner coil {tex}{S}_1{/tex} is {tex} \phi_1 =B_2 A_1 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\mu_0 {I}_2}{2 r_2} \cdot \pi r_1^2 {/tex} Also, {tex}\phi_1=M_{12} I_2{/tex} {tex} \therefore M_{12}=\frac{\mu_0}{2 r_2} \cdot \pi r_1^2 {/tex} {tex} \varepsilon =-L \frac{d I}{d t} {/tex} {tex} L =\frac{0.4 \times 50 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{-3}} {/tex} {tex} =5 {H} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Magnetic field at centre of outer coil {tex}{S}_2{/tex}
    {tex} B_2=\frac{\mu_0 {I}_2}{2 r_2} {/tex}
    Flux linked with inner coil {tex}{S}_1{/tex} is
    {tex} \phi_1 =B_2 A_1 {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{\mu_0 {I}_2}{2 r_2} \cdot \pi r_1^2 {/tex}
    Also, {tex}\phi_1=M_{12} I_2{/tex}
    {tex} \therefore M_{12}=\frac{\mu_0}{2 r_2} \cdot \pi r_1^2 {/tex}
  2. {tex} \varepsilon =-L \frac{d I}{d t} {/tex}
    {tex} L =\frac{0.4 \times 50 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{-3}} {/tex}
    {tex} =5 {H} {/tex}

question_html:
Assertion (A): It is difficult to move a magnet into a coil of large number of turns when the circuit of the coil is closed.
Reason (R): The direction of induced current in a coil with its circuit closed, due to motion of a magnet, is such that it opposes the cause.
question_text: Assertion (A): It is difficult to move a magnet into a coil of large number of turns when the circuit of the coil is closed. Reason (R): The direction of induced current in a coil with its circuit closed, due to motion of a magnet, is such that it opposes the cause.

question_text: A conducting circular loop is placed in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\mathrm{B}=50 \mathrm{mT}{/tex} with its plane perpendicular to the magnetic field. The radius of the loop is made to shrink at a constant rate of {tex}1 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}{/tex} . At the instant the radius of the loop is 4 cm , the induced emf in the loop is:

options.explanation: To find the induced emf {tex}(\mathcal{E}){/tex} , use Faraday's law: {tex} \mathcal{E}=-B \frac{d A}{d t}{/tex} The area {tex}A=\pi r^2{/tex} . The radius shrinks at {tex}1 {~mm} / {s}{/tex} , and at {tex}r=4 {~cm}{/tex} : {tex} \frac{d A}{d t}=2 \pi r \frac{d r}{d t}=2 \pi(0.04)(-0.001)=-0.08 \pi {~m}^2 / {s}{/tex} Now, {tex} \mathcal{E}=50 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.08 \pi=4 \pi \mu {~V}{/tex} Thus, the induced emf is {tex}4 \pi \mu {~V}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Consider the arrangement of two coils P and Q shown in the figure. When current in coil P is switched on or switched off, a current flows in coil Q.

  1. Explain the phenomenon involved in it.
  2. Mention two factors on which the current produced in coil Q depends.
  3. Give the direction of current in coil Q when there is a current in the coil {tex}P{/tex} and (i) {tex}R{/tex} is increased, and (ii) {tex}R{/tex} is decreased.
question_text: Consider the arrangement of two coils P and Q shown in the figure. When current in coil P is switched on or switched off, a current flows in coil Q. Explain the phenomenon involved in it. Mention two factors on which the current produced in coil Q depends. Give the direction of current in coil Q when there is a current in the coil {tex}P{/tex} and (i) {tex}R{/tex} is increased, and (ii) {tex}R{/tex} is decreased.
explanation: Mutual Induction Mutual induction occurs when an alternating voltage is applied to the primary coil, causing an alternating current that generates a changing magnetic flux. This flux links with the secondary coil, inducing an electromotive force (emf) in it. Factors Affecting Current in Coil Q The current produced in coil Q depends on several factors, including: Number of Turns in Coils P and Q : A greater number of turns increases the magnetic flux linkage, enhancing the induced emf and, consequently, the current in coil Q. Current Flowing Through Coil P : The strength of the magnetic field produced by coil P is proportional to the current flowing through it. A higher current leads to a stronger magnetic flux linked with coil Q. Direction of Current in Coil Q The direction of current through coil Q is determined by changes in resistance in coil P: Clockwise when R is increased : This opposes the decrease in magnetic flux. Anticlockwise when R is decreased : This opposes the increase in magnetic flux.
solution: Mutual Induction Mutual induction occurs when an alternating voltage is applied to the primary coil, causing an alternating current that generates a changing magnetic flux. This flux links with the secondary coil, inducing an electromotive force (emf) in it. Factors Affecting Current in Coil Q The current produced in coil Q depends on several factors, including: Number of Turns in Coils P and Q : A greater number of turns increases the magnetic flux linkage, enhancing the induced emf and, consequently, the current in coil Q. Current Flowing Through Coil P : The strength of the magnetic field produced by coil P is proportional to the current flowing through it. A higher current leads to a stronger magnetic flux linked with coil Q. Direction of Current in Coil Q The direction of current through coil Q is determined by changes in resistance in coil P: Clockwise when R is increased : This opposes the decrease in magnetic flux. Anticlockwise when R is decreased : This opposes the increase in magnetic flux.
solution_html:
1. Mutual Induction
    Mutual induction occurs when an alternating voltage is applied to the primary coil, causing an alternating current that generates a changing magnetic flux. This flux links with the secondary coil, inducing an electromotive force (emf) in it.
  2. Factors Affecting Current in Coil Q
    The current produced in coil Q depends on several factors, including:
    1. Number of Turns in Coils P and Q: A greater number of turns increases the magnetic flux linkage, enhancing the induced emf and, consequently, the current in coil Q.
    2. Current Flowing Through Coil P: The strength of the magnetic field produced by coil P is proportional to the current flowing through it. A higher current leads to a stronger magnetic flux linked with coil Q.
  3. Direction of Current in Coil Q
    The direction of current through coil Q is determined by changes in resistance in coil P:
    1. Clockwise when R is increased: This opposes the decrease in magnetic flux.
    2. Anticlockwise when R is decreased: This opposes the increase in magnetic flux.

The solenoid current drops from 1.5 A to zero in 25 ms. Calculate the current induced in the coil in this duration. (Take {tex}\left.\pi^2=10\right){/tex}
explanation: Induced emf {tex}(\varepsilon)=\frac{-N d \phi}{d t} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{-N A d B}{d t} {/tex} {tex} =-N A \frac{d}{d t}\left(\mu_0 n I\right) {/tex} {tex} =-N \mu_0 n\left(\pi r^2\right) \frac{d I}{d t} {/tex} {tex} \varepsilon=\frac{100 \times 4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 250 \times 10^2 \times \pi \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2 \times 1.5}{25 \times 10^{-3}} {/tex} {tex} =0.1536 {~V} {/tex} {tex} I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R} {/tex} {tex} =0.03 {~A} {/tex}
solution: Induced emf {tex}(\varepsilon)=\frac{-N d \phi}{d t} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{-N A d B}{d t} {/tex} {tex} =-N A \frac{d}{d t}\left(\mu_0 n I\right) {/tex} {tex} =-N \mu_0 n\left(\pi r^2\right) \frac{d I}{d t} {/tex} {tex} \varepsilon=\frac{100 \times 4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 250 \times 10^2 \times \pi \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2 \times 1.5}{25 \times 10^{-3}} {/tex} {tex} =0.1536 {~V} {/tex} {tex} I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R} {/tex} {tex} =0.03 {~A} {/tex}
solution_html:
Induced emf {tex}(\varepsilon)=\frac{-N d \phi}{d t} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{-N A d B}{d t} {/tex}
{tex} =-N A \frac{d}{d t}\left(\mu_0 n I\right) {/tex}
{tex} =-N \mu_0 n\left(\pi r^2\right) \frac{d I}{d t} {/tex}
{tex} \varepsilon=\frac{100 \times 4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 250 \times 10^2 \times \pi \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2 \times 1.5}{25 \times 10^{-3}} {/tex}
{tex} =0.1536 {~V} {/tex}
{tex} I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R} {/tex}
{tex} =0.03 {~A} {/tex}

1. A rectangular coil of N turns and area of cross-section A is rotated at a steady angular speed {tex}\omega{/tex} in a uniform magnetic field. Obtain an expression for the emf induced in the coil at any instant of time.
  2. Two coplanar and concentric circular loops {tex}{L}_1{/tex} and {tex}{L}_2{/tex} are placed coaxially with their centres coinciding. The radii of {tex}{L}_1{/tex} and {tex}{L}_2{/tex} are 1 cm and 100 cm respectively. Calculate the mutual inductance of the loops. (Take {tex}\pi^2=10{/tex})
question_text: A rectangular coil of N turns and area of cross-section A is rotated at a steady angular speed {tex}\omega{/tex} in a uniform magnetic field. Obtain an expression for the emf induced in the coil at any instant of time. Two coplanar and concentric circular loops {tex}{L}_1{/tex} and {tex}{L}_2{/tex} are placed coaxially with their centres coinciding. The radii of {tex}{L}_1{/tex} and {tex}{L}_2{/tex} are 1 cm and 100 cm respectively. Calculate the mutual inductance of the loops. (Take {tex}\pi^2=10{/tex} )
explanation: The flux at any instant t is {tex}\phi=N B A \cos \theta=\mathrm{NBA} \cos \omega t{/tex} From Faraday‟s law {tex} \varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi_B}{d t} {/tex} {tex} =-N B A \frac{d}{d t}(\cos \omega t) {/tex} {tex} \varepsilon=-N B A \omega \sin \omega t {/tex} {tex} M =\frac{\mu_0 \pi r_1^2}{2 r_2}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times \pi r_1^2}{2 r_2} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{2 \times 10 \times 10^{-7} \times\left(10^{-2}\right)^2}{100 \times 10^{-7}} {/tex} {tex} =2 \times 10^{-10} {H} {/tex}
solution: The flux at any instant t is {tex}\phi=N B A \cos \theta=\mathrm{NBA} \cos \omega t{/tex} From Faraday‟s law {tex} \varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi_B}{d t} {/tex} {tex} =-N B A \frac{d}{d t}(\cos \omega t) {/tex} {tex} \varepsilon=-N B A \omega \sin \omega t {/tex} {tex} M =\frac{\mu_0 \pi r_1^2}{2 r_2}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times \pi r_1^2}{2 r_2} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{2 \times 10 \times 10^{-7} \times\left(10^{-2}\right)^2}{100 \times 10^{-7}} {/tex} {tex} =2 \times 10^{-10} {H} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. The flux at any instant t is
    {tex}\phi=N B A \cos \theta=\mathrm{NBA} \cos \omega t{/tex}
    From Faraday‟s law
    {tex} \varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi_B}{d t} {/tex}
    {tex} =-N B A \frac{d}{d t}(\cos \omega t) {/tex}
    {tex} \varepsilon=-N B A \omega \sin \omega t {/tex}
  2. {tex} M =\frac{\mu_0 \pi r_1^2}{2 r_2}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times \pi r_1^2}{2 r_2} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{2 \times 10 \times 10^{-7} \times\left(10^{-2}\right)^2}{100 \times 10^{-7}} {/tex}
    {tex} =2 \times 10^{-10} {H} {/tex}

question_html:
1. With the help of a diagram, briefly explain the construction and working of ac generator.
  2. An electron is revolving around a proton in an orbit of radius {tex}r{/tex} with a speed {tex}v{/tex}. Obtain expression for magnetic moment associated with the electron.
question_text: With the help of a diagram, briefly explain the construction and working of ac generator. An electron is revolving around a proton in an orbit of radius {tex}r{/tex} with a speed {tex}v{/tex} . Obtain expression for magnetic moment associated with the electron.
explanation: Construction - It consists of a coil placed in a magnetic field. The coil is mounted on a rotor shaft. The ends of the coil are connected to an external circuit by means of slip rings and brushes. Working - The coil is rotated in the uniform magnetic field by some external means. The rotation of the coil causes the magnetic flux through it to change, so an emf is induced in the coil. The equivalent current {tex}{I}=\frac{{q}}{{t}}=\frac{{e}}{\frac{2 \pi {r}}{{v}}}=\frac{{ev}}{2 \pi {r}}{/tex} Magnetic moment of revolving electron {tex}{m} ={IA}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{{ev}}{2 \pi {r}} \times \pi {r}^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} {evr}{/tex}

The ends of the coil are connected to an external circuit by means of slip rings and brushes. Working - The coil is rotated in the uniform magnetic field by some external means. The rotation of the coil causes the magnetic flux through it to change, so an emf is induced in the coil. The equivalent current {tex}{I}=\frac{{q}}{{t}}=\frac{{e}}{\frac{2 \pi {r}}{{v}}}=\frac{{ev}}{2 \pi {r}}{/tex} Magnetic moment of revolving electron {tex}{m} ={IA}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{{ev}}{2 \pi {r}} \times \pi {r}^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} {evr}{/tex}

1. Construction - It consists of a coil placed in a magnetic field. The coil is mounted on a rotor shaft. The ends of the coil are connected to an external circuit by means of slip rings and brushes.
    Working - The coil is rotated in the uniform magnetic field by some external means. The rotation of the coil causes the magnetic flux through it to change, so an emf is induced in the coil.
  2. The equivalent current
    {tex}{I}=\frac{{q}}{{t}}=\frac{{e}}{\frac{2 \pi {r}}{{v}}}=\frac{{ev}}{2 \pi {r}}{/tex}
    Magnetic moment of revolving electron
    {tex}{m} ={IA}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{{ev}}{2 \pi {r}} \times \pi {r}^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{2} {evr}{/tex}

explanation: The polarity of induced emf is such that it tends to produce a current which opposes the change in magnetic flux that produced it. question_html:
1. What is meant by current sensitivity of a galvanometer ? Mention the factors on which it depends.
    2. A galvanometer of resistance G is converted into a voltmeter of range {tex}(0-V){/tex} by using a resistance R. Find the resistance, in terms of R and G , required to convert it into a voltmeter of range {tex}\left(0-\frac{{V}}{2}\right){/tex}.
  2. The magnetic flux through a coil of resistance {tex}5 \Omega{/tex} increases with time as:
    {tex} \phi=\left(2.0 {t}^3+5.0 {t}^2+6.0 {t}\right) {mWb}{/tex}
    Find the magnitude of induced current through the coil at {tex}{t}=2 {~s}{/tex}.
question_text: What is meant by current sensitivity of a galvanometer ? Mention the factors on which it depends. A galvanometer of resistance G is converted into a voltmeter of range {tex}(0-V){/tex} by using a resistance R. Find the resistance, in terms of R and G , required to convert it into a voltmeter of range {tex}\left(0-\frac{{V}}{2}\right){/tex} . The magnetic flux through a coil of resistance {tex}5 \Omega{/tex} increases with time as: {tex} \phi=\left(2.0 {t}^3+5.0 {t}^2+6.0 {t}\right) {mWb}{/tex} Find the magnitude of induced current through the coil at {tex}{t}=2 {~s}{/tex} .
explanation: Current sensitivity of galvanometer is defined as the deflection per unit current. {tex}\frac{\phi}{I}=\frac{N B A}{K}{/tex} Factors Number of turns in coil, Magnetic field intensity, Area of coil, Torsional Constant {tex} R=\frac{V}{I}-G {/tex} for (0-V) Range {tex} R_1=\frac{V}{2 I}-G{/tex} for {tex}\left(0-\frac{V}{2}\right){/tex} Range {tex} \frac{V}{I}=R+G {/tex} {tex} R_1=\left(\frac{R+G}{2}\right)-G {/tex} {tex} R_1=\frac{R-\vec{G}}{2} {/tex} {tex}\phi=\left(2.0 t^3+5.0 t^2+6.0 t\right) {mWb} {/tex} {tex} |\varepsilon|=\frac{d \phi}{d t}=50 \times 10^{-3} {~V} {/tex} {tex} {I}=\frac{|\varepsilon|}{R} {/tex} {tex} {I}=\frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{5} {~A}=10 {~mA} {/tex}
solution: Current sensitivity of galvanometer is defined as the deflection per unit current. {tex}\frac{\phi}{I}=\frac{N B A}{K}{/tex} Factors Number of turns in coil, Magnetic field intensity, Area of coil, Torsional Constant {tex} R=\frac{V}{I}-G {/tex} for (0-V) Range {tex} R_1=\frac{V}{2 I}-G{/tex} for {tex}\left(0-\frac{V}{2}\right){/tex} Range {tex} \frac{V}{I}=R+G {/tex} {tex} R_1=\left(\frac{R+G}{2}\right)-G {/tex} {tex} R_1=\frac{R-\vec{G}}{2} {/tex} {tex}\phi=\left(2.0 t^3+5.0 t^2+6.0 t\right) {mWb} {/tex} {tex} |\varepsilon|=\frac{d \phi}{d t}=50 \times 10^{-3} {~V} {/tex} {tex} {I}=\frac{|\varepsilon|}{R} {/tex} {tex} {I}=\frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{5} {~A}=10 {~mA} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Current sensitivity of galvanometer is defined as the deflection per unit current.
      {tex}\frac{\phi}{I}=\frac{N B A}{K}{/tex}
      Factors Number of turns in coil, Magnetic field intensity, Area of coil, Torsional Constant
    2. {tex} R=\frac{V}{I}-G {/tex} for (0-V) Range
      {tex} R_1=\frac{V}{2 I}-G{/tex} for {tex}\left(0-\frac{V}{2}\right){/tex} Range
      {tex} \frac{V}{I}=R+G {/tex}
      {tex} R_1=\left(\frac{R+G}{2}\right)-G {/tex}
      {tex} R_1=\frac{R-\vec{G}}{2} {/tex}
  2. {tex}\phi=\left(2.0 t^3+5.0 t^2+6.0 t\right) {mWb} {/tex}
    {tex} |\varepsilon|=\frac{d \phi}{d t}=50 \times 10^{-3} {~V} {/tex}
    {tex} {I}=\frac{|\varepsilon|}{R} {/tex}
    {tex} {I}=\frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{5} {~A}=10 {~mA} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction and mention the utility of Lenz's law. Obtain an expression for self-inductance of a coil in terms of its geometry and permeability of the medium.
  2. A resistance of {tex}20 \Omega{/tex}, a capacitance of {tex}80 \mu {~F}{/tex} and an inductor of 50 mH are connected in series. This combination is connected across a 220 V ac supply of variable frequency. When the frequency of supply equals the natural frequency of the circuit, calculate:
    1. angular frequency of supply
    2. impedance of the circuit
question_text: State Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction and mention the utility of Lenz's law. Obtain an expression for self-inductance of a coil in terms of its geometry and permeability of the medium. A resistance of {tex}20 \Omega{/tex} , a capacitance of {tex}80 \mu {~F}{/tex} and an inductor of 50 mH are connected in series. This combination is connected across a 220 V ac supply of variable frequency. When the frequency of supply equals the natural frequency of the circuit, calculate: angular frequency of supply impedance of the circuit
explanation: The magnitude of induced e.m.f in a circuit is equal to the time rate of change of magnetic flux through the circuit Utility of Lenz's law It give polarity of the induced e.m.f. Expression for self inductance Consider a long solenoid of cross-sectional area A and length 1, having {tex}n{/tex} turns per unit length. If {tex}I{/tex} is the current flowing in the solenoid, magnetic field inside the solenoids is {tex}{B}=\mu_{0} {nI}{/tex} Total magnetic flux linked with the solenoid is {tex}{N} \varphi_{{B}}=({nl})\left(\mu_{0} {nI}\right)({A}){/tex} {tex}{N} \varphi_{{B}}=\mu_{0} {n}^{2} {AlI}{/tex} Self inductance {tex}{L} =\frac{{N} \varphi_{{B}}}{{I}}{/tex} {tex}{~L} =\mu_{0} {n}^{2} {Al}{/tex} If solenoid is filled with a material of relative permeability {tex}\mu_{{r}}{/tex} , then {tex}{L}=\mu_{{r}} \mu_{0} {n}^{2} {Al}{/tex} Resonant angular frequency is {tex}\omega_{0}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{{LC}}}{/tex} {tex}\omega_{0}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{50 \times 10^{-3} \times 80 \times 10^{-6}}}=500 {rad} {~s}^{-1}{/tex} When frequency of supply is equal to natural frequency of the circuit {tex}Z=R{/tex} {tex}Z=20 \Omega{/tex}
solution: The magnitude of induced e.m.f in a circuit is equal to the time rate of change of magnetic flux through the circuit Utility of Lenz's law It give polarity of the induced e.m.f. Expression for self inductance Consider a long solenoid of cross-sectional area A and length 1, having {tex}n{/tex} turns per unit length. If {tex}I{/tex} is the current flowing in the solenoid, magnetic field inside the solenoids is {tex}{B}=\mu_{0} {nI}{/tex} Total magnetic flux linked with the solenoid is {tex}{N} \varphi_{{B}}=({nl})\left(\mu_{0} {nI}\right)({A}){/tex} {tex}{N} \varphi_{{B}}=\mu_{0} {n}^{2} {AlI}{/tex} Self inductance {tex}{L} =\frac{{N} \varphi_{{B}}}{{I}}{/tex} {tex}{~L} =\mu_{0} {n}^{2} {Al}{/tex} If solenoid is filled with a material of relative permeability {tex}\mu_{{r}}{/tex} , then {tex}{L}=\mu_{{r}} \mu_{0} {n}^{2} {Al}{/tex} Resonant angular frequency is {tex}\omega_{0}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{{LC}}}{/tex} {tex}\omega_{0}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{50 \times 10^{-3} \times 80 \times 10^{-6}}}=500 {rad} {~s}^{-1}{/tex} When frequency of supply is equal to natural frequency of the circuit {tex}Z=R{/tex} {tex}Z=20 \Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The magnitude of induced e.m.f in a circuit is equal to the time rate of change of magnetic flux through the circuit
    Utility of Lenz's law
    It give polarity of the induced e.m.f.
    Expression for self inductance
    Consider a long solenoid of cross-sectional area A and length 1, having {tex}n{/tex} turns per unit length. If {tex}I{/tex} is the current flowing in the solenoid, magnetic field inside the solenoids is
    {tex}{B}=\mu_{0} {nI}{/tex}
    Total magnetic flux linked with the solenoid is
    {tex}{N} \varphi_{{B}}=({nl})\left(\mu_{0} {nI}\right)({A}){/tex}
    {tex}{N} \varphi_{{B}}=\mu_{0} {n}^{2} {AlI}{/tex}
    Self inductance
    {tex}{L} =\frac{{N} \varphi_{{B}}}{{I}}{/tex}
    {tex}{~L} =\mu_{0} {n}^{2} {Al}{/tex}
    If solenoid is filled with a material of relative permeability {tex}\mu_{{r}}{/tex}, then
    {tex}{L}=\mu_{{r}} \mu_{0} {n}^{2} {Al}{/tex}
    1. Resonant angular frequency is
      {tex}\omega_{0}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{{LC}}}{/tex}
      {tex}\omega_{0}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{50 \times 10^{-3} \times 80 \times 10^{-6}}}=500 {rad} {~s}^{-1}{/tex}
    2. When frequency of supply is equal to natural frequency of the circuit
      {tex}Z=R{/tex}
      {tex}Z=20 \Omega{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the working of an ac generator. Obtain the expression for the emf induced at an instant 't'.
  2. A long, straight horizontal wire {tex}{X}^{\prime} {X}{/tex} is held stationary and carries a current of 3.0 A. A square loop MNPS of side 10 cm, carrying a current of 1.0 A is kept near the wire {tex}{X}^{\prime} {X}{/tex} as shown in the figure. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic force acting on the loop due to the wire.
question_text: With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the working of an ac generator. Obtain the expression for the emf induced at an instant 't'. A long, straight horizontal wire {tex}{X}^{\prime} {X}{/tex} is held stationary and carries a current of 3.0 A. A square loop MNPS of side 10 cm, carrying a current of 1.0 A is kept near the wire {tex}{X}^{\prime} {X}{/tex} as shown in the figure. Find the magnitude and direction of the net magnetic force acting on the loop due to the wire.
explanation: Working of ac generator When coil is rotated in a uniform magnetic field with a constant angular speed {tex}\omega{/tex} , magnetic flux through it changes. As a result, an e.m.f is induced in the coil. Flux linked with the coil at any instant ' {tex}t{/tex} ' is {tex}\varphi_{{B}} ={BA} \cos \omega {t}{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon =-{N} \frac{{~d} \varphi_{{B}}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon ={NBA} \omega \sin \omega {t}{/tex} {tex}{F}=\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{1} {I}_{2} 1}{2 \pi {r}}{/tex} force on arm MN of the loop {tex}{F}_{1} =\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3 \times 1 \times 10 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \pi \times 20 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}{~F}_{1} =3 \times 10^{-7} {~N}{/tex} Force is directed away from the wire Force on arm SP of the loop {tex}{F}_{2} =\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3 \times 1 \times 10 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \pi \times 30 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}{~F}_{2} =2 \times 10^{-7} {~N}{/tex} Force is directed towards the wire Net force on the loop {tex}{F}={F}_{1}-{F}_{2}=10^{-7} {~N}{/tex} Net force on the loop is away from the wire.
solution: Working of ac generator When coil is rotated in a uniform magnetic field with a constant angular speed {tex}\omega{/tex} , magnetic flux through it changes. As a result, an e.m.f is induced in the coil. Flux linked with the coil at any instant ' {tex}t{/tex} ' is {tex}\varphi_{{B}} ={BA} \cos \omega {t}{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon =-{N} \frac{{~d} \varphi_{{B}}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon ={NBA} \omega \sin \omega {t}{/tex} {tex}{F}=\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{1} {I}_{2} 1}{2 \pi {r}}{/tex} force on arm MN of the loop {tex}{F}_{1} =\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3 \times 1 \times 10 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \pi \times 20 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}{~F}_{1} =3 \times 10^{-7} {~N}{/tex} Force is directed away from the wire Force on arm SP of the loop {tex}{F}_{2} =\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3 \times 1 \times 10 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \pi \times 30 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}{~F}_{2} =2 \times 10^{-7} {~N}{/tex} Force is directed towards the wire Net force on the loop {tex}{F}={F}_{1}-{F}_{2}=10^{-7} {~N}{/tex} Net force on the loop is away from the wire.
solution_html:
1. Working of ac generator
    When coil is rotated in a uniform magnetic field with a constant angular speed {tex}\omega{/tex}, magnetic flux through it changes. As a result, an e.m.f is induced in the coil.
    Flux linked with the coil at any instant '{tex}t{/tex}' is
    {tex}\varphi_{{B}} ={BA} \cos \omega {t}{/tex}
    {tex}\varepsilon =-{N} \frac{{~d} \varphi_{{B}}}{{dt}}{/tex}
    {tex}\varepsilon ={NBA} \omega \sin \omega {t}{/tex}
  2. {tex}{F}=\frac{\mu_{0} {I}_{1} {I}_{2} 1}{2 \pi {r}}{/tex}
    force on arm MN of the loop
    {tex}{F}_{1} =\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3 \times 1 \times 10 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \pi \times 20 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
    {tex}{~F}_{1} =3 \times 10^{-7} {~N}{/tex}
    Force is directed away from the wire Force on arm SP of the loop
    {tex}{F}_{2} =\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3 \times 1 \times 10 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \pi \times 30 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
    {tex}{~F}_{2} =2 \times 10^{-7} {~N}{/tex}
    Force is directed towards the wire
    Net force on the loop
    {tex}{F}={F}_{1}-{F}_{2}=10^{-7} {~N}{/tex}
    Net force on the loop is away from the wire.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Define mutual inductance. Write its SI unit.
  2. Derive an expression for the mutual inductance of a system of two long coaxial solenoids of same length {tex}l{/tex}, having turns {tex}{N}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{N}_{2}{/tex} and of radii {tex}{r}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{r}_{2}\left( \gt {r}_{1}\right){/tex}.
question_text: Define mutual inductance. Write its SI unit. Derive an expression for the mutual inductance of a system of two long coaxial solenoids of same length {tex}l{/tex} , having turns {tex}{N}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{N}_{2}{/tex} and of radii {tex}{r}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{r}_{2}\left( \gt {r}_{1}\right){/tex} .
explanation: Mutual inductance between two coils is defined as the magnetic flux associated with a coil when unit current flows through neighbouring coil. SI unit of mutual inductance is henry (H). When current {tex}{I}_{2}{/tex} flows in outer solenoid, the resulting flux linkage with inner solenoid. {tex}{N}_{1} \phi_{1}={N}_{1} {~B}_{2} {~A}_{1}{/tex} {tex}{~N}_{1} \phi_{1}={N}_{1}\left(\frac{\mu_{0} {~N}_{2} {I}_{2}}{1}\right) \pi {r}_{1}^{2}{/tex} {tex}{~N}_{1} \phi_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0} {~N}_{1} {~N}_{2} \pi {r}_{1}^{2} {I}_{2}}{1}{/tex} ...(1) {tex}{~N}_{1} \phi_{1}={M}_{12} {I}_{2}{/tex} ...(2) From equations (1) and (2) {tex}{M}_{12}=\frac{\mu_{0} {~N}_{1} {~N}_{2} \pi {r}_{1}^{2}}{1}{/tex}
solution: Mutual inductance between two coils is defined as the magnetic flux associated with a coil when unit current flows through neighbouring coil. SI unit of mutual inductance is henry (H). When current {tex}{I}_{2}{/tex} flows in outer solenoid, the resulting flux linkage with inner solenoid. {tex}{N}_{1} \phi_{1}={N}_{1} {~B}_{2} {~A}_{1}{/tex} {tex}{~N}_{1} \phi_{1}={N}_{1}\left(\frac{\mu_{0} {~N}_{2} {I}_{2}}{1}\right) \pi {r}_{1}^{2}{/tex} {tex}{~N}_{1} \phi_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0} {~N}_{1} {~N}_{2} \pi {r}_{1}^{2} {I}_{2}}{1}{/tex} ...(1) {tex}{~N}_{1} \phi_{1}={M}_{12} {I}_{2}{/tex} ...(2) From equations (1) and (2) {tex}{M}_{12}=\frac{\mu_{0} {~N}_{1} {~N}_{2} \pi {r}_{1}^{2}}{1}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Mutual inductance between two coils is defined as the magnetic flux associated with a coil when unit current flows through neighbouring coil.
    SI unit of mutual inductance is henry (H).

  2. When current {tex}{I}_{2}{/tex} flows in outer solenoid, the resulting flux linkage with inner solenoid.
    {tex}{N}_{1} \phi_{1}={N}_{1} {~B}_{2} {~A}_{1}{/tex}
    {tex}{~N}_{1} \phi_{1}={N}_{1}\left(\frac{\mu_{0} {~N}_{2} {I}_{2}}{1}\right) \pi {r}_{1}^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}{~N}_{1} \phi_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0} {~N}_{1} {~N}_{2} \pi {r}_{1}^{2} {I}_{2}}{1}{/tex} ...(1)
    {tex}{~N}_{1} \phi_{1}={M}_{12} {I}_{2}{/tex} ...(2)
    From equations (1) and (2)
    {tex}{M}_{12}=\frac{\mu_{0} {~N}_{1} {~N}_{2} \pi {r}_{1}^{2}}{1}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A small circular loop of area {tex}\frac{6}{\pi} {~cm}^{2}{/tex} is placed inside a long solenoid at its centre such that its axis makes an angle of {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex} with the axis of the solenoid. The number of turns per cm is 10 in the solenoid. The current in the solenoid changes uniformly from 5 A to zero in 10 ms. Calculate the emf induced in the loop.
question_text: A small circular loop of area {tex}\frac{6}{\pi} {~cm}^{2}{/tex} is placed inside a long solenoid at its centre such that its axis makes an angle of {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex} with the axis of the solenoid. The number of turns per cm is 10 in the solenoid. The current in the solenoid changes uniformly from 5 A to zero in 10 ms. Calculate the emf induced in the loop.
explanation: E.m.f induced in the circular loop: {tex}|\varepsilon| =\frac{{d} \varphi}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{{d}}{{dt}}({BA} \cos \theta){/tex} {tex}=\frac{{d}}{{dt}}\left(\mu_{0} {nIA} \cos \theta\right){/tex} {tex}|\varepsilon| =\left(\mu_{0} {nA} \cos \theta \cdot \frac{{dI}}{{dt}}\right){/tex} {tex}|\varepsilon| =4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1000 \times \frac{6}{\pi} \times 10^{-4} \times \cos 60^{0} \times \frac{5}{10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}=6 \times 10^{-5} {~V}{/tex}
solution: E.m.f induced in the circular loop: {tex}|\varepsilon| =\frac{{d} \varphi}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{{d}}{{dt}}({BA} \cos \theta){/tex} {tex}=\frac{{d}}{{dt}}\left(\mu_{0} {nIA} \cos \theta\right){/tex} {tex}|\varepsilon| =\left(\mu_{0} {nA} \cos \theta \cdot \frac{{dI}}{{dt}}\right){/tex} {tex}|\varepsilon| =4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1000 \times \frac{6}{\pi} \times 10^{-4} \times \cos 60^{0} \times \frac{5}{10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}=6 \times 10^{-5} {~V}{/tex}
solution_html:
E.m.f induced in the circular loop:
{tex}|\varepsilon| =\frac{{d} \varphi}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{{d}}{{dt}}({BA} \cos \theta){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{{d}}{{dt}}\left(\mu_{0} {nIA} \cos \theta\right){/tex}
{tex}|\varepsilon| =\left(\mu_{0} {nA} \cos \theta \cdot \frac{{dI}}{{dt}}\right){/tex}
{tex}|\varepsilon| =4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1000 \times \frac{6}{\pi} \times 10^{-4} \times \cos 60^{0} \times \frac{5}{10^{-2}}{/tex}
{tex}=6 \times 10^{-5} {~V}{/tex}

question_html: Consider an induced magnetic field due to changing electric field and an induced electric field due to changing magnetic field. Which one is more easily observed? Justify your answer.
question_text: Consider an induced magnetic field due to changing electric field and an induced electric field due to changing magnetic field. Which one is more easily observed? Justify your answer.
explanation: Induced electric field due to changing magnetic field is easily observed. Induced electric field due to changing magnetic field can be easily produced by various ways like rotating/moving a coil in magnetic field, changing the shape of coil in magnetic field, bringing bar magnet near a coil etc.
solution: Induced electric field due to changing magnetic field is easily observed. Induced electric field due to changing magnetic field can be easily produced by various ways like rotating/moving a coil in magnetic field, changing the shape of coil in magnetic field, bringing bar magnet near a coil etc.
solution_html: Induced electric field due to changing magnetic field is easily observed. Two coils C1 and C2 are placed close to each other. The magnetic flux {tex}\phi_2{/tex} linked with the coil C2 varies with the current I1 flowing in coil C1, as shown in the figure. Find

question_text: Two coils C 1 and C 2 are placed close to each other. The magnetic flux {tex}\phi_2{/tex} linked with the coil C 2 varies with the current I 1 flowing in coil C 1 , as shown in the figure. Find the mutual inductance of the arrangement, and the rate of change of current {tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{dI}_1}{\mathrm{dt}}\right){/tex} that will induce of emf of 100 V in coil C 2 .
explanation: {tex}\phi_2=M I_1{/tex} Slope of the graph , M = {tex}\frac{\phi_2}{\mathrm{I}_1}{/tex} M = 2.5 H {tex}\left|\epsilon_2\right|=M \frac{d I_1}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d I_1}{d t}=\frac{\left|\epsilon_2\right|}{M}=\frac{100}{2.5}{/tex} = 40 As -1
solution: {tex}\phi_2=M I_1{/tex} Slope of the graph , M = {tex}\frac{\phi_2}{\mathrm{I}_1}{/tex} M = 2.5 H {tex}\left|\epsilon_2\right|=M \frac{d I_1}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d I_1}{d t}=\frac{\left|\epsilon_2\right|}{M}=\frac{100}{2.5}{/tex} = 40 As -1
solution_html:
1. {tex}\phi_2=M I_1{/tex}
    Slope of the graph , M = {tex}\frac{\phi_2}{\mathrm{I}_1}{/tex}
    M = 2.5 H
  2. {tex}\left|\epsilon_2\right|=M \frac{d I_1}{d t}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{d I_1}{d t}=\frac{\left|\epsilon_2\right|}{M}=\frac{100}{2.5}{/tex} = 40 As-1

explanation: Lenz’s law “ the polarity of induced e.m.f is such that it tends to produce a current which opposes the change in magnetic flux that produced it Direction of induced current is abcd or in anticlockwise sense.

question_html:
A horizontal straight metallic rod of length 4 m is held at some height above the surface of Earth, in east-west direction. If it is allowed to fall from rest, find the:

  1. emf induced in the rod 2 s after it starts falling,
  2. polarity of the emf induced, and
  3. the end of the rod which is at the higher potential.

The horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field at the place is 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 Wb/m2 and take g = 10 m/s2.
question_text: A horizontal straight metallic rod of length 4 m is held at some height above the surface of Earth, in east-west direction. If it is allowed to fall from rest, find the: emf induced in the rod 2 s after it starts falling, polarity of the emf induced, and the end of the rod which is at the higher potential. The horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field at the place is 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 Wb/m 2 and take g = 10 m/s 2 .
explanation: {tex}\varepsilon=B l v{/tex} B = 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 Wbm -2 v = 20 ms -1 l = 4m v = 0 + 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 v = 20 m/s {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 V = 2.4 mV hence, emf induced in the rod 2 s after it starts falling is 2.4 mV. West to east East end of the rod is at higher potential.
solution: {tex}\varepsilon=B l v{/tex} B = 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 Wbm -2 v = 20 ms -1 l = 4m v = 0 + 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 v = 20 m/s {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 V = 2.4 mV hence, emf induced in the rod 2 s after it starts falling is 2.4 mV. West to east East end of the rod is at higher potential.
solution_html:
1. {tex}\varepsilon=B l v{/tex}
    B = 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 Wbm-2
    v = 20 ms-1
    l = 4m
    v = 0 + 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 2
    v = 20 m/s
    {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 4
    = 24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 V = 2.4 mV
    hence, emf induced in the rod 2 s after it starts falling is 2.4 mV.
  2. West to east
  3. East end of the rod is at higher potential.

subject: Physics
question_html:
Answer the following, giving reasons:

  1. Induced emf does not always produce induced current.
  2. The motion of a copper plate is damped when it is allowed to oscillate between pole pieces of a strong magnet.
  3. No power is consumed in an ac circuit containing a pure inductor.
question_text: Answer the following, giving reasons: Induced emf does not always produce induced current. The motion of a copper plate is damped when it is allowed to oscillate between pole pieces of a strong magnet. No power is consumed in an ac circuit containing a pure inductor.
explanation: Even in the open loop e.m.f. can be induced but since circuit is open no current can flow. When copper plate oscillates between poles of a strong magnet eddy currents are generated in the plate opposing the change in flux. Power consumed by a pure inductor oscillates throghout the cycle. For first half of the cycle it is positive and for the next half it is negative. So for a complete cycle no power is consumed.
solution: Even in the open loop e.m.f. can be induced but since circuit is open no current can flow. When copper plate oscillates between poles of a strong magnet eddy currents are generated in the plate opposing the change in flux. Power consumed by a pure inductor oscillates throghout the cycle. For first half of the cycle it is positive and for the next half it is negative. So for a complete cycle no power is consumed.
solution_html:
1. Even in the open loop e.m.f. can be induced but since circuit is open no current can flow.
  2. When copper plate oscillates between poles of a strong magnet eddy currents are generated in the plate opposing the change in flux.
  3. Power consumed by a pure inductor oscillates throghout the cycle. For first half of the cycle it is positive and for the next half it is negative. So for a complete cycle no power is consumed.

question_html:
1. The figure shows the variation of induced emf as a function of rate of change of current for two identical solenoids X and Y. One is air cored and the other is iron cored. Which one of them is iron cored? Why?
  2. Obtain an expression for self-inductance of a long solenoid of length L and cross-sectional area A having N turns.
question_text: The figure shows the variation of induced emf as a function of rate of change of current for two identical solenoids X and Y. One is air cored and the other is iron cored. Which one of them is iron cored? Why? Obtain an expression for self-inductance of a long solenoid of length L and cross-sectional area A having N turns.
explanation: X is iron cored. Reason- From the graph- Slope of the graph = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{|d I / d t|}{/tex} = L Slope of X is more than that of Y. Hence X is iron cored because Inductance of iron cored coil is more than that of air cored coil. Magnetic field due to solenoid (axis) {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0 N I}{l}{/tex} Magnetic flux through the solenoid {tex}\phi_B=N \phi=\frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{L} I{/tex} Since self-inductance = {tex}\frac{\phi_B}{I}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{L}{/tex}
solution: X is iron cored. Reason- From the graph- Slope of the graph = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{|d I / d t|}{/tex} = L Slope of X is more than that of Y. Hence X is iron cored because Inductance of iron cored coil is more than that of air cored coil. Magnetic field due to solenoid (axis) {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0 N I}{l}{/tex} Magnetic flux through the solenoid {tex}\phi_B=N \phi=\frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{L} I{/tex} Since self-inductance = {tex}\frac{\phi_B}{I}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{L}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. X is iron cored.
    Reason- From the graph-
    Slope of the graph = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{|d I / d t|}{/tex} = L
    Slope of X is more than that of Y. Hence X is iron cored because Inductance of iron cored coil is more than that of air cored coil.
  2. Magnetic field due to solenoid (axis)
    {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0 N I}{l}{/tex}
    Magnetic flux through the solenoid
    {tex}\phi_B=N \phi=\frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{L} I{/tex}
    Since self-inductance = {tex}\frac{\phi_B}{I}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{L}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: State the basic principle behind the working of an ac generator. Briefly describe its working and obtain the expression for the instantaneous value of emf induced.
question_text: State the basic principle behind the working of an ac generator. Briefly describe its working and obtain the expression for the instantaneous value of emf induced.
explanation: Principle: Whenever magnetic flux linked through a coil changes an emf is induced. Working: The coil (armature) is mechanically rotated in the uniform magnetic field by some external means. The rotation of the coil causes the magnetic flux through it to change. So an emf is induced in the coil. Expression for induced emf Flux linked with the coil at any instant of time is- {tex}\phi_B=B A \cos \omega t{/tex} {tex}\epsilon=-N \frac{d \phi_B}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\epsilon=-N B A \frac{d}{d t}(\cos \omega t){/tex} Thus, instantaneous value of emf is {tex}\epsilon=N B A \omega \sin \omega t{/tex}
solution: Principle: Whenever magnetic flux linked through a coil changes an emf is induced. Working: The coil (armature) is mechanically rotated in the uniform magnetic field by some external means. The rotation of the coil causes the magnetic flux through it to change. So an emf is induced in the coil. Expression for induced emf Flux linked with the coil at any instant of time is- {tex}\phi_B=B A \cos \omega t{/tex} {tex}\epsilon=-N \frac{d \phi_B}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\epsilon=-N B A \frac{d}{d t}(\cos \omega t){/tex} Thus, instantaneous value of emf is {tex}\epsilon=N B A \omega \sin \omega t{/tex}
solution_html:
Principle: Whenever magnetic flux linked through a coil changes an emf is induced.
Working: The coil (armature) is mechanically rotated in the uniform magnetic field by some external means. The rotation of the coil causes the magnetic flux through it to change. So an emf is induced in the coil.
Expression for induced emf
Flux linked with the coil at any instant of time is-
{tex}\phi_B=B A \cos \omega t{/tex}
{tex}\epsilon=-N \frac{d \phi_B}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}\epsilon=-N B A \frac{d}{d t}(\cos \omega t){/tex}
Thus, instantaneous value of emf is
{tex}\epsilon=N B A \omega \sin \omega t{/tex}
