question_html: A rectangular frame of wire is placed in a uniform magnetic field directed outwards, normal to the paper. AB is connected to a spring which is stretched to A'B' and then released at time t = 0. Explain qualitatively how induced e.m.f. in the coil would vary with time. (Neglect damping of oscillations of spring)
question_text: A rectangular frame of wire is placed in a uniform magnetic field directed outwards, normal to the paper. AB is connected to a spring which is stretched to A'B' and then released at time t = 0. Explain qualitatively how induced e.m.f. in the coil would vary with time. (Neglect damping of oscillations of spring)
explanation: SHM nature of oscillation of the wire AB. Expression for instantaneous magnetic flux. Expression for instantaneous induced emf. Qualitative explanation The wire AB would oscillate in a simple harmonic way we can write x = -a cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t as x = -a at t = 0 Therefore instantaneous magnetic Flux {tex}\phi{/tex} (t) = Blx (l = AB) Instantaneous induced emf, e(t) = {tex}\frac {d \phi}{dt}{/tex} = aBl {tex}\omega{/tex} t sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t The induced emf, therefore varies with time sinusoidally.
solution: SHM nature of oscillation of the wire AB. Expression for instantaneous magnetic flux. Expression for instantaneous induced emf. Qualitative explanation The wire AB would oscillate in a simple harmonic way we can write x = -a cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t as x = -a at t = 0 Therefore instantaneous magnetic Flux {tex}\phi{/tex} (t) = Blx (l = AB) Instantaneous induced emf, e(t) = {tex}\frac {d \phi}{dt}{/tex} = aBl {tex}\omega{/tex} t sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t The induced emf, therefore varies with time sinusoidally.
solution_html:
SHM nature of oscillation of the wire AB.
Expression for instantaneous magnetic flux.
Expression for instantaneous induced emf.
Qualitative explanation
The wire AB would oscillate in a simple harmonic way
we can write
x = -a cos {tex}\omega{/tex}t
as x = -a at t = 0
Therefore instantaneous magnetic Flux
{tex}\phi{/tex}(t) = Blx (l = AB)
Instantaneous induced emf,
e(t) = {tex}\frac {d \phi}{dt}{/tex} = aBl{tex}\omega{/tex}t sin{tex}\omega{/tex}t
The induced emf, therefore varies with time sinusoidally.

subject: Physics
question_html:
In the Figure, shows a metal rod PQ resting on the smooth rails AB and positioned between the poles of a permanent magnet. The rails, the rod, and the magnetic field are in three mutual perpendicular directions. A galvanometer G connects the rails through a switch K. Length of the rod = 15 cm, B = 0.50 T, resistance of the closed-loop containing the rod = 9.0 m{tex}\Omega{/tex}. Assume the field to be uniform.

  1. Suppose K is open and the rod is moved with a speed of 12 cm s-1 in the direction shown. Give the polarity and magnitude of the induced emf.
  2. Is there an excess charge built up at the ends of the rods when K is open? What if K is closed?
  3. With K open and the rod moving uniformly, there is no net force on the electrons in the rod PQ even though they do experience a magnetic force due to the motion of the rod. Explain.
  4. What is the retarding force on the rod when K is closed?
  5. How much power is required (by an external agent) to keep the rod moving at the same speed (=12 cm s-1) when K is closed? How much power is required when K is open?
  6. How much power is dissipated as heat in the closed-circuit? What is the source of this power?
  7. What is the induced emf in the moving rod if the magnetic field is parallel to the rails instead of being perpendicular?
question_text: In the Figure, shows a metal rod PQ resting on the smooth rails AB and positioned between the poles of a permanent magnet. The rails, the rod, and the magnetic field are in three mutual perpendicular directions. A galvanometer G connects the rails through a switch K. Length of the rod = 15 cm, B = 0.50 T, resistance of the closed-loop containing the rod = 9.0 m {tex}\Omega{/tex} . Assume the field to be uniform. Suppose K is open and the rod is moved with a speed of 12 cm s -1 in the direction shown. Give the polarity and magnitude of the induced emf. Is there an excess charge built up at the ends of the rods when K is open? What if K is closed? With K open and the rod moving uniformly, there is no net force on the electrons in the rod PQ even though they do experience a magnetic force due to the motion of the rod. Explain. What is the retarding force on the rod when K is closed? How much power is required (by an external agent) to keep the rod moving at the same speed (=12 cm s -1 ) when K is closed? How much power is required when K is open? How much power is dissipated as heat in the closed-circuit? What is the source of this power? What is the induced emf in the moving rod if the magnetic field is parallel to the rails instead of being perpendicular?
explanation: Let length of the rod, l = 15 cm = 0.15 m Magnetic field strength, B = 0.50 T Resistance of the closed loop, R = 9m {tex}\Omega{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Induced emf = 9 mv; polarity of the induced emf is such that end P shows positive while end Q shows negative ends. Speed of the rod, v = 12cm/s = 0.12 m/s Induced emf is given as: e = Bvl {tex}=0.5 \times 0.12 \times 0.15{/tex} {tex}=9 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{v}{/tex} = 9 mV The polarity of the induced emf is such that end P shows positive while end Q shows negative ends. Yes; when key K is closed, excess charge is maintained by the continuous flow of current. When key k is open, there is excess charge built up at both ends of the rods. When key K is closed, excess charge is maintained by the continuous flow of current. Magnetic force is cancelled by the electric force set-up due to the excess charge of opposite nature at both ends of the rod. There is no net force on the electrons in rod PQ when key K is open and the rod is moving uniformly. This is because magnetic force is cancelled by the electric force set-up due to the excess charge of opposite nature at both ends of the rods. Retarding force exerted on the rod, F = IBl Where, I = current flowing through the rod {tex}=\frac{e}{R}=\frac{9 \times 10^{-3}}{9 \times 10^{-3}}=1 \mathrm{A}{/tex} {tex}\therefore F=1 \times 0.5 \times 0.15{/tex} {tex}=75 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{N}{/tex} 9 mW; no power is expended when key K is open. Speed of the rod, v = 12 cm/s = 0.12 m/s Hence, power is given as: P = Fv {tex}=75 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.12{/tex} {tex}=9 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{W}{/tex} = 9 mW When key K is open, now power is expended. 9 mW; power is provided by an external agent. Power dissipated as heat = I 2 R {tex}=(1)^{2} \times 9 \times 10^{-3}{/tex} = 9mW The source of this power is an external agent. zero In this case, no emf is induced in the coll because the motion of the rod does not cut across the field lines.
solution: Let length of the rod, l = 15 cm = 0.15 m Magnetic field strength, B = 0.50 T Resistance of the closed loop, R = 9m {tex}\Omega{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Induced emf = 9 mv; polarity of the induced emf is such that end P shows positive while end Q shows negative ends. Speed of the rod, v = 12cm/s = 0.12 m/s Induced emf is given as: e = Bvl {tex}=0.5 \times 0.12 \times 0.15{/tex} {tex}=9 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{v}{/tex} = 9 mV The polarity of the induced emf is such that end P shows positive while end Q shows negative ends. Yes; when key K is closed, excess charge is maintained by the continuous flow of current. When key k is open, there is excess charge built up at both ends of the rods. When key K is closed, excess charge is maintained by the continuous flow of current. Magnetic force is cancelled by the electric force set-up due to the excess charge of opposite nature at both ends of the rod. There is no net force on the electrons in rod PQ when key K is open and the rod is moving uniformly. This is because magnetic force is cancelled by the electric force set-up due to the excess charge of opposite nature at both ends of the rods. Retarding force exerted on the rod, F = IBl Where, I = current flowing through the rod {tex}=\frac{e}{R}=\frac{9 \times 10^{-3}}{9 \times 10^{-3}}=1 \mathrm{A}{/tex} {tex}\therefore F=1 \times 0.5 \times 0.15{/tex} {tex}=75 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{N}{/tex} 9 mW; no power is expended when key K is open. Speed of the rod, v = 12 cm/s = 0.12 m/s Hence, power is given as: P = Fv {tex}=75 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.12{/tex} {tex}=9 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{W}{/tex} = 9 mW When key K is open, now power is expended. 9 mW; power is provided by an external agent. Power dissipated as heat = I 2 R {tex}=(1)^{2} \times 9 \times 10^{-3}{/tex} = 9mW The source of this power is an external agent. zero In this case, no emf is induced in the coll because the motion of the rod does not cut across the field lines.
solution_html:
Let length of the rod, l = 15 cm = 0.15 m
Magnetic field strength, B = 0.50 T
Resistance of the closed loop, R = 9m{tex}\Omega{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3{tex}\Omega{/tex}

  1. Induced emf = 9 mv; polarity of the induced emf is such that end P shows positive while end Q shows negative ends.
    Speed of the rod, v = 12cm/s = 0.12 m/s
    Induced emf is given as:
    e = Bvl
    {tex}=0.5 \times 0.12 \times 0.15{/tex}
    {tex}=9 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{v}{/tex}
    = 9 mV
    The polarity of the induced emf is such that end P shows positive while end Q shows negative ends.
  2. Yes; when key K is closed, excess charge is maintained by the continuous flow of current.
    When key k is open, there is excess charge built up at both ends of the rods.
    When key K is closed, excess charge is maintained by the continuous flow of current.
  3. Magnetic force is cancelled by the electric force set-up due to the excess charge of opposite nature at both ends of the rod.
    There is no net force on the electrons in rod PQ when key K is open and the rod is moving uniformly. This is because magnetic force is cancelled by the electric force set-up due to the excess charge of opposite nature at both ends of the rods.
  4. Retarding force exerted on the rod, F = IBl
    Where,
    I = current flowing through the rod
    {tex}=\frac{e}{R}=\frac{9 \times 10^{-3}}{9 \times 10^{-3}}=1 \mathrm{A}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore F=1 \times 0.5 \times 0.15{/tex}
    {tex}=75 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{N}{/tex}
  5. 9 mW; no power is expended when key K is open.
    Speed of the rod, v = 12 cm/s = 0.12 m/s
    Hence, power is given as:
    P = Fv
    {tex}=75 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.12{/tex}
    {tex}=9 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{W}{/tex}
    = 9 mW
    When key K is open, now power is expended.
  6. 9 mW; power is provided by an external agent.
    Power dissipated as heat = I2 R
    {tex}=(1)^{2} \times 9 \times 10^{-3}{/tex}
    = 9mW
    The source of this power is an external agent.
  7. zero
    In this case, no emf is induced in the coll because the motion of the rod does not cut across the field lines.

subject: Physics
question_html: A rectangular wire frame, as shown in figure (a) is placed in uniform magnetic field directed upward and normal to the plane of the paper. The part AB is connected to a spring. The spring is stretched and released when the wire AB has come to the position A'B' (t=0). Explain qualitatively how induced emf in the coil would vary with time. Neglect damping of oscillations of the spring.
question_text: A rectangular wire frame, as shown in figure (a) is placed in uniform magnetic field directed upward and normal to the plane of the paper. The part AB is connected to a spring. The spring is stretched and released when the wire AB has come to the position A'B' (t=0). Explain qualitatively how induced emf in the coil would vary with time. Neglect damping of oscillations of the spring.
explanation: When the spring is stretched and released, the wire AB moves to and fro simple harmonically under the restoring force developed in the spring. The displacement of conductor AB at any instant will be x = -a cos {tex}\omega t{/tex} [x = -a at t = 0] Magnetic flux linked with the loop at any instant, {tex}\phi(t)=B l x=-B l a \cos \omega t{/tex} Due to change flux, induced emf is set up across AB. {tex}\varepsilon(t)=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=-\frac{d}{d t}(-B \operatorname{la} \cos \omega t)=B l a \omega \sin \omega t{/tex} Clearly, induced emf {tex}\varepsilon(t) {/tex} varies sinusoidally with time, as shown in figure.
solution: When the spring is stretched and released, the wire AB moves to and fro simple harmonically under the restoring force developed in the spring. The displacement of conductor AB at any instant will be x = -a cos {tex}\omega t{/tex} [x = -a at t = 0] Magnetic flux linked with the loop at any instant, {tex}\phi(t)=B l x=-B l a \cos \omega t{/tex} Due to change flux, induced emf is set up across AB. {tex}\varepsilon(t)=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=-\frac{d}{d t}(-B \operatorname{la} \cos \omega t)=B l a \omega \sin \omega t{/tex} Clearly, induced emf {tex}\varepsilon(t) {/tex} varies sinusoidally with time, as shown in figure.
solution_html:
When the spring is stretched and released, the wire AB moves to and fro simple harmonically under the restoring force developed in the spring. The displacement of conductor AB at any instant will be
x = -a cos {tex}\omega t{/tex} [x = -a at t = 0]
Magnetic flux linked with the loop at any instant,
{tex}\phi(t)=B l x=-B l a \cos \omega t{/tex}
Due to change flux, induced emf is set up across AB.
{tex}\varepsilon(t)=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=-\frac{d}{d t}(-B \operatorname{la} \cos \omega t)=B l a \omega \sin \omega t{/tex}
Clearly, induced emf {tex}\varepsilon(t) {/tex} varies sinusoidally with time, as shown in figure.

question_html:
1. State the principle of an ac generator and explain its working with the help of a labelled diagram. Obtain the expression for the emf induced in a coil having N turns each of cross-sectional area A, rotating with a constant angular speed {tex}\omega{/tex} in a magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex}, directed perpendicular to the axis of rotation.
  2. An aeroplane is flying horizontally from west to east with a velocity of 900 km/hour. Calculate the potential difference developed between the ends of its wings having a span of 20 m. The horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field is 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 T and the angle of dip is 30o.
question_text: State the principle of an ac generator and explain its working with the help of a labelled diagram. Obtain the expression for the emf induced in a coil having N turns each of cross-sectional area A, rotating with a constant angular speed {tex}\omega{/tex} in a magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} , directed perpendicular to the axis of rotation. An aeroplane is flying horizontally from west to east with a velocity of 900 km/hour. Calculate the potential difference developed between the ends of its wings having a span of 20 m. The horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field is 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T and the angle of dip is 30 o .
explanation: A.C. generator: A.C. generator is a device that converts mechanical energy into electric energy. Principle: It works on the principle of electromagnetic induction .Whenever a coil is rotated in a uniform magnetic field about an axis perpendicular to the field, the magnetic flux linked with coil changes and an induced emf is set up across its ends. Working: Armature coil of a large number of turns of copper wire wound over soft iron core is used to increase magnetic flux. Field magnets are used to apply a magnetic field in which the armature coil is rotated with its axis perpendicular to field lines. Slip rings used to provide movable contacts of armature coils with an external circuit containing the load. Brushes are the metallic pieces used to pass electric current from the armature coil to the external circuit containing the load. Theory: Consider a coil PQRS free to rotate in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} . The initial flux through the coil is maximum i.e., {tex}\phi{/tex} = BA but as the coil is rotating with angular velocity {tex}\omega{/tex} , at any instant 't' the flux is given by {tex}\phi{/tex} = BA cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = BA cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t As the coil rotates, the magnetic flux linked with it changes. An induced emf is set up in the coil which is given by, {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{d}{d t}(B A \cos \omega t){/tex} = {tex}\omega B A \sin \omega t{/tex} If the coil has N turns, then the total induced emf will be {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = {tex}N B A \omega \sin t{/tex} Thus, the induced emf varies sinusoidally with time 'T'. The value of induced emf is maximum when sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t = 1 or sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t = 90 o , i.e. when the plane of the coil parallel to the field {tex}\vec B{/tex} . Denoting this maximum value by {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex} , we have {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex} = NBA {tex}\omega{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex} sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t = sin 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} ft where / is the frequency of rotation of the coil. We consider the following special cases: When {tex}\omega{/tex} t = 0 o , the plane of the coil is perpendicular to B, sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t = sin 0 o so that {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 0 When {tex}\omega{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , the plane of coil is parallel to field B, sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} = 1, so that {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex} When {tex}\omega{/tex} t = {tex}\pi{/tex} , the plane of the coil is again perpendicular to B, sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t = sin {tex}\pi{/tex} = 0 so that {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 0 When {tex}\omega{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{3\pi}{2}{/tex} , the plane of the coil is again parallel to B, sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t - sin {tex}\frac{3\pi}{2}{/tex} = -1 so that {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = {tex}-\varepsilon_0{/tex} When {tex}\omega{/tex} = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} , the plane of the coil again becomes perpendicular to B after completing one rotation, sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t - sin 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} = 0 so that {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 0. As the coil continues to rotate in the same sense the same cycle of changes repeats again and again. Such an emf is called sinusoidal or alternating emf. Both the magnitude and direction of this emf changes regularly with time. The fact that an induced emf is set up in a coil when rotated a magnetic field forms the basic principle of a dynamo or a generator. The electric current produced varies sinusoidally with time, so is known as 'alternating current' and hence the generator is known as A.C. generator'. As {tex}\frac{B_{V}}{B_{H}}{/tex} = {tex}\tan \delta{/tex} B V = B H {tex}\tan \delta{/tex} and angle of dip =30 0 = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 tan 30 o and tan 30 0 = {tex}1\over\sqrt{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} thus the potential diffrence is given by ,e = B v lv = {tex}\frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 250 = 1.44 v

solution_html:
1. A.C. generator: A.C. generator is a device that converts mechanical energy into electric energy.
    Principle: It works on the principle of electromagnetic induction .Whenever a coil is rotated in a uniform magnetic field about an axis perpendicular to the field, the magnetic flux linked with coil changes and an induced emf is set up across its ends.
    Working: 
    1. Armature coil of a large number of turns of copper wire wound over soft iron core is used to increase magnetic flux.
    2. Field magnets are used to apply a magnetic field in which the armature coil is rotated with its axis perpendicular to field lines.
    3. Slip rings used to provide movable contacts of armature coils with an external circuit containing the load.
    4. Brushes are the metallic pieces used to pass electric current from the armature coil to the external circuit containing the load.


      Theory: Consider a coil PQRS free to rotate in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex}. The initial flux through the coil is maximum i.e., {tex}\phi{/tex} = BA but as the coil is rotating with angular velocity {tex}\omega{/tex}, at any instant 't' the flux is given by
      {tex}\phi{/tex} = BA cos{tex}\theta{/tex} = BA cos{tex}\omega{/tex}t

      As the coil rotates, the magnetic flux linked with it changes. An induced emf is set up in the coil which is given by,
      {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{d}{d t}(B A \cos \omega t){/tex} = {tex}\omega B A \sin \omega t{/tex}
      If the coil has N turns, then the total induced emf will be
      {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = {tex}N B A \omega \sin t{/tex}
      Thus, the induced emf varies sinusoidally with time 'T'. The value of induced emf is maximum when sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t = 1 or sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t = 90o, i.e. when the plane of the coil parallel to the field {tex}\vec B{/tex}. Denoting this maximum value by {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex}, we have {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex} = NBA{tex}\omega{/tex}
      {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex} sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t = sin 2{tex}\pi{/tex}ft where / is the frequency of rotation of the coil.
      We consider the following special cases:
      1. When {tex}\omega{/tex}t = 0o, the plane of the coil is perpendicular to B, sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t = sin 0o so that {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 0
      2. When {tex}\omega{/tex}t = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}, the plane of coil is parallel to field B, sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} = 1, so that {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex}
      3. When {tex}\omega{/tex}t = {tex}\pi{/tex}, the plane of the coil is again perpendicular to B, sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t = sin {tex}\pi{/tex} = 0 so that {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 0
      4. When {tex}\omega{/tex}t =  {tex}\frac{3\pi}{2}{/tex}, the plane of the coil is again parallel to B, sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t - sin {tex}\frac{3\pi}{2}{/tex} = -1 so that {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = {tex}-\varepsilon_0{/tex}
      5. When {tex}\omega{/tex} = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}, the plane of the coil again becomes perpendicular to B after completing one rotation, sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t - sin 2{tex}\pi{/tex} = 0 so that {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 0.

As the coil continues to rotate in the same sense the same cycle of changes repeats again and again. Such an emf is called sinusoidal or alternating emf. Both the magnitude and direction of this emf changes regularly with time.

  • The fact that an induced emf is set up in a coil when rotated a magnetic field forms the basic principle of a dynamo or a generator.
  • The electric current produced varies sinusoidally with time, so is known as 'alternating current' and hence the generator is known as A.C. generator'.
  1. As
    {tex}\frac{B_{V}}{B_{H}}{/tex} = {tex}\tan \delta{/tex}
    BV = BH {tex}\tan \delta{/tex} and angle of dip =300
    = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 tan 30o   and tan 300={tex}1\over\sqrt{3}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
     thus the potential diffrence is given by ,e = Bvlv
    = {tex}\frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 250
    = 1.44 v

question_html: State Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction.
question_text: State Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction.
explanation: First law: Whenever a conductor is placed in the varying magnetic field, electromagnetic fields are induced known as induced emf. If the circuit is closed, a current is also induced which are called induced current. 2nd Law: It states that the induced emf in a coil is equal to the rate of change of flux. Here the flux is the product of the number of turns in the coil and flux connected with the coil. The formula of Faraday’s law is given below: {tex}\varepsilon=-N\frac{d\phi}{dt}{/tex}
solution: First law: Whenever a conductor is placed in the varying magnetic field, electromagnetic fields are induced known as induced emf. If the circuit is closed, a current is also induced which are called induced current. 2nd Law: It states that the induced emf in a coil is equal to the rate of change of flux. Here the flux is the product of the number of turns in the coil and flux connected with the coil. The formula of Faraday’s law is given below: {tex}\varepsilon=-N\frac{d\phi}{dt}{/tex}
solution_html:
First law: Whenever a conductor is placed in the varying magnetic field, electromagnetic fields are induced known as induced emf. If the circuit is closed, a current is also induced which are called induced current.

2nd Law: It states that the induced emf in a coil is equal to the rate of change of flux. Here the flux is the product of the number of turns in the coil and flux connected with the coil. The formula of Faraday’s law is given below:
{tex}\varepsilon=-N\frac{d\phi}{dt}{/tex}

question_html: Define the term self-inductance of a coil. Write its SI unit.
question_text: Define the term self-inductance of a coil. Write its SI unit.
explanation: Self-inductance of a coil is equal to the total magnetic flux linked with the coil, when unit current passes through it. SI unit of self-inductance is Henry (H).
solution: Self-inductance of a coil is equal to the total magnetic flux linked with the coil, when unit current passes through it. SI unit of self-inductance is Henry (H).
solution_html:
Self-inductance of a coil is equal to the total magnetic flux linked with the coil, when unit current passes through it. 
SI unit of self-inductance is Henry (H).

question_html: State Lenz's law. Give one example to illustrate this law. "The Lenz's law is a consequence of the principle of conservation of energy." Justify this statement.
question_text: State Lenz's law. Give one example to illustrate this law. "The Lenz's law is a consequence of the principle of conservation of energy." Justify this statement.
explanation: According to Lenz's law, the direction of the induced current (caused by induced emf) is always such as to oppose the change causing it. {tex}\varepsilon = - k\frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}}{/tex} where k is a positive constant. The negative sign expresses Lenz's law. It means that the induced emf is such that, if the circuit is closed, the induced current opposes the change in flux. Example: When the north pole of a coil is brought near a closed coil, the direction of current induced in the coil is such as to oppose the approach of north pole. For this the nearer face of coil behaves as north pole. This necessitates an anticlockwise current in the coil, when seen from the magnet side [fig. (a)] Similarly when north pole of the magnet is moved away from the coil, the direction of current in the coil will be such as to attract the magnet. For this the nearer face of coil behaves as south pole. This necessitates a clockwise current in the coil, when seen from the magnet side (fig. b). Thus, in each case whenever there is a relative motion between a coil and the magnet, a force begins to act which opposes the relative motion. Therefore to maintain the relative motion, a mechanical work must be done. This work appears in the form of electric energy of coil. Thus Lenz’s law is based on conservation of energy.

For this the nearer face of coil behaves as south pole. This necessitates a clockwise current in the coil, when seen from the magnet side (fig. b). Thus, in each case whenever there is a relative motion between a coil and the magnet, a force begins to act which opposes the relative motion. Therefore to maintain the relative motion, a mechanical work must be done. This work appears in the form of electric energy of coil. Thus Lenz’s law is based on conservation of energy.
solution_html:
According to Lenz's law, the direction of the induced current (caused by induced emf) is always such as to oppose the change causing it.
{tex}\varepsilon = - k\frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}}{/tex}
where k is a positive constant. The negative sign expresses Lenz's law. It means that the induced emf is such that, if the circuit is closed, the induced current opposes the change in flux.
Example: When the north pole of a coil is brought near a closed coil, the direction of current induced in the coil is such as to oppose the approach of north pole. For this the nearer face of coil behaves as north pole. This necessitates an anticlockwise current in the coil, when seen from the magnet side [fig. (a)]

Similarly when north pole of the magnet is moved away from the coil, the direction of current in the coil will be such as to attract the magnet. For this the nearer face of coil behaves as south pole. This necessitates a clockwise current in the coil, when seen from the magnet side (fig. b).

Thus, in each case whenever there is a relative motion between a coil and the magnet, a force begins to act which opposes the relative motion. Therefore to maintain the relative motion, a mechanical work must be done. This work appears in the form of electric energy of coil. Thus Lenz’s law is based on conservation of energy.

question_html:
1. A closed-loop is held stationary in the magnetic field between the north and south poles of two permanent magnets held fixed. Can we hope to generate current in the loop by using very strong magnets?
  2. A closed-loop move normal to the constant electric field between the plates of a large capacitor. Is a current induced in the loop?
    1. when it is wholly inside the region between the capacitor plates.
    2. when it is partially outside the plates of the capacitor? The electric field is normal to the plane of the loop.
  3. A rectangular loop and a circular loop are moving out of a uniform magnetic field region (Fig.) to a field-free region with a constant velocity v. In which loop do you expect the induced emf to be constant during the passage out of the field region? The field is normal to the loops.
  4. Predict the polarity of the capacitor in the situation described by Figure.
question_text: A closed-loop is held stationary in the magnetic field between the north and south poles of two permanent magnets held fixed. Can we hope to generate current in the loop by using very strong magnets? A closed-loop move normal to the constant electric field between the plates of a large capacitor. Is a current induced in the loop? when it is wholly inside the region between the capacitor plates. when it is partially outside the plates of the capacitor? The electric field is normal to the plane of the loop. A rectangular loop and a circular loop are moving out of a uniform magnetic field region (Fig.) to a field-free region with a constant velocity v . In which loop do you expect the induced emf to be constant during the passage out of the field region? The field is normal to the loops. Predict the polarity of the capacitor in the situation described by Figure.
explanation: No. However strong the magnet may be, current can be induced only by changing the magnetic flux through the loop. Or, The electricity get generated due to the phenomenon of electromagnetic induction. This happens due to changing of magnetic force by changing the position of the magnet but in this case, the magnet is held stationary. So no current is generated. No current is induced in either case. Current can not be induced by changing the electric flux. The induced emf is expected to be constant only in the case of the rectangular loop. In the case of a circular loop, the rate of change of the area of the loop during its passage out of the field region is not constant, hence induced emf will vary accordingly. The polarity of plate ‘A’ will be positive with respect to plate ‘B’ in the capacitor.

No current is induced in either case. Current can not be induced by changing the electric flux. The induced emf is expected to be constant only in the case of the rectangular loop. In the case of a circular loop, the rate of change of the area of the loop during its passage out of the field region is not constant, hence induced emf will vary accordingly. The polarity of plate ‘A’ will be positive with respect to plate ‘B’ in the capacitor.
solution_html:
1. No. However strong the magnet may be, current can be induced only by changing the magnetic flux through the loop. Or, The electricity get generated due to the phenomenon of electromagnetic induction. This happens due to changing of magnetic force by changing the position of the magnet but in this case, the magnet is held stationary. So no current is generated.
  2. No current is induced in either case. Current can not be induced by changing the electric flux.
  3. The induced emf is expected to be constant only in the case of the rectangular loop. In the case of a circular loop, the rate of change of the area of the loop during its passage out of the field region is not constant, hence induced emf will vary accordingly.
  4. The polarity of plate ‘A’ will be positive with respect to plate ‘B’ in the capacitor.

question_html: A bar magnet is moved in the direction indicated by an arrow between two coils AB and CD as shown in the figure. Indicate the direction of the induced current in each coil.
question_text: A bar magnet is moved in the direction indicated by an arrow between two coils AB and CD as shown in the figure. Indicate the direction of the induced current in each coil.
explanation: By Lenz's law, the ends of both the coils closer to the magnet will behave as south poles. Hence the current induced in both the coils will flow clockwise when seen from the magnet side. So the direction of current is: B to A through the ammeter. D to C through the ammeter.
solution: By Lenz's law, the ends of both the coils closer to the magnet will behave as south poles. Hence the current induced in both the coils will flow clockwise when seen from the magnet side. So the direction of current is: B to A through the ammeter. D to C through the ammeter.
solution_html:
By Lenz's law, the ends of both the coils closer to the magnet will behave as south poles. Hence the current induced in both the coils will flow clockwise when seen from the magnet side. So the direction of current is:
B to A through the ammeter.
D to C through the ammeter.

question_html:
1. Define mutual inductance and write its S.I. unit.
  2. Derive an expression for the mutual inductance of two long co-axial solenoids of the same length wound one over the other.
  3. In an experiment, two coils C1 and C2 are placed close to each other. Find out the expression for the emf induced the coil C1 due to a change in the current through the coil C2.
question_text: Define mutual inductance and write its S.I. unit. Derive an expression for the mutual inductance of two long co-axial solenoids of the same length wound one over the other. In an experiment, two coils C 1 and C 2 are placed close to each other. Find out the expression for the emf induced the coil C 1 due to a change in the current through the coil C 2 .
explanation: When two coils (or solenoids) are mutually coupled, then change in current of one coil causes a change in magnetic flux in the other coil and as a result an emf is induced in the other coil. This process is called mutual induction. Or Mutual Inductance is the interaction of one coils magnetic field on another coil as it induces a voltage in the adjacent coil. SI unit of mutual inductance = Henry (H) Let a current l 2 flow in the secondary coil {tex}\therefore{/tex} B 2 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N_{2} i_{2}}{l}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Flux linked with the primary coil = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N_{2} N_{1} A_{1} i_{2}}{l}{/tex} = M 12 i 2 Hence, M 12 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N_{2} N_{1} A_{1}}{l}{/tex} {tex}\mu_{0} n_{2} n_{1} A_{1} l{/tex} {tex}\left(n_{1}=\frac{N_{1}}{l} ; n_{2}=\frac{N_{2}}{l}\right){/tex} Let a magnetic flux be ( {tex}\phi_1{/tex} ) linked with coil C 1 due to current (I 2 ) in coil C 2 . We have: {tex}\phi_1{/tex} {tex}\propto{/tex} I 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\phi_1{/tex} = MI 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d \phi_{1}}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}M \frac{d I_{2}}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e = {tex}-M \frac{d I_{2}}{d t}{/tex}
solution: When two coils (or solenoids) are mutually coupled, then change in current of one coil causes a change in magnetic flux in the other coil and as a result an emf is induced in the other coil. This process is called mutual induction. Or Mutual Inductance is the interaction of one coils magnetic field on another coil as it induces a voltage in the adjacent coil. SI unit of mutual inductance = Henry (H) Let a current l 2 flow in the secondary coil {tex}\therefore{/tex} B 2 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N_{2} i_{2}}{l}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Flux linked with the primary coil = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N_{2} N_{1} A_{1} i_{2}}{l}{/tex} = M 12 i 2 Hence, M 12 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N_{2} N_{1} A_{1}}{l}{/tex} {tex}\mu_{0} n_{2} n_{1} A_{1} l{/tex} {tex}\left(n_{1}=\frac{N_{1}}{l} ; n_{2}=\frac{N_{2}}{l}\right){/tex} Let a magnetic flux be ( {tex}\phi_1{/tex} ) linked with coil C 1 due to current (I 2 ) in coil C 2 . We have: {tex}\phi_1{/tex} {tex}\propto{/tex} I 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\phi_1{/tex} = MI 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d \phi_{1}}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}M \frac{d I_{2}}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e = {tex}-M \frac{d I_{2}}{d t}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. When two coils (or solenoids) are mutually coupled, then change in current of one coil causes a change in magnetic flux in the other coil and as a result an emf is induced in the other coil. This process is called mutual induction. Or Mutual Inductance is the interaction of one coils magnetic field on another coil as it induces a voltage in the adjacent coil.
    SI unit of mutual inductance = Henry (H)


  2. Let a current l2 flow in the secondary coil
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} B2 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N_{2} i_{2}}{l}{/tex} 
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Flux linked with the primary coil = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N_{2} N_{1} A_{1} i_{2}}{l}{/tex}
    = M12i2
    Hence, M12 = {tex}\frac{\mu_{0} N_{2} N_{1} A_{1}}{l}{/tex} 
    {tex}\mu_{0} n_{2} n_{1} A_{1} l{/tex} {tex}\left(n_{1}=\frac{N_{1}}{l} ; n_{2}=\frac{N_{2}}{l}\right){/tex}
  3. Let a magnetic flux be ({tex}\phi_1{/tex}) linked with coil C1 due to current (I2) in coil C2.
    We have:
    {tex}\phi_1{/tex} {tex}\propto{/tex} I2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\phi_1{/tex} = MI2
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d \phi_{1}}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}M \frac{d I_{2}}{d t}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e = {tex}-M \frac{d I_{2}}{d t}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: What is the direction of induced current in metal rings 1 and 2, when current I in the wire is increasing steadily?
question_text: What is the direction of induced current in metal rings 1 and 2, when current I in the wire is increasing steadily?
explanation: The direction of induced current is predicted using Lenz's law. So, current is induced in a direction so as to oppose the increasing magnetic flux. Therefore, direction of current is clockwise in ring 1 and anticlockwise in ring 2.
solution: The direction of induced current is predicted using Lenz's law. So, current is induced in a direction so as to oppose the increasing magnetic flux. Therefore, direction of current is clockwise in ring 1 and anticlockwise in ring 2.
solution_html:
The direction of induced current is predicted using Lenz's law. So, current is induced in a direction so as to oppose the increasing magnetic flux.
Therefore, direction of current is clockwise in ring 1 and anticlockwise in ring 2.

subject: Physics
question_html: A long straight current carrying wire passes normally through the centre of circular loop. If the current through the wire increases, will there be an induced emf in the loop? Justify.
question_text: A long straight current carrying wire passes normally through the centre of circular loop. If the current through the wire increases, will there be an induced emf in the loop? Justify.
explanation: From Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, induced emf (e) {tex} \propto {/tex} rate of change of magnetic flux {tex}({\phi _B}){/tex} with time Thus, {tex}{\phi _B} = \vec B . \vec A{/tex} = BAcos {tex}~ \theta{/tex} Now from the given figure, it is clear that B is perpendicular to A i.e. {tex}B \bot A~ \therefore {\phi _B} = BA\cos {90^0} = 0{/tex} So, induced emf = 0 Hence, a change in current in the wire will not create any emf in the loop.
solution: From Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, induced emf (e) {tex} \propto {/tex} rate of change of magnetic flux {tex}({\phi _B}){/tex} with time Thus, {tex}{\phi _B} = \vec B . \vec A{/tex} = BAcos {tex}~ \theta{/tex} Now from the given figure, it is clear that B is perpendicular to A i.e. {tex}B \bot A~ \therefore {\phi _B} = BA\cos {90^0} = 0{/tex} So, induced emf = 0 Hence, a change in current in the wire will not create any emf in the loop.
solution_html:
From Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction,
induced emf (e) {tex} \propto {/tex} rate of change of magnetic flux {tex}({\phi _B}){/tex} with time
Thus, {tex}{\phi _B} = \vec B . \vec A{/tex} = BAcos{tex}~ \theta{/tex}
Now from the given figure, it is clear that B is perpendicular to A i.e.
{tex}B \bot A~ \therefore {\phi _B} = BA\cos {90^0} = 0{/tex}
So, induced emf = 0
Hence, a change in current in the wire will not create any emf in the loop.

question_text: For MRI, a patient is slowly pushed in a time of 10 s within the coils of the magnet where magnetic field is B = 2.0 T. If the patient's trunk is 0.8 m in circumference, the induced emf around the patient's trunk is

explanation: Here, A = 200 cm 2 N = 20 {tex}\omega=50 \frac{\mathrm{rad}}{\mathrm{s}}{/tex} {tex}\mathbf{B}=3 \times \mathbf{1 0}^{-2} \mathbf{T}{/tex} For maximum current emf should be maximum. So for maximum emf e = NAB {tex}\omega \sin \omega t{/tex} {tex}\sin \omega t=1{/tex} {tex}\mathbf{e}_{\max }=\mathbf{N} \mathbf{A B} \omega{/tex} {tex}e_{\max }=20 \times 200 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} {tex}\times 50 \times 3 \times 10^{-2}{/tex} e max = 0.6 V Since resistance is not given, let us consider R {tex}\mathrm{i}_{\max }=\frac{\mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{max}}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{0.6}{\mathrm{R}}{/tex}
solution: Here, A = 200 cm 2 N = 20 {tex}\omega=50 \frac{\mathrm{rad}}{\mathrm{s}}{/tex} {tex}\mathbf{B}=3 \times \mathbf{1 0}^{-2} \mathbf{T}{/tex} For maximum current emf should be maximum. So for maximum emf e = NAB {tex}\omega \sin \omega t{/tex} {tex}\sin \omega t=1{/tex} {tex}\mathbf{e}_{\max }=\mathbf{N} \mathbf{A B} \omega{/tex} {tex}e_{\max }=20 \times 200 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} {tex}\times 50 \times 3 \times 10^{-2}{/tex} e max = 0.6 V Since resistance is not given, let us consider R {tex}\mathrm{i}_{\max }=\frac{\mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{max}}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{0.6}{\mathrm{R}}{/tex}

question_html: A metallic rod of length l is moved perpendicular to its length with velocity v in a magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} acting perpendicular to the plane in which rod moves. Derive the expression for the induced emf.
question_text: A metallic rod of length l is moved perpendicular to its length with velocity v in a magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} acting perpendicular to the plane in which rod moves. Derive the expression for the induced emf.
explanation: Let the conductor PQ of length = 1, move freely in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} . Let any arbitrary charge q also move in the circuit with same velocity. Lorentz Force = F m = Bqv WD in moving charge from P to Q = W = F m {tex}\times{/tex} L w = qvBl Now, {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{w D}{q}=\frac{q v B l}{q}{/tex} or {tex}\varepsilon=B l v{/tex}
solution: Let the conductor PQ of length = 1, move freely in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} . Let any arbitrary charge q also move in the circuit with same velocity. Lorentz Force = F m = Bqv WD in moving charge from P to Q = W = F m {tex}\times{/tex} L w = qvBl Now, {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{w D}{q}=\frac{q v B l}{q}{/tex} or {tex}\varepsilon=B l v{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the conductor PQ of length = 1, move freely in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex}. Let any arbitrary charge q also move in the circuit with same velocity.
Lorentz Force = Fm = Bqv
WD in moving charge from P to Q = W = Fm {tex}\times{/tex} L
w = qvBl
Now, {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{w D}{q}=\frac{q v B l}{q}{/tex} or {tex}\varepsilon=B l v{/tex}

question_html: A wheel with 15 metallic spokes each 60 cm long is rotated at 360 rev/min in a plane normal to the horizontal component of earth's magnetic field. The angle of dip at that place is 60o. If the emf induced between rim of the wheel and the axle is 400 mV. calculate the horizontal component of earth's magnetic field at the place. How will the induced emf change, if the number of spokes is increased?
question_text: A wheel with 15 metallic spokes each 60 cm long is rotated at 360 rev/min in a plane normal to the horizontal component of earth's magnetic field. The angle of dip at that place is 60 o . If the emf induced between rim of the wheel and the axle is 400 mV. calculate the horizontal component of earth's magnetic field at the place. How will the induced emf change, if the number of spokes is increased?
explanation: Here, N = 15, r = 60cm = 0.6m n = 360 rpm {tex}=\frac{360}{60}=6 \mathrm{rps}{/tex} {tex}\delta=60^{\circ}{/tex} , e = 400mV {tex}=400 \times 10^{-3} V{/tex} = 0.4V H = ? As the wheel rotates, linear velocity of spoke end at the rim = r {tex}\omega{/tex} , and linear velocity of spoke end at the axle is zero. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Average linear velocity {tex}v=\frac{0+r \omega}{2}=\frac{r \omega}{2}{/tex} e.m.f. induced across ends of each spoke. e = Blv {tex}=H r\left(\frac{r \omega}{2}\right)=\frac{H}{2} r^{2} \omega{/tex} {tex}0.4=\frac{H}{2}(0.6)^{2}(2 \pi \times 6){/tex} {tex}H=\frac{0.8}{12 \pi(0.36)}=0.059 T{/tex} As the spokes are in parallel in induced e.m.f. is not affected by changing their number.
solution: Here, N = 15, r = 60cm = 0.6m n = 360 rpm {tex}=\frac{360}{60}=6 \mathrm{rps}{/tex} {tex}\delta=60^{\circ}{/tex} , e = 400mV {tex}=400 \times 10^{-3} V{/tex} = 0.4V H = ? As the wheel rotates, linear velocity of spoke end at the rim = r {tex}\omega{/tex} , and linear velocity of spoke end at the axle is zero. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Average linear velocity {tex}v=\frac{0+r \omega}{2}=\frac{r \omega}{2}{/tex} e.m.f. induced across ends of each spoke. e = Blv {tex}=H r\left(\frac{r \omega}{2}\right)=\frac{H}{2} r^{2} \omega{/tex} {tex}0.4=\frac{H}{2}(0.6)^{2}(2 \pi \times 6){/tex} {tex}H=\frac{0.8}{12 \pi(0.36)}=0.059 T{/tex} As the spokes are in parallel in induced e.m.f. is not affected by changing their number.
solution_html:
Here, N = 15, r = 60cm = 0.6m
n = 360 rpm {tex}=\frac{360}{60}=6 \mathrm{rps}{/tex}
{tex}\delta=60^{\circ}{/tex}, e = 400mV {tex}=400 \times 10^{-3} V{/tex} = 0.4V
H = ?
As the wheel rotates, linear velocity of spoke end at the rim = r{tex}\omega{/tex}, and linear velocity of spoke end at the axle is zero.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Average linear velocity {tex}v=\frac{0+r \omega}{2}=\frac{r \omega}{2}{/tex}
e.m.f. induced across ends of each spoke.
e = Blv {tex}=H r\left(\frac{r \omega}{2}\right)=\frac{H}{2} r^{2} \omega{/tex}
{tex}0.4=\frac{H}{2}(0.6)^{2}(2 \pi \times 6){/tex}
{tex}H=\frac{0.8}{12 \pi(0.36)}=0.059 T{/tex}
As the spokes are in parallel in induced e.m.f. is not affected by changing their number.

question_html: Obtain the expression for the mutual inductance of two long co-axial solenoids S1 and S2 wound one over the other, each of length L and radii r1 and r2 and n1 and n2 number of turns per unit length, when a current I is set up in the outer solenoid S2.
question_text: Obtain the expression for the mutual inductance of two long co-axial solenoids S 1 and S 2 wound one over the other, each of length L and radii r 1 and r 2 and n 1 and n 2 number of turns per unit length, when a current I is set up in the outer solenoid S 2 .
explanation: Let a current l 2 flow through the outer coil. The magnetic field due to this current {tex}=\mu_{0} \frac{\mathrm{N}_{2}}{l} \times \mathrm{I}_{2}{/tex} The resulting magnetic flux linked with inner coil. {tex}=\phi_{12}=\mathrm{N}_{1}\left(\mu_{0} \frac{\mathrm{N}_{2}}{l} \times \mathrm{I}_{2}\right) \times \pi r_{1}^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\mu_{0} \frac{\mathrm{N}_{1} \mathrm{~N}_{2}}{l} \pi r_{1}^{2}\right) \mathrm{I}_{2}{/tex} = M 12 I 2 {tex}\mathrm{M}_{12}=\mu_{0} \frac{\mathrm{N}_{1} \mathrm{~N}_{2}}{l} \cdot \pi r_{1}^{2}{/tex}
solution: Let a current l 2 flow through the outer coil. The magnetic field due to this current {tex}=\mu_{0} \frac{\mathrm{N}_{2}}{l} \times \mathrm{I}_{2}{/tex} The resulting magnetic flux linked with inner coil. {tex}=\phi_{12}=\mathrm{N}_{1}\left(\mu_{0} \frac{\mathrm{N}_{2}}{l} \times \mathrm{I}_{2}\right) \times \pi r_{1}^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\mu_{0} \frac{\mathrm{N}_{1} \mathrm{~N}_{2}}{l} \pi r_{1}^{2}\right) \mathrm{I}_{2}{/tex} = M 12 I 2 {tex}\mathrm{M}_{12}=\mu_{0} \frac{\mathrm{N}_{1} \mathrm{~N}_{2}}{l} \cdot \pi r_{1}^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let a current l2 flow through the outer coil.
The magnetic field due to this current {tex}=\mu_{0} \frac{\mathrm{N}_{2}}{l} \times \mathrm{I}_{2}{/tex}
The resulting magnetic flux linked with inner coil.
{tex}=\phi_{12}=\mathrm{N}_{1}\left(\mu_{0} \frac{\mathrm{N}_{2}}{l} \times \mathrm{I}_{2}\right) \times \pi r_{1}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\left(\mu_{0} \frac{\mathrm{N}_{1} \mathrm{~N}_{2}}{l} \pi r_{1}^{2}\right) \mathrm{I}_{2}{/tex}
= M12I2
{tex}\mathrm{M}_{12}=\mu_{0} \frac{\mathrm{N}_{1} \mathrm{~N}_{2}}{l} \cdot \pi r_{1}^{2}{/tex}

question_html: A rectangular coil of area A, having number of turns N is rotated at 'f' revolutions per second in a uniform magnetic field B, the field being perpendicular to the coil. Prove that the maximum emf induced in the coil is 2{tex}\pi{/tex}f NBA.
question_text: A rectangular coil of area A, having number of turns N is rotated at 'f' revolutions per second in a uniform magnetic field B, the field being perpendicular to the coil. Prove that the maximum emf induced in the coil is 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} f NBA.
explanation: We know that flux is given by {tex}\phi=N B A \cos \theta{/tex} We also know, {tex}e=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=\left(-N B A(-\sin \theta) \frac{d \theta}{d t}\right){/tex} {tex}=N B A \sin \theta(2 \pi f){/tex} For maximum induced emf = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1 {tex}e_{\max }=\left(-N B A \sin \theta \frac{d \theta}{d t}\right){/tex} = NBA(2 {tex}\pi{/tex} f) Hence proved.
solution: We know that flux is given by {tex}\phi=N B A \cos \theta{/tex} We also know, {tex}e=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=\left(-N B A(-\sin \theta) \frac{d \theta}{d t}\right){/tex} {tex}=N B A \sin \theta(2 \pi f){/tex} For maximum induced emf = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1 {tex}e_{\max }=\left(-N B A \sin \theta \frac{d \theta}{d t}\right){/tex} = NBA(2 {tex}\pi{/tex} f) Hence proved.
solution_html:
We know that flux is given by
{tex}\phi=N B A \cos \theta{/tex}
We also know,
{tex}e=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=\left(-N B A(-\sin \theta) \frac{d \theta}{d t}\right){/tex}
{tex}=N B A \sin \theta(2 \pi f){/tex}
For maximum induced emf = sin{tex}\theta{/tex} = 1
{tex}e_{\max }=\left(-N B A \sin \theta \frac{d \theta}{d t}\right){/tex}
= NBA(2{tex}\pi{/tex}f)
Hence proved.

question_text: Two circular coils, one of radius r and the other of radius R are placed coaxially with their centres coinciding. For R > > r, obtain an expression for the mutual inductance of the arrangement.
explanation: Suppose a current I 2 flows through the outer circular coil. Magnetic field at the centre of the coil is {tex}{B_2} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_2}}}{{2R}}{/tex} Field B 2 may be considered constant over the cross-sectional area of the inner smaller coil. Hence, {tex}\phi = B.A{/tex} {tex}{\phi _1} = \pi {r^2}{B_2} = \frac{{{\mu _0}\pi {r^2}{I_2}}}{{2R}} = M{I_2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore M = \frac{{{\phi _1}}}{{{I_2}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}\pi {r^2}}}{{2R}}{/tex}
solution: Suppose a current I 2 flows through the outer circular coil. Magnetic field at the centre of the coil is {tex}{B_2} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_2}}}{{2R}}{/tex} Field B 2 may be considered constant over the cross-sectional area of the inner smaller coil. Hence, {tex}\phi = B.A{/tex} {tex}{\phi _1} = \pi {r^2}{B_2} = \frac{{{\mu _0}\pi {r^2}{I_2}}}{{2R}} = M{I_2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore M = \frac{{{\phi _1}}}{{{I_2}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}\pi {r^2}}}{{2R}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Suppose a current I2 flows through the outer circular coil. Magnetic field at the centre of the coil is
{tex}{B_2} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_2}}}{{2R}}{/tex}
Field B2 may be considered constant over the cross-sectional area of the inner smaller coil.
Hence,
{tex}\phi = B.A{/tex}
{tex}{\phi _1} = \pi {r^2}{B_2} = \frac{{{\mu _0}\pi {r^2}{I_2}}}{{2R}} = M{I_2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore M = \frac{{{\phi _1}}}{{{I_2}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}\pi {r^2}}}{{2R}}{/tex}

question_html: Define mutual inductance. Give its SI unit.
question_text: Define mutual inductance. Give its SI unit.
explanation: When two coils (or solenoids) are mutually coupled, then change in current of one coil causes a change in magnetic flux in the other coil and as a result an emf is induced in the other coil. This process is called mutual induction. SI unit of mutual inductance = Henry (H)
solution: When two coils (or solenoids) are mutually coupled, then change in current of one coil causes a change in magnetic flux in the other coil and as a result an emf is induced in the other coil. This process is called mutual induction. SI unit of mutual inductance = Henry (H)
solution_html:
When two coils (or solenoids) are mutually coupled, then change in current of one coil causes a change in magnetic flux in the other coil and as a result an emf is induced in the other coil. This process is called mutual induction. 
SI unit of mutual inductance = Henry (H)

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Explain the meaning of the term mutual inductance. Consider two concentric circular coils, one of the radius r1 and the other of radius r2 (r1 < r2) placed coaxially with centres coinciding with each other. Obtain the expression for the mutual inductance of the arrangement.
  2. A rectangular coil of area A, having number of turns N is rotated at f revolutions per second in a uniform magnetic field B, the field being perpendicular to the coil. Prove that the maximum emf induced in the coil is {tex}2\pi fNBA{/tex}.
question_text: Explain the meaning of the term mutual inductance. Consider two concentric circular coils, one of the radius r 1 and the other of radius r 2 (r 1 < r 2 ) placed coaxially with centres coinciding with each other. Obtain the expression for the mutual inductance of the arrangement. A rectangular coil of area A, having number of turns N is rotated at f revolutions per second in a uniform magnetic field B, the field being perpendicular to the coil. Prove that the maximum emf induced in the coil is {tex}2\pi fNBA{/tex} .
explanation: Whenever the current passing through a coil or circuit changes, the magnetic flux linked with a neighbouring coil or circuit will also change. Hence, an emf will be induced in the neighbouring coil or circuit. This phenomenon is called 'mutual induction'. Now, mutual inductance is the induced magnetic flux linked with one coil when unit current flows in the other neighbouring coil. According to the question, let the current in big coil of radius r 2 be I 1 , so, magnetic field at point at the centre due to this coil will be {tex}\mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } / 2 r _ { 2 }{/tex} . Change in magnetic flux in the coil of radius r 1 is, {tex}\phi = BA = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_1}}}{{2{r_2}}} \times {tex}\pi{/tex} r_1^2{/tex} Now, mutual inductance, {tex}M = \frac{\phi }{{{I_1}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_1}\pi r_1^2}}{{2{r_2} \times {I_1}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}\pi r_1^2}}{{2{r_2}}}{/tex} This is the required expression. According to question, if the coil having N turns rotates with an angular velocity of {tex}\omega {/tex} through an angle {tex}\theta {/tex} in time t, thus {tex}\theta {/tex} = {tex}\omega{/tex} t {tex}\therefore \phi = \vec B. \vec A=BA\cos \theta = BA\cos \omega t{/tex} As the coil rotates, the magnetic flux linked with it changes. An induced emf is set up in the coil, which is given by {tex}e = \frac { - d \phi } { d t } = \frac { - d } { d t } ( B A \cos \omega t ) = B A\omega \operatorname { sin } \omega t{/tex} (From Faraday's 2nd law of electromagnetic induction with Lenz's correction) For N number of turns, e = NBA {tex}\omega {/tex} sin {tex}\omega {/tex} t For maximum value of emf, rotation of the coil must be equal to 90°. So, maximum emf induced is = NBA {tex}\omega {/tex} sin 90 0 = NBA {tex}\omega {/tex} {tex}Thus,\,e = NBA×2\pi f = 2 \pi NBAf{/tex} {tex}[\because \omega = 2\pi f]{/tex}
solution: Whenever the current passing through a coil or circuit changes, the magnetic flux linked with a neighbouring coil or circuit will also change. Hence, an emf will be induced in the neighbouring coil or circuit. This phenomenon is called 'mutual induction'. Now, mutual inductance is the induced magnetic flux linked with one coil when unit current flows in the other neighbouring coil. According to the question, let the current in big coil of radius r 2 be I 1 , so, magnetic field at point at the centre due to this coil will be {tex}\mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } / 2 r _ { 2 }{/tex} . Change in magnetic flux in the coil of radius r 1 is, {tex}\phi = BA = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_1}}}{{2{r_2}}} \times {tex}\pi{/tex} r_1^2{/tex} Now, mutual inductance, {tex}M = \frac{\phi }{{{I_1}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_1}\pi r_1^2}}{{2{r_2} \times {I_1}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}\pi r_1^2}}{{2{r_2}}}{/tex} This is the required expression. According to question, if the coil having N turns rotates with an angular velocity of {tex}\omega {/tex} through an angle {tex}\theta {/tex} in time t, thus {tex}\theta {/tex} = {tex}\omega{/tex} t {tex}\therefore \phi = \vec B. \vec A=BA\cos \theta = BA\cos \omega t{/tex} As the coil rotates, the magnetic flux linked with it changes. An induced emf is set up in the coil, which is given by {tex}e = \frac { - d \phi } { d t } = \frac { - d } { d t } ( B A \cos \omega t ) = B A\omega \operatorname { sin } \omega t{/tex} (From Faraday's 2nd law of electromagnetic induction with Lenz's correction) For N number of turns, e = NBA {tex}\omega {/tex} sin {tex}\omega {/tex} t For maximum value of emf, rotation of the coil must be equal to 90°. So, maximum emf induced is = NBA {tex}\omega {/tex} sin 90 0 = NBA {tex}\omega {/tex} {tex}Thus,\,e = NBA×2\pi f = 2 \pi NBAf{/tex} {tex}[\because \omega = 2\pi f]{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Whenever the current passing through a coil or circuit changes, the magnetic flux linked with a neighbouring coil or circuit will also change. Hence, an emf will be induced in the neighbouring coil or circuit. This phenomenon is called 'mutual induction'.
    Now, mutual inductance is the induced magnetic flux linked with one coil when unit current flows in the other neighbouring coil.
    According to the question, let the current in big coil of radius r2 be I1, so, magnetic field at point at the centre due to this coil will be {tex}\mu _ { 0 } I _ { 1 } / 2 r _ { 2 }{/tex}.
    Change in magnetic flux in the coil of radius r1 is,
    {tex}\phi = BA = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_1}}}{{2{r_2}}} \times {tex}\pi{/tex}r_1^2{/tex}
    Now, mutual inductance, {tex}M = \frac{\phi }{{{I_1}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}{I_1}\pi r_1^2}}{{2{r_2} \times {I_1}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}\pi r_1^2}}{{2{r_2}}}{/tex}
    This is the required expression.
  2. According to question, if the coil having N turns rotates with an angular velocity of {tex}\omega {/tex} through an angle {tex}\theta {/tex}  in time t, thus {tex}\theta {/tex} = {tex}\omega{/tex}t
    {tex}\therefore \phi = \vec B. \vec A=BA\cos \theta = BA\cos \omega t{/tex}
    As the coil rotates, the magnetic flux linked with it changes. An induced emf is set up in the coil, which is given by
    {tex}e = \frac { - d \phi } { d t } = \frac { - d } { d t } ( B A \cos \omega t ) = B A\omega \operatorname { sin } \omega t{/tex} 
    (From Faraday's 2nd law of electromagnetic induction with Lenz's correction)
    For N number of turns, e = NBA{tex}\omega {/tex} sin{tex}\omega {/tex}t
    For maximum value of emf, rotation of the coil must be equal to 90°.
    So, maximum emf induced is =  NBA{tex}\omega {/tex} sin 900 = NBA{tex}\omega {/tex}
    {tex}Thus,\,e = NBA×2\pi f = 2 \pi NBAf{/tex} {tex}[\because \omega = 2\pi f]{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: A pair of adjacent coils has a mutual inductance of 1.5 H. If the current in one coil changes from 0 to 20 A in 0.5 s, what is the change of flux linkage with the other coil?
question_text: A pair of adjacent coils has a mutual inductance of 1.5 H. If the current in one coil changes from 0 to 20 A in 0.5 s, what is the change of flux linkage with the other coil?
explanation: Mutual inductance of a pair of could, {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.5 H Initial current I 1 = 0A Final current I 2 = 20A Change in current in the circuit is given by, dI = I 2 - I 1 = 20 - 0 = 20 A Time taken for the change, t = 0.5 S Induced emf, {tex}e=\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} ..... (i) Where {tex}d \phi{/tex} is the change in the flux linkage with the coil. Emf is related with mutual inductance as: {tex}e=\mu \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} ..... (ii) Equating equations (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}=\mu \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} {tex}d \phi=1.5 \times(20){/tex} = 30 Wb Hence, the change in the flux linkage is 30 Wb.
solution: Mutual inductance of a pair of could, {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.5 H Initial current I 1 = 0A Final current I 2 = 20A Change in current in the circuit is given by, dI = I 2 - I 1 = 20 - 0 = 20 A Time taken for the change, t = 0.5 S Induced emf, {tex}e=\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} ..... (i) Where {tex}d \phi{/tex} is the change in the flux linkage with the coil. Emf is related with mutual inductance as: {tex}e=\mu \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} ..... (ii) Equating equations (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}=\mu \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} {tex}d \phi=1.5 \times(20){/tex} = 30 Wb Hence, the change in the flux linkage is 30 Wb.
solution_html:
Mutual inductance of a pair of could, {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.5 H
Initial current I1 = 0A
Final current I2 = 20A
Change in current in the circuit is given by, dI = I2 - I1 = 20 - 0 = 20 A
Time taken for the change, t = 0.5 S
Induced emf, {tex}e=\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} ..... (i)
Where {tex}d \phi{/tex} is the change in the flux linkage with the coil.
Emf is related with mutual inductance as:
{tex}e=\mu \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} ..... (ii)
Equating equations (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}=\mu \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}d \phi=1.5 \times(20){/tex}
= 30 Wb
Hence, the change in the flux linkage is 30 Wb.

question_html:
1. Define self-inductance of a coil and hence write the definition of Henry.
  2. Write any two factors each on which the following depends:
    1. Self-inductance of a coil.
    2. Mutual inductance of a pair of coils.
question_text: Define self-inductance of a coil and hence write the definition of Henry. Write any two factors each on which the following depends: Self-inductance of a coil. Mutual inductance of a pair of coils.
explanation: The self-inductance, L, of a coil equals the magnetic flux linked with it when a unit current flows through it. OR, It is the property of the current-carrying coil that resist the change of current flowing through it. One henry is the self-inductance of a coil that the magnetic flux, linked with it, due to a current of 1 A, flowing in it, equals one weber. Self-inductance of a coil depends on Its geometry (area and length of a coil) Nature of medium in the intervening space. Mutual inductance of a given pair of coils depends on Their distance of separation Nature of medium in the intervening space.
solution: The self-inductance, L, of a coil equals the magnetic flux linked with it when a unit current flows through it. OR, It is the property of the current-carrying coil that resist the change of current flowing through it. One henry is the self-inductance of a coil that the magnetic flux, linked with it, due to a current of 1 A, flowing in it, equals one weber. Self-inductance of a coil depends on Its geometry (area and length of a coil) Nature of medium in the intervening space. Mutual inductance of a given pair of coils depends on Their distance of separation Nature of medium in the intervening space.
solution_html:
1. The self-inductance, L, of a coil equals the magnetic flux linked with it when a unit current flows through it. OR, It is the property of the current-carrying coil that resist the change of current flowing through it. One henry is the self-inductance of a coil that the magnetic flux, linked with it, due to a current of 1 A, flowing in it, equals one weber.
  2. Self-inductance of a coil depends on
    1. Its geometry (area and length of a coil)
    2. Nature of medium in the intervening space.
  3. Mutual inductance of a given pair of coils depends on
    1. Their distance of separation
    2. Nature of medium in the intervening space.

question_html: A long solenoid with 15 turns per cm has a small loop of area 2.0 cm2 placed inside the solenoid normal to its axis. If the current carried by the solenoid changes steadily from 2.0 A to 4.0 A in 0.1 s, what is the induced emf in the loop while the current is changing?
question_text: A long solenoid with 15 turns per cm has a small loop of area 2.0 cm 2 placed inside the solenoid normal to its axis. If the current carried by the solenoid changes steadily from 2.0 A to 4.0 A in 0.1 s, what is the induced emf in the loop while the current is changing?
explanation: Given, In a solenoid, Number of turns per unit length, n = 15 turns/cm = 1500 turns/m Area of the small loop, A = 2 cm 2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −4 m 2 Initial current, I 1 = 2 A Final current, I 2 = 4 A {tex}\Delta {/tex} t = 0.1 s The magnetic field associated with current I 1 . {tex}\mathrm{B}_{1}=\mu_{0} \mathrm{n} \mathrm{I}_{1}{/tex} The magnetic field associated with current I 2 . {tex}\mathrm{B}_{1}=\mu_{0} \mathrm{n} \mathrm{I}_{2}{/tex} The change in flux associated with the change in current in solenoid, {tex}\Delta \phi=\left(B_{2}-B_{1}\right) A{/tex} = 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1500 {tex}\times{/tex} (4 - 2) {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −4 = 7.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −7 weber The induced EMF while current is changing, {tex}|\mathrm{E}|=\frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{7.6 \times 10^{-7}}{0.1}{/tex} = 7.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −6 V
solution: Given, In a solenoid, Number of turns per unit length, n = 15 turns/cm = 1500 turns/m Area of the small loop, A = 2 cm 2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −4 m 2 Initial current, I 1 = 2 A Final current, I 2 = 4 A {tex}\Delta {/tex} t = 0.1 s The magnetic field associated with current I 1 . {tex}\mathrm{B}_{1}=\mu_{0} \mathrm{n} \mathrm{I}_{1}{/tex} The magnetic field associated with current I 2 . {tex}\mathrm{B}_{1}=\mu_{0} \mathrm{n} \mathrm{I}_{2}{/tex} The change in flux associated with the change in current in solenoid, {tex}\Delta \phi=\left(B_{2}-B_{1}\right) A{/tex} = 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1500 {tex}\times{/tex} (4 - 2) {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −4 = 7.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −7 weber The induced EMF while current is changing, {tex}|\mathrm{E}|=\frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{7.6 \times 10^{-7}}{0.1}{/tex} = 7.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −6 V
solution_html:
Given, In a solenoid,
Number of turns per unit length, n = 15 turns/cm = 1500 turns/m
Area of the small loop, A = 2 cm2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−4 m2 
Initial current, I1 = 2 A Final current, I2 = 4 A
{tex}\Delta {/tex}t = 0.1 s
The magnetic field associated with current I1.
{tex}\mathrm{B}_{1}=\mu_{0} \mathrm{n} \mathrm{I}_{1}{/tex}
The magnetic field associated with current I2.
{tex}\mathrm{B}_{1}=\mu_{0} \mathrm{n} \mathrm{I}_{2}{/tex}
The change in flux associated with the change in current in solenoid,
{tex}\Delta \phi=\left(B_{2}-B_{1}\right) A{/tex}
= 4{tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10−7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1500 {tex}\times{/tex} (4 - 2) {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−4
= 7.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−7weber 
The induced EMF while current is changing,
{tex}|\mathrm{E}|=\frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{7.6 \times 10^{-7}}{0.1}{/tex}
= 7.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−6 V

question_html:
A square loop of side 20 cm is initially kept 30 cm away from a region of uniform magnetic field of 0.1 T as shown in the figure. It is then moved towards the right with a velocity of 10 cm s-1 till it goes out of the field. Plot a graph showing the variation of

  1. magnetic flux ({tex}\phi {/tex}) through the loop with time t.
  2. induced emf ({tex}\varepsilon {/tex}) in the loop with time t.
  3. induced current in the loop, if it has resistance of 0.1{tex}\Omega {/tex}
question_text: A square loop of side 20 cm is initially kept 30 cm away from a region of uniform magnetic field of 0.1 T as shown in the figure. It is then moved towards the right with a velocity of 10 cm s -1 till it goes out of the field. Plot a graph showing the variation of magnetic flux ( {tex}\phi {/tex} ) through the loop with time t. induced emf ( {tex}\varepsilon {/tex} ) in the loop with time t. induced current in the loop, if it has resistance of 0.1 {tex}\Omega {/tex}
explanation: Given, I = 20cm = 0.2m, {tex}B = 0.1 T , v = 10 \mathrm { cms } ^ { - 1 } = 0.1 \mathrm { ms } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} Magnetic flux through loop, {tex}\phi _ { B } =\vec B \cdot \vec A = BA \text {cos} 0^0 = B l x{/tex} (with A = lx) {tex}{\phi _{\max }} = 0.1 \times 0.2 \times 0.2 = 0.004 = 4 \times {10^{ - 3}}{\text{Wb}}{/tex} (for maximum flux x = l = 0.2m) Induced emf, {tex}\varepsilon = \frac { - d \phi } { d l } = - B l v{/tex} {tex}| \varepsilon | _ { m a x } = 0.1 \times 0.2 \times 0.1 = 0.002 = 2 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { V }{/tex} Induced current, {tex}I = \frac { | \varepsilon | } { R } = \frac { 2 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } { 0.1 } = 2 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } \mathrm { A }{/tex}
solution: Given, I = 20cm = 0.2m, {tex}B = 0.1 T , v = 10 \mathrm { cms } ^ { - 1 } = 0.1 \mathrm { ms } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} Magnetic flux through loop, {tex}\phi _ { B } =\vec B \cdot \vec A = BA \text {cos} 0^0 = B l x{/tex} (with A = lx) {tex}{\phi _{\max }} = 0.1 \times 0.2 \times 0.2 = 0.004 = 4 \times {10^{ - 3}}{\text{Wb}}{/tex} (for maximum flux x = l = 0.2m) Induced emf, {tex}\varepsilon = \frac { - d \phi } { d l } = - B l v{/tex} {tex}| \varepsilon | _ { m a x } = 0.1 \times 0.2 \times 0.1 = 0.002 = 2 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { V }{/tex} Induced current, {tex}I = \frac { | \varepsilon | } { R } = \frac { 2 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } { 0.1 } = 2 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } \mathrm { A }{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, I = 20cm = 0.2m,
{tex}B = 0.1 T , v = 10 \mathrm { cms } ^ { - 1 } = 0.1 \mathrm { ms } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}

  1. Magnetic flux through loop, {tex}\phi _ { B } =\vec B \cdot \vec A = BA \text {cos} 0^0 = B l x{/tex} (with A = lx)
    {tex}{\phi _{\max }} = 0.1 \times 0.2 \times 0.2 = 0.004 = 4 \times {10^{ - 3}}{\text{Wb}}{/tex} (for maximum flux x = l = 0.2m)
  2. Induced emf, {tex}\varepsilon = \frac { - d \phi } { d l } = - B l v{/tex}
    {tex}| \varepsilon | _ { m a x } = 0.1 \times 0.2 \times 0.1 = 0.002 = 2 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { V }{/tex}
  3. Induced current,
    {tex}I = \frac { | \varepsilon | } { R } = \frac { 2 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } { 0.1 } = 2 \times 10 ^ { - 2 } \mathrm { A }{/tex}

question_html: The arm PQ of the rectangular conductor is moved from x = 0, outwards. The uniform magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane and extends from x = 0 to x = b and is zero for x > b. Only the arm PQ possesses substantial resistance r. Consider the situation when the arm PQ is pulled outwards from x = 0 to x = 2b, and is then moved back to x = 0 with constant speed v. Obtain expressions for the flux, the induced emf, the force necessary to pull the arm and the power dissipated as Joule heat. Sketch the variation of these quantities with distance.
question_text: The arm PQ of the rectangular conductor is moved from x = 0, outwards. The uniform magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane and extends from x = 0 to x = b and is zero for x > b. Only the arm PQ possesses substantial resistance r. Consider the situation when the arm PQ is pulled outwards from x = 0 to x = 2b, and is then moved back to x = 0 with constant speed v. Obtain expressions for the flux, the induced emf, the force necessary to pull the arm and the power dissipated as Joule heat. Sketch the variation of these quantities with distance.
explanation: The uniform magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane and extends from x = 0 to x = b and is zero for x>b. Only the arm PQ possesses substantial resistance ‘r’ and the arm PQ is pulled outwards from x = 0 to x = 2b, and is then moved back to x = 0 with constant speed ‘ѵ’. Let us first consider the forward motion from x = 0 to x = 2b The flux {tex}\phi_{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} linked with the circuit SPQR is {tex}\phi_{\mathrm{B}}=B l x \quad 0 \leq x<b{/tex} = Blb {tex}b \leq x<2 b{/tex} The induced emf is, {tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \Phi_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{d} t}{/tex} = -Blv, {tex}0 \leq x<b{/tex} = 0, {tex}b \leq x<2 b{/tex} When the induced emf is non-zero, the current I is (in magnitude) {tex}I=\frac{B l v}{r}{/tex} The force required to keep the arm PQ in constant motion is {tex}F=IlB{/tex} its direction is to the left. In magnitude {tex}F=\frac{B^{2} L^{2} v}{r},0 \leq x<b{/tex} = 0, {tex}b \leq x<2 b{/tex} The Joule heating loss is P J = I 2 r {tex}=\frac{B^{2} l^{2} v^{2}}{r},0 \leq x<b{/tex} = 0, {tex}b \leq x<2 b{/tex} One obtains similar expressions for the inward motion from x = 2b to x = 0. One can appreciate the whole process by examining the sketch of various quantities displayed in Figure.
solution: The uniform magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane and extends from x = 0 to x = b and is zero for x>b. Only the arm PQ possesses substantial resistance ‘r’ and the arm PQ is pulled outwards from x = 0 to x = 2b, and is then moved back to x = 0 with constant speed ‘ѵ’. Let us first consider the forward motion from x = 0 to x = 2b The flux {tex}\phi_{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} linked with the circuit SPQR is {tex}\phi_{\mathrm{B}}=B l x \quad 0 \leq x<b{/tex} = Blb {tex}b \leq x<2 b{/tex} The induced emf is, {tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \Phi_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{d} t}{/tex} = -Blv, {tex}0 \leq x<b{/tex} = 0, {tex}b \leq x<2 b{/tex} When the induced emf is non-zero, the current I is (in magnitude) {tex}I=\frac{B l v}{r}{/tex} The force required to keep the arm PQ in constant motion is {tex}F=IlB{/tex} its direction is to the left. In magnitude {tex}F=\frac{B^{2} L^{2} v}{r},0 \leq x<b{/tex} = 0, {tex}b \leq x<2 b{/tex} The Joule heating loss is P J = I 2 r {tex}=\frac{B^{2} l^{2} v^{2}}{r},0 \leq x<b{/tex} = 0, {tex}b \leq x<2 b{/tex} One obtains similar expressions for the inward motion from x = 2b to x = 0. One can appreciate the whole process by examining the sketch of various quantities displayed in Figure.
solution_html:
The uniform magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane and extends from x = 0 to x = b and is zero for x>b. Only the arm PQ possesses substantial resistance ‘r’ and the arm PQ is pulled outwards from x = 0 to x = 2b, and is then moved back to x = 0 with constant speed ‘ѵ’. Let us first consider the forward motion from x = 0 to x = 2b
The flux {tex}\phi_{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} linked with the circuit SPQR is
{tex}\phi_{\mathrm{B}}=B l x \quad 0 \leq x<b{/tex}
= Blb {tex}b \leq x<2 b{/tex}
The induced emf is,
{tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \Phi_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{d} t}{/tex}
= -Blv, {tex}0 \leq x<b{/tex}
= 0, {tex}b \leq x<2 b{/tex}
When the induced emf is non-zero, the current I is (in magnitude)
{tex}I=\frac{B l v}{r}{/tex}

The force required to keep the arm PQ in constant motion is {tex}F=IlB{/tex} its direction is to the left. In magnitude
{tex}F=\frac{B^{2} L^{2} v}{r},0 \leq x<b{/tex}
= 0, {tex}b \leq x<2 b{/tex}
The Joule heating loss is
PJ = I2r
{tex}=\frac{B^{2} l^{2} v^{2}}{r},0 \leq x<b{/tex}
= 0, {tex}b \leq x<2 b{/tex}
One obtains similar expressions for the inward motion from x = 2b to x = 0. One can appreciate the whole process by examining the sketch of various quantities displayed in Figure.
