question_html:
1. When a bar magnet is pushed towards (or away) from the coil connected to a galvanometer, the pointer in the galvanometer deflects. Identify the phenomenon causing this deflection and write the factors on which the amount and direction of the deflection depends. State the laws describing this phenomenon.
  2. Sketch the change in flux, emf and force when a conducting rod PQ of resistance R and length l moves freely to and fro between A and C with speed v on a rectangular conductor placed in uniform magnetic field as shown in the figure.
question_text: When a bar magnet is pushed towards (or away) from the coil connected to a galvanometer, the pointer in the galvanometer deflects. Identify the phenomenon causing this deflection and write the factors on which the amount and direction of the deflection depends. State the laws describing this phenomenon. Sketch the change in flux, emf and force when a conducting rod PQ of resistance R and length l moves freely to and fro between A and C with speed v on a rectangular conductor placed in uniform magnetic field as shown in the figure.
explanation: The deflection is caused by a phenomenon called electromagnetic induction. The amount of deflection in the galvanometer depends upon the speed with which the magnet is moved. The direction of deflection in the galvanometer depends on the direction (towards/away) of the movement of the magnet. The laws of electromagnetic induction are: Whenever the magnetic flux linked with a closed circuit changes, an induced emf is produced in it. The magnitude of the induced emf is equal to the rate of change of magnetic flux linked with the closed circuit. {tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} The flux enclosed by the rod is {tex}\Phi=B l x{/tex} for {tex}0 \leq x \leq b{/tex} = Blx for {tex}b \leq x \leq 2 b{/tex} Now, the magnitude of induced emf acording to faraday's law is {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{d \phi}{d t}=-B l \frac{d x}{d t}=-B l v{/tex} for {tex}0 \leq x \leq b{/tex} {tex}=-B l \frac{d b}{d t}=0{/tex} for {tex}b \leq x \leq 2 b{/tex} Now, the magnitude of induced current, when induced emf is non-zero is {tex}\varepsilon=I R{/tex} {tex}\therefore I=\frac{\varepsilon}{r}=\frac{B l v}{r}{/tex} The force required to keep the conductor in motion is F = Bll {tex}\therefore F=B \frac{B l v}{R} l=\frac{B^{2} l^{2} v}{R}{/tex} {tex}\therefore F=\frac{B^{2} l^{2} v}{R}{/tex} for {tex}b \leq x<b{/tex} = 0 for {tex}b \leq x \leq 2 b{/tex} Therefore, the variation of flux, emf, and force is as shown in the following diagram
solution: The deflection is caused by a phenomenon called electromagnetic induction. The amount of deflection in the galvanometer depends upon the speed with which the magnet is moved. The direction of deflection in the galvanometer depends on the direction (towards/away) of the movement of the magnet. The laws of electromagnetic induction are: Whenever the magnetic flux linked with a closed circuit changes, an induced emf is produced in it. The magnitude of the induced emf is equal to the rate of change of magnetic flux linked with the closed circuit. {tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} The flux enclosed by the rod is {tex}\Phi=B l x{/tex} for {tex}0 \leq x \leq b{/tex} = Blx for {tex}b \leq x \leq 2 b{/tex} Now, the magnitude of induced emf acording to faraday's law is {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{d \phi}{d t}=-B l \frac{d x}{d t}=-B l v{/tex} for {tex}0 \leq x \leq b{/tex} {tex}=-B l \frac{d b}{d t}=0{/tex} for {tex}b \leq x \leq 2 b{/tex} Now, the magnitude of induced current, when induced emf is non-zero is {tex}\varepsilon=I R{/tex} {tex}\therefore I=\frac{\varepsilon}{r}=\frac{B l v}{r}{/tex} The force required to keep the conductor in motion is F = Bll {tex}\therefore F=B \frac{B l v}{R} l=\frac{B^{2} l^{2} v}{R}{/tex} {tex}\therefore F=\frac{B^{2} l^{2} v}{R}{/tex} for {tex}b \leq x<b{/tex} = 0 for {tex}b \leq x \leq 2 b{/tex} Therefore, the variation of flux, emf, and force is as shown in the following diagram
solution_html:
1. The deflection is caused by a phenomenon called electromagnetic induction. The amount of deflection in the galvanometer depends upon the speed with which the magnet is moved. The direction of deflection in the galvanometer depends on the direction (towards/away) of the movement of the magnet. The laws of electromagnetic induction are:
    1. Whenever the magnetic flux linked with a closed circuit changes, an induced emf is produced in it.
    2. The magnitude of the induced emf is equal to the rate of change of magnetic flux linked with the closed circuit.
      {tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex}
  2. The flux enclosed by the rod is
    {tex}\Phi=B l x{/tex} for {tex}0 \leq x \leq b{/tex}
    = Blx for {tex}b \leq x \leq 2 b{/tex}
    Now, the magnitude of induced emf acording to faraday's law is
    {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{d \phi}{d t}=-B l \frac{d x}{d t}=-B l v{/tex} for {tex}0 \leq x \leq b{/tex}
    {tex}=-B l \frac{d b}{d t}=0{/tex} for {tex}b \leq x \leq 2 b{/tex}
    Now, the magnitude of induced current, when induced emf is non-zero is {tex}\varepsilon=I R{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore I=\frac{\varepsilon}{r}=\frac{B l v}{r}{/tex}
    The force required to keep the conductor in motion is F = Bll
    {tex}\therefore F=B \frac{B l v}{R} l=\frac{B^{2} l^{2} v}{R}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore F=\frac{B^{2} l^{2} v}{R}{/tex} for {tex}b \leq x<b{/tex}
    = 0 for {tex}b \leq x \leq 2 b{/tex}
    Therefore, the variation of flux, emf, and force is as shown in the following diagram

question_html: A jet plane is traveling towards the west at the speed of 1800 km/h. What is the voltage difference developed between the ends of the wing having a span of 25 m, if the Earth's magnetic field at the location has a magnitude of {tex}5 \times {10^{ - 4}}T{/tex} and the dip angle is 30°.
question_text: A jet plane is traveling towards the west at the speed of 1800 km/h. What is the voltage difference developed between the ends of the wing having a span of 25 m, if the Earth's magnetic field at the location has a magnitude of {tex}5 \times {10^{ - 4}}T{/tex} and the dip angle is 30°.
explanation: Here, v = 1800 km h -1 {tex} = \frac{{1800 \times 1000}}{{60 \times 60}} = 500\;m/s{/tex} Earth's field, {tex}B = 5.0 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex} Angle of dip, {tex}\delta = 30^\circ {/tex} Length of wing = 25 m The vertical component (B v ) of earth's field is normal to both wings and the direction of motion. {tex}\therefore {B_v} = B\sin \delta {/tex} {tex} = 5.0 \times {10^{ - 4}}\sin 30^\circ {/tex} {tex}{B_v} = 5 \times {10^{ - 4}} \times \frac{1}{2} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}T{/tex} Induced emf produced, {tex}e = {B_v}lv = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}} \times 500 \times 25{/tex} = 3.125 V
solution: Here, v = 1800 km h -1 {tex} = \frac{{1800 \times 1000}}{{60 \times 60}} = 500\;m/s{/tex} Earth's field, {tex}B = 5.0 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex} Angle of dip, {tex}\delta = 30^\circ {/tex} Length of wing = 25 m The vertical component (B v ) of earth's field is normal to both wings and the direction of motion. {tex}\therefore {B_v} = B\sin \delta {/tex} {tex} = 5.0 \times {10^{ - 4}}\sin 30^\circ {/tex} {tex}{B_v} = 5 \times {10^{ - 4}} \times \frac{1}{2} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}T{/tex} Induced emf produced, {tex}e = {B_v}lv = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}} \times 500 \times 25{/tex} = 3.125 V
solution_html:
Here, v = 1800 km h-1 {tex} = \frac{{1800 \times 1000}}{{60 \times 60}} = 500\;m/s{/tex}
Earth's field, {tex}B = 5.0 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex}
Angle of dip, {tex}\delta = 30^\circ {/tex}
Length of wing = 25 m
The vertical component (Bv) of earth's field is normal to both wings and the direction of motion.
{tex}\therefore {B_v} = B\sin \delta {/tex}
{tex} = 5.0 \times {10^{ - 4}}\sin 30^\circ {/tex}
{tex}{B_v} = 5 \times {10^{ - 4}} \times \frac{1}{2} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}T{/tex}
Induced emf produced,
{tex}e = {B_v}lv = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}} \times 500 \times 25{/tex} = 3.125 V

question_html: In the given figure, a bar magnet is quickly moved towards a conducting loop having a capacitor. Predict the polarity of the plates A and B of the capacitor.
question_text: In the given figure, a bar magnet is quickly moved towards a conducting loop having a capacitor. Predict the polarity of the plates A and B of the capacitor.
explanation: According to the Lenz's Law, the induced current will oppose the change. The flow of induced current is anti-clockwise so that it can oppose the north pole approaching. Hence A acquires positive polarity and B acquires negative polarity.
solution: According to the Lenz's Law, the induced current will oppose the change. The flow of induced current is anti-clockwise so that it can oppose the north pole approaching. Hence A acquires positive polarity and B acquires negative polarity.
solution_html:
According to the Lenz's Law, the induced current will oppose the change.
The flow of induced current is anti-clockwise so that it can oppose the north pole approaching. Hence A acquires positive polarity and B acquires negative polarity.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State the law which relates to generation of induced emf in a conductor being moved in a magnetic field.
  2. A rod of length l is moved horizontally with a uniform velocity ‘v’ in a direction perpendicular to its length through a region in which a uniform magnetic field is acting vertically downward. Derive the expression for the emf induced across the ends of the rod.
  3. How does one understand this motional emf by invoking the Lorentz force acting on the free charge carriers of the conductors? Explain.
question_text: State the law which relates to generation of induced emf in a conductor being moved in a magnetic field. A rod of length l is moved horizontally with a uniform velocity ‘v’ in a direction perpendicular to its length through a region in which a uniform magnetic field is acting vertically downward. Derive the expression for the emf induced across the ends of the rod. How does one understand this motional emf by invoking the Lorentz force acting on the free charge carriers of the conductors? Explain.
explanation: According to Faraday's flux rule, the magnitude of induced emf is equal to the rate of change of magnetic flux linked with the closed circuit. {tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} By faraday's laws of electromagnetic induction, {tex}\mathbf{V}=\frac{\mathbf{d} \phi}{\mathbf{d t}}{/tex} {tex}\mathbf{V}=\mathbf{B} \frac{\mathbf{d} \mathbf{A}}{\mathbf{d t}}{/tex} V = Blv as {tex}\frac {dA}{dt}{/tex} = lv This is the induced emf across the ends of the rod. We have the force on the free electrons from Q to P as {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathbf{F}}_{\mathrm{b}}=\overrightarrow{\mathbf{q} \mathbf{v}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathbf{B}}{/tex} . where v is the velocity of the rod as well as of the free electrons inside it, B is the uniform magnetic field. The free electrons will move towards P and the positive charge will appear at Q. An electrostatic field is developed from Q to P in the wire which exerts a force {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathbf{F}}_{\mathrm{e}}=\mathbf{q} \overrightarrow{\mathbf{E}}{/tex} on each electrons. The charge keeps on gathering until. {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathbf{F}}_{\mathrm{b}}=\overrightarrow{\mathbf{F}}_{\mathrm{e}}{/tex} and the resultant force on each electron is zero. {tex}|\mathbf{q} \overrightarrow{\mathbf{v}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathbf{B}}|=|\mathbf{q} \overrightarrow{\mathbf{E}}|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} vB = E The potential difference between Q and P is then V = E = vBl which is maintained by the magnetic force of the moving electron producing an emf, e = Bvl
solution: According to Faraday's flux rule, the magnitude of induced emf is equal to the rate of change of magnetic flux linked with the closed circuit. {tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} By faraday's laws of electromagnetic induction, {tex}\mathbf{V}=\frac{\mathbf{d} \phi}{\mathbf{d t}}{/tex} {tex}\mathbf{V}=\mathbf{B} \frac{\mathbf{d} \mathbf{A}}{\mathbf{d t}}{/tex} V = Blv as {tex}\frac {dA}{dt}{/tex} = lv This is the induced emf across the ends of the rod. We have the force on the free electrons from Q to P as {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathbf{F}}_{\mathrm{b}}=\overrightarrow{\mathbf{q} \mathbf{v}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathbf{B}}{/tex} . where v is the velocity of the rod as well as of the free electrons inside it, B is the uniform magnetic field. The free electrons will move towards P and the positive charge will appear at Q. An electrostatic field is developed from Q to P in the wire which exerts a force {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathbf{F}}_{\mathrm{e}}=\mathbf{q} \overrightarrow{\mathbf{E}}{/tex} on each electrons. The charge keeps on gathering until. {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathbf{F}}_{\mathrm{b}}=\overrightarrow{\mathbf{F}}_{\mathrm{e}}{/tex} and the resultant force on each electron is zero. {tex}|\mathbf{q} \overrightarrow{\mathbf{v}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathbf{B}}|=|\mathbf{q} \overrightarrow{\mathbf{E}}|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} vB = E The potential difference between Q and P is then V = E = vBl which is maintained by the magnetic force of the moving electron producing an emf, e = Bvl
solution_html:
1. According to Faraday's flux rule, the magnitude of induced emf is equal to the rate of change of magnetic flux linked with the closed circuit.
    {tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex}
  2. By faraday's laws of electromagnetic induction,
    {tex}\mathbf{V}=\frac{\mathbf{d} \phi}{\mathbf{d t}}{/tex}
    {tex}\mathbf{V}=\mathbf{B} \frac{\mathbf{d} \mathbf{A}}{\mathbf{d t}}{/tex}
    V = Blv as {tex}\frac {dA}{dt}{/tex} = lv
    This is the induced emf across the ends of the rod.
  3. We have the force on the free electrons from Q to P as {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathbf{F}}_{\mathrm{b}}=\overrightarrow{\mathbf{q} \mathbf{v}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathbf{B}}{/tex}.
    where v is the velocity of the rod as well as of the free electrons inside it, B is the uniform magnetic field. The free electrons will move towards P and the positive charge will appear at Q. An electrostatic field is developed from Q to P in the wire which exerts a force {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathbf{F}}_{\mathrm{e}}=\mathbf{q} \overrightarrow{\mathbf{E}}{/tex} on each electrons. The charge keeps on gathering until.
    {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathbf{F}}_{\mathrm{b}}=\overrightarrow{\mathbf{F}}_{\mathrm{e}}{/tex} and the resultant force on each electron is zero.
    {tex}|\mathbf{q} \overrightarrow{\mathbf{v}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathbf{B}}|=|\mathbf{q} \overrightarrow{\mathbf{E}}|{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} vB = E
    The potential difference between Q and P is then
    V = E = vBl
    which is maintained by the magnetic force of the moving electron producing an emf, e = Bvl

question_html:
Draw a schematic sketch of an AC generator describing its basic elements. State briefly its working principle. Show a plot of variation of

  1. magnetic flux and
  2. alternating emf versus time generated by a loop of wire rotating in a magnetic field.
question_text: Draw a schematic sketch of an AC generator describing its basic elements. State briefly its working principle. Show a plot of variation of magnetic flux and alternating emf versus time generated by a loop of wire rotating in a magnetic field.
explanation: Principle: A dynamo or generator is a device which converts mechanical energy into electrical energy. It is based on the principle of electromagnetic induction. Working: When the armature coil is rotated in the strong magnetic field, the magnetic flux linked the coil changes and the current is induced in the coil. The direction of current is given by Flemming's left hand rule. It remains same during the first half turn of the armature. During the second half revolution, the direction of current is reversed. If N is the number in the coil, f is the frequency of rotation. A is the area of the coil and B is the magnetic field idensity then induced emf, {tex}\mathrm{e}=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}(\mathrm{NBA} \cos 2 \pi \mathrm{ft})=2 \pi \mathrm{NBAf} \sin 2 \pi \mathrm{ft}{/tex} Therefore, the emf produced is alternating in nature and the current is also alternating. Current produced in a.c. generator can not be measured by moving coil galvanometer, because average value of a.c. over full cycle is zero. Graph between magnetic flux and time, is shown below: The graph between alternating emf versus time, is shown below:
solution: Principle: A dynamo or generator is a device which converts mechanical energy into electrical energy. It is based on the principle of electromagnetic induction. Working: When the armature coil is rotated in the strong magnetic field, the magnetic flux linked the coil changes and the current is induced in the coil. The direction of current is given by Flemming's left hand rule. It remains same during the first half turn of the armature. During the second half revolution, the direction of current is reversed. If N is the number in the coil, f is the frequency of rotation. A is the area of the coil and B is the magnetic field idensity then induced emf, {tex}\mathrm{e}=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}(\mathrm{NBA} \cos 2 \pi \mathrm{ft})=2 \pi \mathrm{NBAf} \sin 2 \pi \mathrm{ft}{/tex} Therefore, the emf produced is alternating in nature and the current is also alternating. Current produced in a.c. generator can not be measured by moving coil galvanometer, because average value of a.c. over full cycle is zero. Graph between magnetic flux and time, is shown below: The graph between alternating emf versus time, is shown below:
solution_html:
Principle: A dynamo or generator is a device which converts mechanical energy into electrical energy. It is based on the principle of electromagnetic induction.

Working: When the armature coil is rotated in the strong magnetic field, the magnetic flux linked the coil changes and the current is induced in the coil. The direction of current is given by Flemming's left hand rule. It remains same during the first half turn of the armature. During the second half revolution, the direction of current is reversed.
If N is the number in the coil, f is the frequency of rotation. A is the area of the coil and B is the magnetic field idensity then induced emf,
{tex}\mathrm{e}=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}(\mathrm{NBA} \cos 2 \pi \mathrm{ft})=2 \pi \mathrm{NBAf} \sin 2 \pi \mathrm{ft}{/tex}
Therefore, the emf produced is alternating in nature and the current is also alternating.
Current produced in a.c. generator can not be measured by moving coil galvanometer, because average value of a.c. over full cycle is zero.

  2. The graph between alternating emf versus time, is shown below:

question_html:
1. Define self-inductance. Write its SI unit.
  2. A long solenoid with 15 turns per cm has a small loop of area 2.0 cm2 placed inside the solenoid normal to its axis. If the current carried by the solenoid changes steadily from 2.0 A to 4.0 A in 0.1 s, then what is the induced emf in the loop while the current is changing?
question_text: Define self-inductance. Write its SI unit. A long solenoid with 15 turns per cm has a small loop of area 2.0 cm 2 placed inside the solenoid normal to its axis. If the current carried by the solenoid changes steadily from 2.0 A to 4.0 A in 0.1 s, then what is the induced emf in the loop while the current is changing?
explanation: When the current in a coil is changed, a back emf is induced in the same coil. This phenomenon is called self-induction. And self-inductance is the magnetic flux linked with the coil when a unit current flows through it. If L is self-inductance of coil, then net magnetic flux, {tex}N \phi \propto I \Rightarrow N \phi = L I{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad L = \frac{{N\phi }}{I}{/tex} The SI unit of self-inductance is Henry (H). Mutual inductance of solenoid coil system, {tex}M = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } A } { l }{/tex} Here, {tex}N _ { 1 } = 15 , N _ { 2 } = 1 , l = 1 \mathrm { cm } = 10 ^ { - 2 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} {tex}A = 2 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 } = 2 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore M = \frac { 4 \pi \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \times 15 \times 1 \times 2\times 10 ^ { - 4 } } { 10 ^ { - 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= 120 \pi \times 10 ^ { - 9} \mathrm { H }{/tex} Induced emf in the loop, {tex}|e_2| = M \frac { d I } { d t }= 120 \pi \times 10 ^ { - 9 } ×\frac { ( 4 - 2 ) } { 0.1 }{/tex} {tex} = 120 \times 3.14 \times {10^{ - 9}} \times \frac{2}{{0.1}}{/tex} {tex}= 7.5 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { V } = 7.5 \mu \mathrm { V }{/tex}
solution: When the current in a coil is changed, a back emf is induced in the same coil. This phenomenon is called self-induction. And self-inductance is the magnetic flux linked with the coil when a unit current flows through it. If L is self-inductance of coil, then net magnetic flux, {tex}N \phi \propto I \Rightarrow N \phi = L I{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad L = \frac{{N\phi }}{I}{/tex} The SI unit of self-inductance is Henry (H). Mutual inductance of solenoid coil system, {tex}M = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } A } { l }{/tex} Here, {tex}N _ { 1 } = 15 , N _ { 2 } = 1 , l = 1 \mathrm { cm } = 10 ^ { - 2 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} {tex}A = 2 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 } = 2 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore M = \frac { 4 \pi \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \times 15 \times 1 \times 2\times 10 ^ { - 4 } } { 10 ^ { - 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= 120 \pi \times 10 ^ { - 9} \mathrm { H }{/tex} Induced emf in the loop, {tex}|e_2| = M \frac { d I } { d t }= 120 \pi \times 10 ^ { - 9 } ×\frac { ( 4 - 2 ) } { 0.1 }{/tex} {tex} = 120 \times 3.14 \times {10^{ - 9}} \times \frac{2}{{0.1}}{/tex} {tex}= 7.5 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { V } = 7.5 \mu \mathrm { V }{/tex}
solution_html:
1. When the current in a coil is changed, a back emf is induced in the same coil. This phenomenon is called self-induction. And self-inductance is the magnetic flux linked with the coil when a unit current flows through it. If L is self-inductance of coil, then net magnetic flux,
    {tex}N \phi \propto I \Rightarrow N \phi = L I{/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow \quad L = \frac{{N\phi }}{I}{/tex}

    The SI unit of self-inductance is Henry (H). 

  2. Mutual inductance of solenoid coil system, {tex}M = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } A } { l }{/tex}

    Here, {tex}N _ { 1 } = 15 , N _ { 2 } = 1 , l = 1 \mathrm { cm } = 10 ^ { - 2 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
    {tex}A = 2 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 } = 2 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore M = \frac { 4 \pi \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \times 15 \times 1 \times 2\times 10 ^ { - 4 } } { 10 ^ { - 2 } }{/tex}
    {tex}= 120 \pi \times 10 ^ { - 9} \mathrm { H }{/tex}
    Induced emf in the loop,
    {tex}|e_2| = M \frac { d I } { d t }= 120 \pi \times 10 ^ { - 9 } ×\frac { ( 4 - 2 ) } { 0.1 }{/tex}
    {tex} = 120 \times 3.14 \times {10^{ - 9}} \times \frac{2}{{0.1}}{/tex}
    {tex}= 7.5 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { V } = 7.5 \mu \mathrm { V }{/tex}

question_html: Define mutual inductance. Write its SI unit. Give two factors on which the coefficient of mutual inductance between a pair of coils depends.
question_text: Define mutual inductance. Write its SI unit. Give two factors on which the coefficient of mutual inductance between a pair of coils depends.
explanation: Mutual inductance. The mutual inductance of two coils is defined as the induced emf set up in one coil when the current in the neighboring coil changes at the unit rate. SI unit of mutual inductance is henry (H). The mutual inductance of two coils depends on the number of turns and the geometrical shape of the two coils. the relative orientation of the two coils.
solution: Mutual inductance. The mutual inductance of two coils is defined as the induced emf set up in one coil when the current in the neighboring coil changes at the unit rate. SI unit of mutual inductance is henry (H). The mutual inductance of two coils depends on the number of turns and the geometrical shape of the two coils. the relative orientation of the two coils.
solution_html:
Mutual inductance. The mutual inductance of two coils is defined as the induced emf set up in one coil when the current in the neighboring coil changes at the unit rate.
SI unit of mutual inductance is henry (H).
The mutual inductance of two coils depends on

  1. the number of turns and the geometrical shape of the two coils.
  2. the relative orientation of the two coils.

question_html:
A square loop MNOP of side 20 cm is 'placed horizontally in a uniform magnetic field acting vertically downwards as shown in the figure. The loop is pulled with a constant velocity of 20 cm s 1 till it goes out of the field.

  1. Depict the direction of the induced current in the loop as it goes out of the field. For how long would the current in the loop persist?
  2. Plot a graph showing the variation of magnetic flux and induced emf as a function of time.
question_text: A square loop MNOP of side 20 cm is 'placed horizontally in a uniform magnetic field acting vertically downwards as shown in the figure. The loop is pulled with a constant velocity of 20 cm s 1 till it goes out of the field. Depict the direction of the induced current in the loop as it goes out of the field. For how long would the current in the loop persist? Plot a graph showing the variation of magnetic flux and induced emf as a function of time.
explanation: As the loop goes out of the field, the induced current in it flows clockwise i.e., along MNOP. Induced current flows during the time the loop goes out of the field i.e., from t {tex}=\frac{80}{20}=4 \mathrm{~s}{/tex} to t = {tex}\frac{100}{20}=5 \mathrm{~s}{/tex} . Hence the induced current persists in the loop for 1s.
solution: As the loop goes out of the field, the induced current in it flows clockwise i.e., along MNOP. Induced current flows during the time the loop goes out of the field i.e., from t {tex}=\frac{80}{20}=4 \mathrm{~s}{/tex} to t = {tex}\frac{100}{20}=5 \mathrm{~s}{/tex} . Hence the induced current persists in the loop for 1s.
solution_html:
As the loop goes out of the field, the induced current in it flows clockwise i.e., along MNOP.
Induced current flows during the time the loop goes out of the field i.e., from t  {tex}=\frac{80}{20}=4 \mathrm{~s}{/tex} to t = {tex}\frac{100}{20}=5 \mathrm{~s}{/tex}. Hence the induced current persists in the loop for 1s.

question_html:
A metallic rod of length l and resistance R is rotated with a frequency {tex}\nu{/tex}, with one end hinged at the centre and the other end at the circumference of a circular metallic ring of radius l, about an axis passing through the centre and perpendicular to the plane of the ring. A constant and uniform magnetic field B parallel to the axis is present everywhere.

  1. Derive the expression for the induced emf and the current in the rod.
  2. Due to the presence of the current in the rod and of the magnetic field, find the expression for the magnitude and direction of the force acting on this rod.
  3. Hence obtain the expression for the power required to rotate the rod.
question_text: A metallic rod of length l and resistance R is rotated with a frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} , with one end hinged at the centre and the other end at the circumference of a circular metallic ring of radius l, about an axis passing through the centre and perpendicular to the plane of the ring. A constant and uniform magnetic field B parallel to the axis is present everywhere. Derive the expression for the induced emf and the current in the rod. Due to the presence of the current in the rod and of the magnetic field, find the expression for the magnitude and direction of the force acting on this rod. Hence obtain the expression for the power required to rotate the rod.
explanation:
In the one revolution, change of area, {tex}d A = \pi l ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}	herefore {/tex} Change of magnetic flux in one revolution of the rod, {tex}d{ \phi_B} = \vec B \cdot d \vec A = B d A \cos 0 ^ { \circ } = B \pi l ^ { 2 }{/tex} (Given, magnetic field intensity, {tex}\vec B{/tex} is parallel to change in area, {tex}d \vec A{/tex} ) If period of revolution is T, Induced emf (e) = {tex}​​​​ rac {d\phi}{dt} =  rac {B {tex}\pi{/tex} l^2}{T} = B {tex}\pi{/tex} l^2 
u{/tex} ( {tex} ecause 
u =  rac {1}{T}{/tex} ) Induced current in the rod, {tex}I =  rac { e } { R } =  rac { \pi 
u B l ^ { 2 } } { R }{/tex} (Given R = resistance of the rod) Magnitude of force acting on the rod, {tex}|\vec F| = | I (\vec l × \vec B)|=B I l sin 90^0 =  rac { \pi 
u B ^ { 2 } l ^ { 3 } } { R }{/tex} The external force required to rotate the rod opposes the Lorentz force acting on the rod, i.e external force acts in the direction opposite to the Lorentz force. Power required to rotate the rod, {tex}P = \vec F. \vec v = Fv cos 0^0 =  rac { \pi 
u B ^ { 2 } l ^ { 3 } v } { R }{/tex}
solution:
In the one revolution, change of area, {tex}d A = \pi l ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}	herefore {/tex} Change of magnetic flux in one revolution of the rod, {tex}d{ \phi_B} = \vec B \cdot d \vec A = B d A \cos 0 ^ { \circ } = B \pi l ^ { 2 }{/tex} (Given, magnetic field intensity, {tex}\vec B{/tex} is parallel to change in area, {tex}d \vec A{/tex} ) If period of revolution is T, Induced emf (e) = {tex}​​​​ rac {d\phi}{dt} =  rac {B {tex}\pi{/tex} l^2}{T} = B {tex}\pi{/tex} l^2 
u{/tex} ( {tex} ecause 
u =  rac {1}{T}{/tex} ) Induced current in the rod, {tex}I =  rac { e } { R } =  rac { \pi 
u B l ^ { 2 } } { R }{/tex} (Given R = resistance of the rod) Magnitude of force acting on the rod, {tex}|\vec F| = | I (\vec l × \vec B)|=B I l sin 90^0 =  rac { \pi 
u B ^ { 2 } l ^ { 3 } } { R }{/tex} The external force required to rotate the rod opposes the Lorentz force acting on the rod, i.e external force acts in the direction opposite to the Lorentz force. Power required to rotate the rod, {tex}P = \vec F. \vec v = Fv cos 0^0 =  rac { \pi 
u B ^ { 2 } l ^ { 3 } v } { R }{/tex}
solution_html:
1. In the one revolution, change of area, 
    {tex}d A = \pi l ^ { 2 }{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} Change of magnetic flux in one revolution of the rod,
    {tex}d{ \phi_B} = \vec B \cdot d \vec A = B d A \cos 0 ^ { \circ } = B \pi l ^ { 2 }{/tex}
    (Given, magnetic field intensity, {tex}\vec B{/tex} is parallel to change in area, {tex}d \vec A{/tex})
    If period of revolution is T,
    1. Induced emf (e) = {tex}​​​​\frac {d\phi}{dt} = \frac {B{tex}\pi{/tex}l^2}{T} = B{tex}\pi{/tex}l^2 \nu{/tex} ({tex}\because \nu = \frac {1}{T}{/tex})
    2. Induced current in the rod,
      {tex}I = \frac { e } { R } = \frac { \pi \nu B l ^ { 2 } } { R }{/tex}
      (Given R = resistance of the rod)
  2. Magnitude of force acting on the rod,
    {tex}|\vec F| = | I (\vec l × \vec B)|=B I l sin 90^0 = \frac { \pi \nu B ^ { 2 } l ^ { 3 } } { R }{/tex}
    The external force required to rotate the rod opposes the Lorentz force acting on the rod, i.e external force acts in the direction opposite to the Lorentz force.
  3. Power required to rotate the rod,
    {tex}P = \vec F. \vec v = Fv cos 0^0 = \frac { \pi \nu B ^ { 2 } l ^ { 3 } v } { R }{/tex}

question_html: The electric current flowing in a wire in the direction from B to A is decreasing. Find out the direction of the induced current in the metallic loop kept near the wire as shown in the figure.
question_text: The electric current flowing in a wire in the direction from B to A is decreasing. Find out the direction of the induced current in the metallic loop kept near the wire as shown in the figure.
explanation: As the current is flowing from B to A and is decreasing, the magnetic field will be inwards of the plane of the loop and as the flux of the magnetic field is decreasing as per Lenz’s law an induced current will flow in the clockwise direction.
solution: As the current is flowing from B to A and is decreasing, the magnetic field will be inwards of the plane of the loop and as the flux of the magnetic field is decreasing as per Lenz’s law an induced current will flow in the clockwise direction.
solution_html: As the current is flowing from B to A and is decreasing, the magnetic field will be inwards of the plane of the loop and as the flux of the magnetic field is decreasing as per Lenz’s law an induced current will flow in the clockwise direction.

question_html: A flexible wire of irregular shape, abed as shown in the figure, turns into a circular shape when placed in a magnetic field which is directed normal to the plane of the loop away from the reader. Predict the direction of the induced current in the wire.
question_text: A flexible wire of irregular shape, abed as shown in the figure, turns into a circular shape when placed in a magnetic field which is directed normal to the plane of the loop away from the reader. Predict the direction of the induced current in the wire.
explanation: The force is acting on the wire outwards.The direction of the induced current should be such that it will produce magnetic field outwards. Hence, induced current is flowing in anti-clockwise direction in the loop from cbadc i.e. anticlockwise.
solution: The force is acting on the wire outwards.The direction of the induced current should be such that it will produce magnetic field outwards. Hence, induced current is flowing in anti-clockwise direction in the loop from cbadc i.e. anticlockwise.
solution_html: The force is acting on the wire outwards.The direction of the induced current should be such that it will produce magnetic field outwards. Hence, induced current is flowing in anti-clockwise direction in the loop from cbadc i.e. anticlockwise.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Describe a simple experiment (or activity) to show that the polarity of emf induced in a coil is always such that it tends to produce a current which opposes the change of magnetic flux that produces it.
  2. The current flowing through an inductor of self-inductance L is continuously increasing. Plot a graph showing the variation of
    1. magnetic flux versus the current.
    2. induced emf versus {tex}\frac{dI}{dt}{/tex}
    3. magnetic potential energy stored versus the current.
question_text: Describe a simple experiment (or activity) to show that the polarity of emf induced in a coil is always such that it tends to produce a current which opposes the change of magnetic flux that produces it. The current flowing through an inductor of self-inductance L is continuously increasing. Plot a graph showing the variation of magnetic flux versus the current. induced emf versus {tex}\frac{dI}{dt}{/tex} magnetic potential energy stored versus the current.
explanation: When the North pole of a bar magnet moves towards the closed coil, the magnetic flux through the coil increases. This produces an induced emf which produces (or tend to produce if the coil is open) an induced current in the anti-clockwise sense. The anti-clockwise sense corresponds to the generation of North pole which opposes the motion of the approaching N pole of the magnet. The face of the coil, facing the approaching magnet, then has the same polarity as that of the approaching pole of the magnet. The induced current, therefore, is seen to oppose the change of magnetic flux that produces it. When a North pole of a magnet is moved away from the coil, the current (I) flows in the clock-wise sense which corresponds to the generation of South pole. The induced South pole opposes the motion of the receding North pole. {tex}\phi{/tex} = LI Where, l = Strength of current through the coil at any time {tex}\phi{/tex} = Amount of magnetic flux linked with all turns of the coil at that time and L = Constant of proportionality called coefficient of self induction Induced emf, {tex}\mathrm{e}=\frac{-\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{-\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}(\mathrm{LI}){/tex} i.e., {tex}\mathrm{e}=-\mathrm{L} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} [The graph is drawn considering only magnitude of e] Magnetic potential energy is given by, {tex}\mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Ll}^{2}{/tex}
solution: When the North pole of a bar magnet moves towards the closed coil, the magnetic flux through the coil increases. This produces an induced emf which produces (or tend to produce if the coil is open) an induced current in the anti-clockwise sense. The anti-clockwise sense corresponds to the generation of North pole which opposes the motion of the approaching N pole of the magnet. The face of the coil, facing the approaching magnet, then has the same polarity as that of the approaching pole of the magnet. The induced current, therefore, is seen to oppose the change of magnetic flux that produces it. When a North pole of a magnet is moved away from the coil, the current (I) flows in the clock-wise sense which corresponds to the generation of South pole. The induced South pole opposes the motion of the receding North pole. {tex}\phi{/tex} = LI Where, l = Strength of current through the coil at any time {tex}\phi{/tex} = Amount of magnetic flux linked with all turns of the coil at that time and L = Constant of proportionality called coefficient of self induction Induced emf, {tex}\mathrm{e}=\frac{-\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{-\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}(\mathrm{LI}){/tex} i.e., {tex}\mathrm{e}=-\mathrm{L} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} [The graph is drawn considering only magnitude of e] Magnetic potential energy is given by, {tex}\mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Ll}^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. When the North pole of a bar magnet moves towards the closed coil, the magnetic flux through the coil increases. This produces an induced emf which produces (or tend to produce if the coil is open) an induced current in the anti-clockwise sense. The anti-clockwise sense corresponds to the generation of North pole which opposes the motion of the approaching N pole of the magnet. The face of the coil, facing the approaching magnet, then has the same polarity as that of the approaching pole of the magnet. The induced current, therefore, is seen to oppose the change of magnetic flux that produces it.

    When a North pole of a magnet is moved away from the coil, the current (I) flows in the clock-wise sense which corresponds to the generation of South pole. The induced South pole opposes the motion of the receding North pole.
    1. {tex}\phi{/tex} = LI
      Where, l = Strength of current through the coil at any time
      {tex}\phi{/tex} = Amount of magnetic flux linked with all turns of the coil at that time
      and L = Constant of proportionality called coefficient of self induction
    2. Induced emf,
      {tex}\mathrm{e}=\frac{-\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{-\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}(\mathrm{LI}){/tex} i.e., {tex}\mathrm{e}=-\mathrm{L} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex}

      [The graph is drawn considering only magnitude of e]
    3. Magnetic potential energy is given by,
      {tex}\mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Ll}^{2}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Define the term self-inductance of a solenoid. Obtain the expression for the magnetic energy stored in an inductor of self-inductance L to build up a current I through it.
question_text: Define the term self-inductance of a solenoid. Obtain the expression for the magnetic energy stored in an inductor of self-inductance L to build up a current I through it.
explanation: Using formula, {tex}|-\varepsilon|=L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} If {tex}\frac{d I}{d t}=1{/tex} A/s, then L = {tex}|-\varepsilon|{/tex} Self inductance of the coil is equal to the magnitude of induced emf produced in the coil itself when the current varies at rate 1 A/s. Expression for magnetic energy: When a time varying current flows through the coil, back emf {tex}(-\varepsilon){/tex} produces, which opposes the growth of the current flow. It means some work needs to be done against induced emf in establishing a current I. This work done will be stored as magnetic potential energy. For the current I at any instant, the rate of work done is {tex}\frac{d W}{d t}=(-\varepsilon) I{/tex} Only for inductive effect of the coil {tex}|-\varepsilon|=L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{d W}{d t}=L\left(\frac{d I}{d t}\right) I \quad \Rightarrow d W=L I d I{/tex} From work-energy theorem, dU = LIdI {tex}\therefore U=\int_{0}^{I} L I d I=\frac{1}{2} L I^{2}{/tex}
solution: Using formula, {tex}|-\varepsilon|=L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} If {tex}\frac{d I}{d t}=1{/tex} A/s, then L = {tex}|-\varepsilon|{/tex} Self inductance of the coil is equal to the magnitude of induced emf produced in the coil itself when the current varies at rate 1 A/s. Expression for magnetic energy: When a time varying current flows through the coil, back emf {tex}(-\varepsilon){/tex} produces, which opposes the growth of the current flow. It means some work needs to be done against induced emf in establishing a current I. This work done will be stored as magnetic potential energy. For the current I at any instant, the rate of work done is {tex}\frac{d W}{d t}=(-\varepsilon) I{/tex} Only for inductive effect of the coil {tex}|-\varepsilon|=L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{d W}{d t}=L\left(\frac{d I}{d t}\right) I \quad \Rightarrow d W=L I d I{/tex} From work-energy theorem, dU = LIdI {tex}\therefore U=\int_{0}^{I} L I d I=\frac{1}{2} L I^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Using formula, {tex}|-\varepsilon|=L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex}
If {tex}\frac{d I}{d t}=1{/tex} A/s, then L = {tex}|-\varepsilon|{/tex}
Self inductance of the coil is equal to the magnitude of induced emf produced in the coil itself when the current varies at rate 1 A/s.
Expression for magnetic energy:

When a time varying current flows through the coil, back emf {tex}(-\varepsilon){/tex} produces, which opposes the growth of the current flow. It means some work needs to be done against induced emf in establishing a current I. This work done will be stored as magnetic potential energy.
For the current I at any instant, the rate of work done is
{tex}\frac{d W}{d t}=(-\varepsilon) I{/tex}
Only for inductive effect of the coil {tex}|-\varepsilon|=L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{d W}{d t}=L\left(\frac{d I}{d t}\right) I \quad \Rightarrow d W=L I d I{/tex}
From work-energy theorem,
dU = LIdI
{tex}\therefore U=\int_{0}^{I} L I d I=\frac{1}{2} L I^{2}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
The current flowing in the two coils of self-inductance L1 = 16 mH and L2 = 12 mH are increasing at the same rate. If the power supplied to the two coils are equal, then find the ratio of

  1. induced voltages
  2. the currents and
  3. the energies stored in the two coils at a given instant.
question_text: The current flowing in the two coils of self-inductance L 1 = 16 mH and L 2 = 12 mH are increasing at the same rate. If the power supplied to the two coils are equal, then find the ratio of induced voltages the currents and the energies stored in the two coils at a given instant.
explanation: Given, L 1 = 16 mH, L 2 = 12 mH Induced voltage is given by {tex}V = L \frac { d I } { d t }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad V \propto L{/tex} (when rate of change of electric current with respect to time remains unchanged) So, {tex}\frac { V _ { 1 } } { V _ { 2 } } = \frac { L _ { 1 } } { L _ { 2 } } \quad \left( \text { as } \frac { d I } { d t } \text { is same } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { V _ { 1 } } { V _ { 2 } } = \frac { 16 } { 12 } = \frac { 4 } { 3 }{/tex} ...(i) Power (P) = V × I Now, {tex}\frac { I _ { 1 } } { I _ { 2 } } = \frac { V _ { 2 } } { V _ { 1 } }{/tex} (as power, P remains constant) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { I _ { 1 } } { I _ { 2 } } = \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex} ...(ii) Energy stored is given by coil-1 and coil-2 {tex}E _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } L _ { 1 } I _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} ....(iii) and {tex}E _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } L _ { 2 } I _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} ....(iv) On dividing Eqs. (iii) and (iv), we get {tex}\frac{{{E_1}}}{{{E_2}}} = \frac{{{L_1}I_1^2}}{{{L_2}I_2^2}} = \frac{{16}}{{12}} \times \frac{9}{{16}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac { E _ { 1 } } { E _ { 2 } } = \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex}
solution: Given, L 1 = 16 mH, L 2 = 12 mH Induced voltage is given by {tex}V = L \frac { d I } { d t }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad V \propto L{/tex} (when rate of change of electric current with respect to time remains unchanged) So, {tex}\frac { V _ { 1 } } { V _ { 2 } } = \frac { L _ { 1 } } { L _ { 2 } } \quad \left( \text { as } \frac { d I } { d t } \text { is same } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { V _ { 1 } } { V _ { 2 } } = \frac { 16 } { 12 } = \frac { 4 } { 3 }{/tex} ...(i) Power (P) = V × I Now, {tex}\frac { I _ { 1 } } { I _ { 2 } } = \frac { V _ { 2 } } { V _ { 1 } }{/tex} (as power, P remains constant) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { I _ { 1 } } { I _ { 2 } } = \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex} ...(ii) Energy stored is given by coil-1 and coil-2 {tex}E _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } L _ { 1 } I _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} ....(iii) and {tex}E _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } L _ { 2 } I _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} ....(iv) On dividing Eqs. (iii) and (iv), we get {tex}\frac{{{E_1}}}{{{E_2}}} = \frac{{{L_1}I_1^2}}{{{L_2}I_2^2}} = \frac{{16}}{{12}} \times \frac{9}{{16}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac { E _ { 1 } } { E _ { 2 } } = \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, L1 = 16 mH, L2 = 12 mH

  1. Induced voltage is given by
    {tex}V = L \frac { d I } { d t }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad V \propto L{/tex} (when rate of change of electric current with respect to time remains unchanged)
    So, {tex}\frac { V _ { 1 } } { V _ { 2 } } = \frac { L _ { 1 } } { L _ { 2 } } \quad \left( \text { as } \frac { d I } { d t } \text { is same } \right){/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { V _ { 1 } } { V _ { 2 } } = \frac { 16 } { 12 } = \frac { 4 } { 3 }{/tex} ...(i)
  2. Power (P) = V × I
    Now, {tex}\frac { I _ { 1 } } { I _ { 2 } } = \frac { V _ { 2 } } { V _ { 1 } }{/tex} (as power, P remains constant)
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { I _ { 1 } } { I _ { 2 } } = \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex} ...(ii)
  3. Energy stored is given by coil-1 and coil-2
    {tex}E _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } L _ { 1 } I _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }{/tex}....(iii) and
    {tex}E _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } L _ { 2 } I _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex}....(iv)
    On dividing Eqs. (iii) and (iv), we get
    {tex}\frac{{{E_1}}}{{{E_2}}} = \frac{{{L_1}I_1^2}}{{{L_2}I_2^2}} = \frac{{16}}{{12}} \times \frac{9}{{16}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \frac { E _ { 1 } } { E _ { 2 } } = \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. A rod of length l is moved horizontally with a uniform velocity v in a direction perpendicular to its length through a region in which a uniform magnetic field is acting vertically downward. Derive the expression for the emf induced across the ends of the rod.
  2. How does one understand this motional emf by invoking the Lorentz force acting on the free charge carriers of the conductor? Explain.
question_text: A rod of length l is moved horizontally with a uniform velocity v in a direction perpendicular to its length through a region in which a uniform magnetic field is acting vertically downward. Derive the expression for the emf induced across the ends of the rod. How does one understand this motional emf by invoking the Lorentz force acting on the free charge carriers of the conductor? Explain.
explanation: Suppose a rod of length 'l' moves with velocity v inward in the region having uniform magnetic field B. Initial magnetic flux enclosed in the rectangular space is {tex}\phi=|B| l x{/tex} As the rod moves with velocity {tex}-v=\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} Using Lenz's law, {tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=-\frac{d}{d t}(B l x)=B l\left(-\frac{d x}{d t}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \varepsilon=B l v{/tex} Suppose any arbitrary charge 'q' in the conductor of length 'l' moving inward in the field as shown in figure, the change q also moves with velocity v in the magnetic field B. The Lorentz force on the charge 'q' is F = qvB and its direction is downwards. So, work done in moving the charge 'q' along the conductor of length l W = F.l W = qvBl Since emf is the work done per unit charge {tex}\therefore \quad \varepsilon=\frac{W}{q}=B l v{/tex} This equation gives emf induced across the rod.
solution: Suppose a rod of length 'l' moves with velocity v inward in the region having uniform magnetic field B. Initial magnetic flux enclosed in the rectangular space is {tex}\phi=|B| l x{/tex} As the rod moves with velocity {tex}-v=\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} Using Lenz's law, {tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=-\frac{d}{d t}(B l x)=B l\left(-\frac{d x}{d t}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \varepsilon=B l v{/tex} Suppose any arbitrary charge 'q' in the conductor of length 'l' moving inward in the field as shown in figure, the change q also moves with velocity v in the magnetic field B. The Lorentz force on the charge 'q' is F = qvB and its direction is downwards. So, work done in moving the charge 'q' along the conductor of length l W = F.l W = qvBl Since emf is the work done per unit charge {tex}\therefore \quad \varepsilon=\frac{W}{q}=B l v{/tex} This equation gives emf induced across the rod.
solution_html:
1. Suppose a rod of length 'l' moves with velocity v inward in the region having uniform magnetic field B.
    Initial magnetic flux enclosed in the rectangular space is {tex}\phi=|B| l x{/tex}
    As the rod moves with velocity {tex}-v=\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex}
    Using Lenz's law,
    {tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=-\frac{d}{d t}(B l x)=B l\left(-\frac{d x}{d t}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad \varepsilon=B l v{/tex}
  2. Suppose any arbitrary charge 'q' in the conductor of length 'l' moving inward in the field as shown in figure, the change q also moves with velocity v in the magnetic field B.
    The Lorentz force on the charge 'q' is F = qvB and its direction is downwards.
    So, work done in moving the charge 'q' along the conductor of length l
    W = F.l
    W = qvBl
    Since emf is the work done per unit charge
    {tex}\therefore \quad \varepsilon=\frac{W}{q}=B l v{/tex}
    This equation gives emf induced across the rod.

subject: Physics
question_html: The electric current in a wire in the direction from B to A is increasing. What is the direction of induced current in the metallic loop kept above the wire as shown in a given figure?
question_text: The electric current in a wire in the direction from B to A is increasing. What is the direction of induced current in the metallic loop kept above the wire as shown in a given figure?
explanation: When the increasing current flows through the wire in the direction from point B to A, the increasing magnetic field is produced; which is directed perpendicular to the plane of the loop (or the plane of paper) and in inward direction. Due to this, induced e.m.f. is produced in the loop which opposes the magnetic field produced due to the current flowing through the wire i.e. induced current in the loop should flow in a direction so that it produces magnetic field perpendicular to the plane of the loop and in outward direction. Maxwell's cork screw rule tells that induced current in the loop will flow in anticlockwise direction.
solution: When the increasing current flows through the wire in the direction from point B to A, the increasing magnetic field is produced; which is directed perpendicular to the plane of the loop (or the plane of paper) and in inward direction. Due to this, induced e.m.f. is produced in the loop which opposes the magnetic field produced due to the current flowing through the wire i.e. induced current in the loop should flow in a direction so that it produces magnetic field perpendicular to the plane of the loop and in outward direction. Maxwell's cork screw rule tells that induced current in the loop will flow in anticlockwise direction.

Maxwell's cork screw rule tells that induced current in the loop will flow in anticlockwise direction.

subject: Physics
question_html: Describe a simple experiment (or activity) to show that the polarity of emf induced in a coil is always such that it tends to produce a current which opposes the change of magnetic flux that produces it.
question_text: Describe a simple experiment (or activity) to show that the polarity of emf induced in a coil is always such that it tends to produce a current which opposes the change of magnetic flux that produces it.
explanation: When a bar magnet is brought close to the coil (fig a), the approaching North pole of the bar magnet increases the magnetic flux linked to it. This produces an induced emf which produces (or tends to produce, if the coil is open) an induced current in the anticlockwise direction. The face of the coil, facing the approaching magnet, then has the same polarity as that of the approaching pole of the magnet. The induced current, therefore, is seen to oppose the change of magnetic flux that produces it. According to Lenz's law, the polarity of the induced emf is such that it opposes a change in magnetic flux responsible for its production.

The face of the coil, facing the approaching magnet, then has the same polarity as that of the approaching pole of the magnet. According to Lenz's law, the polarity of the induced emf is such that it opposes a change in magnetic flux responsible for its production.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Define mutual inductance.
  2. A pair of adjacent coils has a mutual inductance of 1.5 H. If the current in one coil changes from 0 to 20 A in 0.5 s, what is the change of flux linkage with the other coil?
question_text: Define mutual inductance. A pair of adjacent coils has a mutual inductance of 1.5 H. If the current in one coil changes from 0 to 20 A in 0.5 s, what is the change of flux linkage with the other coil?
explanation: Mutual inductance is numerically equal to the induced emf in the secondary coil when the current in the primary coil changes by unity. Or Mutual Inductance is the interaction of one coils magnetic field on another coil as it induces a voltage in the adjacent coil. Mutual inductance of a pair of coils, {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.5 H Initial current, I 1 = 0 A Final current I 2 = 20 A Change in current will be: {tex}\Delta I{/tex} = l 2 - l 1 20 - 0 = 20 A and we know, {tex}\Delta \phi{/tex} = M {tex}\Delta{/tex} I, where, {tex}\Delta \phi{/tex} is change in magnetic flux {tex}\Delta \phi{/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 30 Wb Hence, change in the flux linkage will be 30 Wb.
solution: Mutual inductance is numerically equal to the induced emf in the secondary coil when the current in the primary coil changes by unity. Or Mutual Inductance is the interaction of one coils magnetic field on another coil as it induces a voltage in the adjacent coil. Mutual inductance of a pair of coils, {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.5 H Initial current, I 1 = 0 A Final current I 2 = 20 A Change in current will be: {tex}\Delta I{/tex} = l 2 - l 1 20 - 0 = 20 A and we know, {tex}\Delta \phi{/tex} = M {tex}\Delta{/tex} I, where, {tex}\Delta \phi{/tex} is change in magnetic flux {tex}\Delta \phi{/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 30 Wb Hence, change in the flux linkage will be 30 Wb.
solution_html:
1. Mutual inductance is numerically equal to the induced emf in the secondary coil when the current in the primary coil changes by unity. Or Mutual Inductance is the interaction of one coils magnetic field on another coil as it induces a voltage in the adjacent coil.
  2. Mutual inductance of a pair of coils, {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.5 H
    Initial current, I1 = 0 A
    Final current I2 = 20 A
    Change in current will be: {tex}\Delta I{/tex} = l2 - l1
    20 - 0 = 20 A
    and we know,
    {tex}\Delta \phi{/tex} = M{tex}\Delta{/tex}I,
    where, {tex}\Delta \phi{/tex} is change in magnetic flux
    {tex}\Delta \phi{/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 30 Wb
    Hence, change in the flux linkage will be 30 Wb.

question_html: State the law that gives the polarity of the induced emf.
question_text: State the law that gives the polarity of the induced emf.
explanation: The law that states the polarity of induced emf is known as Lenz's law, It states that the direction of induced emf in a circuit is such that it always opposes the cause that produces it. It means that if current is induced due to increasing magnetic flux then the direction of induced current is such that it tries to decrease the magnetic flux and vice versa.

question_html: Predict the polarity of the capacitor in the situation described by adjoining as shown in figure. Explain the reason too.
question_text: Predict the polarity of the capacitor in the situation described by adjoining as shown in figure. Explain the reason too.
explanation: According to figure shown in the question, induced current is in anti-clockwise, when seen from left hand side and its direction is in clockwise when seen from right hand side(direction of current seen from both sides are identified from Lenz's law). Thus, direction of induced current is in clockwise sense. This implies that plate A of the capacitor is at the higher potential than plate B(since current is directed from higher to lower potential), i.e. B is a negative plate while A is a positive plate.

subject: Physics
question_html: Derive the expression for the self-inductance of a long solenoid of cross-sectional area A and length l, having n turns per unit length.
question_text: Derive the expression for the self-inductance of a long solenoid of cross-sectional area A and length l, having n turns per unit length.
explanation: Total number of turns in solenoid N = nl Magnetic field inside the long solenoid B = {tex}{\mu}_{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} ni Flux through one turn {tex}\phi_{1}{/tex} = BA = {tex}\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} niA Thus total flux through N turns {tex}\phi_{\mathrm{t}}=\mathbf{N} \phi_{1}=\mathbf{n} \mathbf{l} \times \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\mathbf{o}} \mathbf{n i} \mathbf{A}=\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\mathbf{o}} \mathbf{n}^{2} \mathbf{l}{/tex} Ai Using {tex}\phi_{\mathrm{t}}{/tex} = Li where L is the self inductance of the coil {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mu_on^2l{/tex} Ai = Li {tex}\Rightarrow {L}={\mu}_{\mathrm{o}} {n}^{2}{/tex}
solution: Total number of turns in solenoid N = nl Magnetic field inside the long solenoid B = {tex}{\mu}_{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} ni Flux through one turn {tex}\phi_{1}{/tex} = BA = {tex}\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} niA Thus total flux through N turns {tex}\phi_{\mathrm{t}}=\mathbf{N} \phi_{1}=\mathbf{n} \mathbf{l} \times \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\mathbf{o}} \mathbf{n i} \mathbf{A}=\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\mathbf{o}} \mathbf{n}^{2} \mathbf{l}{/tex} Ai Using {tex}\phi_{\mathrm{t}}{/tex} = Li where L is the self inductance of the coil {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mu_on^2l{/tex} Ai = Li {tex}\Rightarrow {L}={\mu}_{\mathrm{o}} {n}^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Total number of turns in solenoid N = nl
Magnetic field inside the long solenoid B = {tex}{\mu}_{\mathrm{o}}{/tex}ni
Flux through one turn {tex}\phi_{1}{/tex} = BA = {tex}\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} niA
Thus total flux through N turns {tex}\phi_{\mathrm{t}}=\mathbf{N} \phi_{1}=\mathbf{n} \mathbf{l} \times \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\mathbf{o}} \mathbf{n i} \mathbf{A}=\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\mathbf{o}} \mathbf{n}^{2} \mathbf{l}{/tex} Ai
Using {tex}\phi_{\mathrm{t}}{/tex} = Li where L is the self inductance of the coil
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mu_on^2l{/tex} Ai = Li {tex}\Rightarrow {L}={\mu}_{\mathrm{o}} {n}^{2}{/tex}

question_html: On what factors, does the magnitude of the emf induced in the circuit due to magnetic flux depend?
question_text: On what factors, does the magnitude of the emf induced in the circuit due to magnetic flux depend?
explanation: The relation between the magnetic flux and the induced emf is given by: {tex}E=\frac{-d \phi}{d t}{/tex} Where {tex}\phi=B A \cos \theta{/tex} where, B is the magnetic field and A is the area of the coil. Induced EMF is also given by: E = Blv sin {tex}\theta{/tex} where l is the length of the coil and v is the velocity of the motion of coil. So, it is clear that the magnetic flux depends on the flux density, length of the coil, velocity with which the coil is moved and the angle between the velocity and flux.
solution: The relation between the magnetic flux and the induced emf is given by: {tex}E=\frac{-d \phi}{d t}{/tex} Where {tex}\phi=B A \cos \theta{/tex} where, B is the magnetic field and A is the area of the coil. Induced EMF is also given by: E = Blv sin {tex}\theta{/tex} where l is the length of the coil and v is the velocity of the motion of coil. So, it is clear that the magnetic flux depends on the flux density, length of the coil, velocity with which the coil is moved and the angle between the velocity and flux.
solution_html:
The relation between the magnetic flux and the induced emf is given by:
{tex}E=\frac{-d \phi}{d t}{/tex}
Where
{tex}\phi=B A \cos \theta{/tex}
where, B is the magnetic field and A is the area of the coil.
Induced EMF is also given by:
E = Blv sin{tex}\theta{/tex}
where l is the length of the coil and v is the velocity of the motion of coil.
So, it is clear that the magnetic flux depends on the flux density, length of the coil, velocity with which the coil is moved and the angle between the velocity and flux.

subject: Physics
question_html:
How does the mutual inductance of a pair of coils change, when

  1. distance between the coils is increased and
  2. number of turns in the coils is increased?
question_text: How does the mutual inductance of a pair of coils change, when distance between the coils is increased and number of turns in the coils is increased?
explanation: Mutual inductance is dependent of separation between the coils. As the coils are moved further apart, less amount of flux will pass through one coil due to the current in the other. Mutual inductance of two coils can be found out by {tex}M = {\mu _0}{n_1}{n_2}Al,{\text{ i}}{\text{. }}e.{/tex} M {tex}\propto n _ { 1 } n _ { 2 }{/tex} So, with the increase in number of turns mutual inductance increases.
solution: Mutual inductance is dependent of separation between the coils. As the coils are moved further apart, less amount of flux will pass through one coil due to the current in the other. Mutual inductance of two coils can be found out by {tex}M = {\mu _0}{n_1}{n_2}Al,{\text{ i}}{\text{. }}e.{/tex} M {tex}\propto n _ { 1 } n _ { 2 }{/tex} So, with the increase in number of turns mutual inductance increases.
solution_html:
1. Mutual inductance is dependent of separation between the coils. As the coils are moved further apart, less amount of flux will pass through one coil due to the current in the other.
  2. Mutual inductance of two coils can be found out by {tex}M = {\mu _0}{n_1}{n_2}Al,{\text{ i}}{\text{. }}e.{/tex}
    M {tex}\propto n _ { 1 } n _ { 2 }{/tex}
    So, with the increase in number of turns mutual inductance increases.

solution_html:
1. Current in the loop PQRS, I = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{r}{/tex} Since {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} = B{tex}\ell{/tex}v So, I = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{B} \ell v}{r}{/tex}
  2. The force required to keep the arm PQ in constant motion F = BIl = B({tex}\frac{Blv}{r}{/tex}) lF = {tex}\frac{(B2l2v)}{r} {/tex}
  3. Power required to move the arm PQ = |F|v| = {tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{B}^2 \ell^2 v}{r}\right)|v|{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{B}^2 \ell^2 v^2}{r}\right){/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: State the Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction.
question_text: State the Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction.
explanation: Faraday, on the basis of his experiments on electromagnetic induction, he gave the following two laws: First law: Whenever magnetic flux linked with a circuit changes, an emf is induced in it, which lasts as long as the change in flux continues. Second law: The emf(e) induced in a loop or in a closed circuit is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux linked with the loop i.e., {tex}e = - N\frac{{d\phi _B}}{{dt}}{/tex} where, N = Number of turns in the coil and negative sign indicates that the induced emf opposes the change in magnetic flux, which produces the induced emf(according to Lenz's law), {tex}\frac{d\phi_B }{dt}{/tex} is the rate of change of magnetic flux with time.
solution: Faraday, on the basis of his experiments on electromagnetic induction, he gave the following two laws: First law: Whenever magnetic flux linked with a circuit changes, an emf is induced in it, which lasts as long as the change in flux continues. Second law: The emf(e) induced in a loop or in a closed circuit is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux linked with the loop i.e., {tex}e = - N\frac{{d\phi _B}}{{dt}}{/tex} where, N = Number of turns in the coil and negative sign indicates that the induced emf opposes the change in magnetic flux, which produces the induced emf(according to Lenz's law), {tex}\frac{d\phi_B }{dt}{/tex} is the rate of change of magnetic flux with time.
solution_html:
Faraday, on the basis of his experiments on electromagnetic induction, he gave the following two laws:
First law: Whenever magnetic flux linked with a circuit changes, an emf is induced in it, which lasts as long as the change in flux continues. 
Second law: The emf(e) induced in a loop or in a closed circuit is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux linked with the loop
i.e., {tex}e = - N\frac{{d\phi _B}}{{dt}}{/tex}
where, N  = Number of turns in the coil and negative sign indicates that the induced emf opposes the change in magnetic flux, which produces the induced emf(according to Lenz's law), {tex}\frac{d\phi_B }{dt}{/tex} is the rate of change of magnetic flux with time.

question_html: A bar magnet is moved in the direction indicated by the arrow between two coils PQ and CD. Predict the directions of induced current in each coil.
question_text: A bar magnet is moved in the direction indicated by the arrow between two coils PQ and CD. Predict the directions of induced current in each coil.
explanation: It is clear that North pole of the magnet is moving away from coil PQ, so current will be induced in such a way that it acts as South pole. Hence, the direction of current will be anti-clockwise. Again, the South pole is approaching towards coil CD, so end C of the coil will act as South pole. Hence, the direction of current will be clockwise.
solution: It is clear that North pole of the magnet is moving away from coil PQ, so current will be induced in such a way that it acts as South pole. Hence, the direction of current will be anti-clockwise. Again, the South pole is approaching towards coil CD, so end C of the coil will act as South pole. Hence, the direction of current will be clockwise.
solution_html: It is clear that North pole of the magnet is moving away from coil PQ, so current will be induced in such a way that it acts as South pole. Hence, the direction of current will be anti-clockwise. Again, the South pole is approaching towards coil CD, so end C of the coil will act as South pole. Hence, the direction of current will be clockwise.

question_html: A metallic rod of length L is rotated with angular frequency of {tex}\omega {/tex}  with one end hinged at the centre and the other end at the circumference of a circular metallic ring of radius L, about an axis passing through the centre and perpendicular to the plane of the ring. A constant and uniform magnetic field B parallel to the axis is present everywhere. Deduce the expression for the emf between the centre and the metallic ring.
question_text: A metallic rod of length L is rotated with angular frequency of {tex}\omega {/tex} with one end hinged at the centre and the other end at the circumference of a circular metallic ring of radius L, about an axis passing through the centre and perpendicular to the plane of the ring. A constant and uniform magnetic field B parallel to the axis is present everywhere. Deduce the expression for the emf between the centre and the metallic ring.
explanation: Angular velocity of rod, {tex}\omega = \frac{{2\pi }}{T}{/tex} Where, T = time period {tex}\therefore {/tex} Change in flux in one revolution = {tex}BA = B(\pi {L^2}){/tex} According to Faraday's law of EMI, magnitude of induced emf {tex}e = d \phi / d T = B \pi L ^ { 2 } / T \Rightarrow e = B \pi L ^ { 2 } / ( 2 \pi / \omega ) [ \because T = 2 \pi / \omega ]{/tex} {tex}e = \frac { 1 } { 2 } B \omega L ^ { 2 }{/tex} This is the required expression.
solution: Angular velocity of rod, {tex}\omega = \frac{{2\pi }}{T}{/tex} Where, T = time period {tex}\therefore {/tex} Change in flux in one revolution = {tex}BA = B(\pi {L^2}){/tex} According to Faraday's law of EMI, magnitude of induced emf {tex}e = d \phi / d T = B \pi L ^ { 2 } / T \Rightarrow e = B \pi L ^ { 2 } / ( 2 \pi / \omega ) [ \because T = 2 \pi / \omega ]{/tex} {tex}e = \frac { 1 } { 2 } B \omega L ^ { 2 }{/tex} This is the required expression.
solution_html:
Angular velocity of rod, {tex}\omega = \frac{{2\pi }}{T}{/tex}
Where, T = time period
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Change in flux in one revolution = {tex}BA = B(\pi {L^2}){/tex}
According to Faraday's law of EMI, magnitude of induced emf
{tex}e = d \phi / d T = B \pi L ^ { 2 } / T \Rightarrow e = B \pi L ^ { 2 } / ( 2 \pi / \omega ) [ \because T = 2 \pi / \omega ]{/tex}
{tex}e = \frac { 1 } { 2 } B \omega L ^ { 2 }{/tex}
This is the required expression.

question_html: Define mutual inductance between two long coaxial solenoids. Find out the expression for the mutual inductance of inner solenoid of length l having the radius r1 and the number of turns n1 per unit length due to the second outer solenoid of same length and n2 number of turns per unit length.
question_text: Define mutual inductance between two long coaxial solenoids. Find out the expression for the mutual inductance of inner solenoid of length l having the radius r 1 and the number of turns n 1 per unit length due to the second outer solenoid of same length and n 2 number of turns per unit length.
explanation: The phenomenon of generation of induced emf in a long solenoid due to a change of current in another neighbourhood coaxial solenoid is known as mutual induction. And mutual inductance is equal to the induced magnetic flux in one solenoid due to unit current in the other. For two long solenoids assuming perfect coupling, as, flux linked with second solenoid {tex} \propto {/tex} current in the first solenoid. {tex}\therefore \quad \phi _ { B _ { 2} } \propto I _ { 1 }{/tex} Again {tex}\phi_{B_2}{/tex} = {tex}N_2 B_1A = (n_2l)×(\mu_0n_1I_1)×( {tex}\pi{/tex} r^2) {/tex} Similarly, flux linked with the first coil due to current in the second coil, {tex}\phi_{B_1}=N_1B_2A=(n_1l)×(\mu_0n_2I_2)×( {tex}\pi{/tex} r^2){/tex} ....(i) Now taking one of the two solenoids, say the first one, {tex}\phi_{B_1} = MI_2{/tex} ....(ii) Comparing equations (i) and (ii) we get, mutual inductance, M = {tex}\mu_0n_1n_2× {tex}\pi{/tex} r^2l{/tex} This expression will remain same for second solenoid too.
solution: The phenomenon of generation of induced emf in a long solenoid due to a change of current in another neighbourhood coaxial solenoid is known as mutual induction. And mutual inductance is equal to the induced magnetic flux in one solenoid due to unit current in the other. For two long solenoids assuming perfect coupling, as, flux linked with second solenoid {tex} \propto {/tex} current in the first solenoid. {tex}\therefore \quad \phi _ { B _ { 2} } \propto I _ { 1 }{/tex} Again {tex}\phi_{B_2}{/tex} = {tex}N_2 B_1A = (n_2l)×(\mu_0n_1I_1)×( {tex}\pi{/tex} r^2) {/tex} Similarly, flux linked with the first coil due to current in the second coil, {tex}\phi_{B_1}=N_1B_2A=(n_1l)×(\mu_0n_2I_2)×( {tex}\pi{/tex} r^2){/tex} ....(i) Now taking one of the two solenoids, say the first one, {tex}\phi_{B_1} = MI_2{/tex} ....(ii) Comparing equations (i) and (ii) we get, mutual inductance, M = {tex}\mu_0n_1n_2× {tex}\pi{/tex} r^2l{/tex} This expression will remain same for second solenoid too.
solution_html:
The phenomenon of generation of induced emf in a long solenoid due to a change of current in another neighbourhood coaxial solenoid is known as mutual induction. And mutual inductance is equal to the induced magnetic flux in one solenoid due to unit current in the other.
For two long solenoids assuming perfect coupling, as, flux linked with second solenoid {tex} \propto {/tex} current in the first solenoid.
{tex}\therefore \quad \phi _ { B _ { 2} } \propto I _ { 1 }{/tex}
Again {tex}\phi_{B_2}{/tex} = {tex}N_2 B_1A = (n_2l)×(\mu_0n_1I_1)×({tex}\pi{/tex}r^2) {/tex}
Similarly, flux linked with the first coil due to current in the second coil,
{tex}\phi_{B_1}=N_1B_2A=(n_1l)×(\mu_0n_2I_2)×({tex}\pi{/tex}r^2){/tex}....(i)
Now taking one of the two solenoids, say the first one,
{tex}\phi_{B_1} = MI_2{/tex}....(ii)
Comparing equations (i) and (ii) we get, mutual inductance,
M = {tex}\mu_0n_1n_2×{tex}\pi{/tex}r^2l{/tex}
This expression will remain same for second solenoid too.

question_html:
1. State the principle on which AC generator works. Draw a labelled diagram and explain its working.
  2. A conducting rod held horizontally along East-West direction is dropped from rest from a certain height near the Earth's surface. Why should there be an induced emf across the ends of the rod? Draw a plot showing the instantaneous variation of emf as a function of time from the instant it begins to fall.
question_text: State the principle on which AC generator works. Draw a labelled diagram and explain its working. A conducting rod held horizontally along East-West direction is dropped from rest from a certain height near the Earth's surface. Why should there be an induced emf across the ends of the rod? Draw a plot showing the instantaneous variation of emf as a function of time from the instant it begins to fall.
explanation: A.C. generator principle: A dynamo or generator is a device which converts mechanical energy into electrical energy. It is based on the principle of electromagnetic induction. Working: When the armature coil is rotated in the strong magnetic field, the magnetic flux linked with the coil changes and the current is induced in the coil, its direction being given by Fleming's right hand rule. Considering the armature to be in vertical position and as it rotates in anticlockwise direction, the wire ab moves upward and cd downward, so that the direction of induced current is shown in fig. In the internal circuit, the current flows along B 1 R L B 2 . The direction of current remains unchanged during the first half turn of armature. During the second half revolution, the wire ab moves downward and cd upward, so the direction of current is reversed and in external circuit it flows along B 2 R L B 1 . Thus the direction of induced emf and current changes in the external circuit after each half revolution. Expression of induced emf: If N is the number of turns in coil, f the frequency of rotation, A area of coil and B the magnetic induction, then induced emf {tex}e=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}\{N B A(\cos 2 \pi f t)\}{/tex} {tex}=2 \pi N B A f \sin 2 \pi f t{/tex} Obviously, the emf produced is alternating and hence the current is also alternating. Current produced by an ac generator cannot be measured by moving coil ammeter because the average value of ac over full cycle is zero. The source of energy generation is the mechanical energy of rotation of armature coil. As the earth's magnetic field lines are cut by the falling rod, the change in magnetic flux takes place. This change in flux induces an emf across the ends of the rod. Since the rod is falling under gravity, v = gt ( {tex}\because{/tex} u = 0) {tex}\varepsilon=Blv=B l g t{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \varepsilon \propto t{/tex}
solution: A.C. generator principle: A dynamo or generator is a device which converts mechanical energy into electrical energy. It is based on the principle of electromagnetic induction. Working: When the armature coil is rotated in the strong magnetic field, the magnetic flux linked with the coil changes and the current is induced in the coil, its direction being given by Fleming's right hand rule. Considering the armature to be in vertical position and as it rotates in anticlockwise direction, the wire ab moves upward and cd downward, so that the direction of induced current is shown in fig. In the internal circuit, the current flows along B 1 R L B 2 . The direction of current remains unchanged during the first half turn of armature. During the second half revolution, the wire ab moves downward and cd upward, so the direction of current is reversed and in external circuit it flows along B 2 R L B 1 . Thus the direction of induced emf and current changes in the external circuit after each half revolution. Expression of induced emf: If N is the number of turns in coil, f the frequency of rotation, A area of coil and B the magnetic induction, then induced emf {tex}e=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}\{N B A(\cos 2 \pi f t)\}{/tex} {tex}=2 \pi N B A f \sin 2 \pi f t{/tex} Obviously, the emf produced is alternating and hence the current is also alternating. Current produced by an ac generator cannot be measured by moving coil ammeter because the average value of ac over full cycle is zero. The source of energy generation is the mechanical energy of rotation of armature coil. As the earth's magnetic field lines are cut by the falling rod, the change in magnetic flux takes place. This change in flux induces an emf across the ends of the rod. solution_html:
1. A.C. generator principle: A dynamo or generator is a device which converts mechanical energy into electrical energy. It is based on the principle of electromagnetic induction.
    Working: When the armature coil is rotated in the strong magnetic field, the magnetic flux linked with the coil changes and the current is induced in the coil, its direction being given by Fleming's right hand rule. Considering the armature to be in vertical position and as it rotates in anticlockwise direction, the wire ab moves upward and cd downward, so that the direction of induced current is shown in fig. In the internal circuit, the current flows along B1RLB2. The direction of current remains unchanged during the first half turn of armature. During the second half revolution, the wire ab moves downward and cd upward, so the direction of current is reversed and in external circuit it flows along B2RLB1. Thus the direction of induced emf and current changes in the external circuit after each half revolution.

    Expression of induced emf: If N is the number of turns in coil, f the frequency of rotation, A area of coil and B the magnetic induction, then induced emf
    {tex}e=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}\{N B A(\cos 2 \pi f t)\}{/tex}
    {tex}=2 \pi N B A f \sin 2 \pi f t{/tex}
    Obviously, the emf produced is alternating and hence the current is also alternating. Current produced by an ac generator cannot be measured by moving coil ammeter because the average value of ac over full cycle is zero. The source of energy generation is the mechanical energy of rotation of armature coil.
  2. As the earth's magnetic field lines are cut by the falling rod, the change in magnetic flux takes place. This change in flux induces an emf across the ends of the rod.
    Since the rod is falling under gravity,
    v = gt ({tex}\because{/tex} u = 0)
    {tex}\varepsilon=Blv=B l g t{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad \varepsilon \propto t{/tex}
