explanation: Let a current I 2 flows through the outer circular coil of radius R. The magnetic field at the centre of the coil is As r << R, hence field B 2 may be considered to be constant over the entire cross-sectional area of inner coil of radius r. Hence,magnetic flux linked with the smaller coil will be {tex}\phi _ { 1 } = B _ { 2 } A _ { 1 } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 2 } } { 2 R } \cdot \pi r ^ { 2 }{/tex} As, by definition {tex}\phi _ { 1 } = M _ { 12 } I _ { 2 }{/tex} Now mutual inductance, {tex} M _ { 12 }=\phi _1/ I _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi r ^ { 2 } } { 2R }{/tex} Say {tex}M _ { 12 } = M _ { 21 } = M {/tex} , {tex}\therefore M = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi r^ { 2 } } { 2R }{/tex}

Let a current I2 flows through the outer circular coil of radius R. The magnetic field at the centre of the coil is

As r << R, hence field B2 may be considered to be constant over the entire cross-sectional area of inner coil of radius r. Hence,magnetic flux linked with the smaller coil will be
{tex}\phi _ { 1 } = B _ { 2 } A _ { 1 } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } I _ { 2 } } { 2 R } \cdot \pi r ^ { 2 }{/tex}
As, by definition {tex}\phi _ { 1 } = M _ { 12 } I _ { 2 }{/tex}
Now mutual inductance, {tex} M _ { 12 }=\phi _1/ I _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi r ^ { 2 } } { 2R }{/tex}

{tex}\therefore M = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi r^ { 2 } } { 2R }{/tex}

question_html: Current in a circuit falls steadily from 2.0 A to 0.0 A in 10 ms. If an average emf of 200 V is induced, then calculate the self-inductance of the circuit.
question_text: Current in a circuit falls steadily from 2.0 A to 0.0 A in 10 ms. If an average emf of 200 V is induced, then calculate the self-inductance of the circuit.
explanation: Here, {tex}\Delta I = - 2 A{/tex} {tex} \Delta t = 10 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } sec{/tex} {tex}V = 200 \mathrm { V }{/tex} L =? {tex}\because {/tex} Induced emf, {tex}e = - L \frac { \Delta I } { \Delta t }{/tex} {tex}200 = - L \left( \frac { - 2 } { 10 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } \right){/tex} {tex}200 = L \times 2 \times 10 ^ { 2 }{/tex} Thus, L = 1 H

question_text: A rectangular loop and a circular loop are moving out of a uniform magnetic field region with a constant velocity v as shown in the figure. In which loop, do you expect the induced emf to be constant during the passage out of the field region? The field is normal to the loops.
explanation: Magnitude of Induced emf is directly proportional to the rate of area moving out of the field for a constant magnetic field. {tex}\varepsilon = - \frac { d \phi } { d t } = - B \frac { d A } { d t }{/tex} For the rectangular coil, the rate of area moving out of the field remain same while it is not so for the circular coil. question_html:
A current is induced in coil C1 due to the motion of current carrying coil C2.

  1. Write any two ways by which a large deflection can be obtained in the galvanometer G.
  2. Suggest an alternative device to demonstrate the induced current in place of a galvanometer.
question_text: A current is induced in coil C 1 due to the motion of current carrying coil C 2 . Write any two ways by which a large deflection can be obtained in the galvanometer G. Suggest an alternative device to demonstrate the induced current in place of a galvanometer.
explanation: Large deflection means a high current for short time. So, to produce large deflection, induced current should be high and to produce high induced current, rate of change of flux should be high, i.e. more change in magnetic flux in less time. Large deflection in the galvanometer connected with the coil C 1 in given question, can be obtained when change in magnetic flux with the coil is made fast. So, according to the diagram given in question, fast change in magnetic flux in the coil C 1 can be done by moving the coil C 2 quickly, towards C 1 or by moving the coil C 2 quickly away from C 1 by switching off and on the key, more change of magnetic flux linked with the coil C 1 can be done in less time. The induced current can be demonstrated by connecting a torch bulb (in place of galvanometer) in Coil C 1 . Due to induced current the bulb begins to glow.
solution: Large deflection means a high current for short time. So, to produce large deflection, induced current should be high and to produce high induced current, rate of change of flux should be high, i.e. more change in magnetic flux in less time. Large deflection in the galvanometer connected with the coil C 1 in given question, can be obtained when change in magnetic flux with the coil is made fast. So, according to the diagram given in question, fast change in magnetic flux in the coil C 1 can be done by moving the coil C 2 quickly, towards C 1 or by moving the coil C 2 quickly away from C 1 by switching off and on the key, more change of magnetic flux linked with the coil C 1 can be done in less time. The induced current can be demonstrated by connecting a torch bulb (in place of galvanometer) in Coil C 1 . Due to induced current the bulb begins to glow.
solution_html:
1. Large deflection means a high current for short time. So, to produce large deflection, induced current should be high and to produce high induced current, rate of change of flux should be high, i.e. more change in magnetic flux in less time.
    Large deflection in the galvanometer connected with the coil C1 in given question, can be obtained when change in magnetic flux with the coil is made fast. So, according to the diagram given in question,
    1. fast change in magnetic flux in the coil C1 can be done by moving the coil C2 quickly, towards C1 or by moving the coil C2 quickly away from C1
    2. by switching off and on the key, more change of magnetic flux linked with the coil C1 can be done in less time.
  2. The induced current can be demonstrated by connecting a torch bulb (in place of galvanometer) in Coil C1. Due to induced current the bulb begins to glow.

question_html:
A magnet is quickly moved in the direction indicated by an arrow between two coils C1 and C2 as shown in the figure.

What will be the direction of induced current in each coil as seen from the magnet? Justify your answer.
question_text: A magnet is quickly moved in the direction indicated by an arrow between two coils C 1 and C 2 as shown in the figure. What will be the direction of induced current in each coil as seen from the magnet? Justify your answer.
explanation: As we know from Lenz's law of electromagnetic induction that, the direction of current in both the coils will be such as to resist the motion of the magnet, which produces the current in the two coils. As N-pole of the magnet is moving away from the coil C 1 , therefore the coil will behave as South-pole and opposes the motion of the N-pole of the magnet. This implies the current in the coil is anti-clockwise. Also in coil C 2 , it behaves as S-pole in order to repel the coming S-pole of the magnet. This implies current in the coil is anticlockwise.
solution: As we know from Lenz's law of electromagnetic induction that, the direction of current in both the coils will be such as to resist the motion of the magnet, which produces the current in the two coils. As N-pole of the magnet is moving away from the coil C 1 , therefore the coil will behave as South-pole and opposes the motion of the N-pole of the magnet. This implies the current in the coil is anti-clockwise. Also in coil C 2 , it behaves as S-pole in order to repel the coming S-pole of the magnet. This implies current in the coil is anticlockwise.
solution_html:
As we know from Lenz's law of electromagnetic induction that, the direction of current in both the coils will be such as to resist the motion of the magnet, which produces the current in the two coils.
As N-pole of the magnet is moving away from the coil C1, therefore the coil will behave as South-pole and opposes the motion of the N-pole of the magnet. This implies the current in the coil is anti-clockwise.
Also in coil C2, it behaves as S-pole in order to repel the coming S-pole of the magnet. This implies current in the coil is anticlockwise.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction.
  2. A horizontal straight wire of length L extending from east to west is falling with speed v at right angles to the horizontal component of Earth's magnetic field B.
    1. Write the expression for the instantaneous value of the emf induced in the wire.
    2. What is the direction of the emf?
    3. Which end of the wire is at the higher potential?
question_text: State Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction. A horizontal straight wire of length L extending from east to west is falling with speed v at right angles to the horizontal component of Earth's magnetic field B. Write the expression for the instantaneous value of the emf induced in the wire. What is the direction of the emf? Which end of the wire is at the higher potential?
explanation: First Law: Whenever a conductor is placed in a varying magnetic field, an EMF gets induced across the conductor (called as induced emf), and if the conductor is a closed circuit then induced current flows through it. Second Law: The magnitude of induced emf is equal to the rate of change of flux linkages with the coil. Regarding the direction of induced emf, Lenz law suggests that, when an emf is induced according to Faraday's law, the polarity (direction) of that induced emf is such that it opposes the cause of its production. Given: Length of wire = v Velocity with which it is falling = v Magnetic field = B H = B Cos {tex}\theta{/tex} Angle between v & B, {tex}\Phi=90^{\circ}{/tex} As the wire fall downwards due to this motion e - within the wire also moves downwards and feels a force due to Earth's magnetic field towards west {tex}\vec{F}=-e \vec{v} \times \vec{B} \Rightarrow-e v B{/tex} As electrons moves towards west end of the wire they apply opposite or repelling force to new incoming charge. As more and more electron's come, eventually we achieve an equilibrium situation where no more electrons can come towards west side end. At this particular situation. eE = -evB E = -vB V = -EL {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = +vBL e.m.f. setup within in the wire. The direction of the e.m.f. will be the polarity of the rod, which is positive at the east end and negative at the west end. As the electrons get accumulated at the west end it would mean that the west end is at negative potential. Which implies east end is at high potential (non zero potential).
solution: First Law: Whenever a conductor is placed in a varying magnetic field, an EMF gets induced across the conductor (called as induced emf), and if the conductor is a closed circuit then induced current flows through it. Second Law: The magnitude of induced emf is equal to the rate of change of flux linkages with the coil. Regarding the direction of induced emf, Lenz law suggests that, when an emf is induced according to Faraday's law, the polarity (direction) of that induced emf is such that it opposes the cause of its production. Given: Length of wire = v Velocity with which it is falling = v Magnetic field = B H = B Cos {tex}\theta{/tex} Angle between v & B, {tex}\Phi=90^{\circ}{/tex} As the wire fall downwards due to this motion e - within the wire also moves downwards and feels a force due to Earth's magnetic field towards west {tex}\vec{F}=-e \vec{v} \times \vec{B} \Rightarrow-e v B{/tex} As electrons moves towards west end of the wire they apply opposite or repelling force to new incoming charge. As more and more electron's come, eventually we achieve an equilibrium situation where no more electrons can come towards west side end. At this particular situation. eE = -evB E = -vB V = -EL {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = +vBL e.m.f. setup within in the wire. The direction of the e.m.f. will be the polarity of the rod, which is positive at the east end and negative at the west end. As the electrons get accumulated at the west end it would mean that the west end is at negative potential. Which implies east end is at high potential (non zero potential).
solution_html:
1. First Law: Whenever a conductor is placed in a varying magnetic field, an EMF gets induced across the conductor (called as induced emf), and if the conductor is a closed circuit then induced current flows through it.
    Second Law: The magnitude of induced emf is equal to the rate of change of flux linkages with the coil.
    Regarding the direction of induced emf, Lenz law suggests that, when an emf is induced according to Faraday's law, the polarity (direction) of that induced emf is such that it opposes the cause of its production.
  2. Given:
    Length of wire = v
    Velocity with which it is falling = v
    Magnetic field = BH = B Cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
    Angle between v & B, {tex}\Phi=90^{\circ}{/tex}
    1. As the wire fall downwards due to this motion e- within the wire also moves downwards and feels a force due to Earth's magnetic field towards west
      {tex}\vec{F}=-e \vec{v} \times \vec{B} \Rightarrow-e v B{/tex}
      As electrons moves towards west end of the wire they apply opposite or repelling force to new incoming charge. As more and more electron's come, eventually we achieve an
      equilibrium situation where no more electrons can come towards west side end. At this particular situation.
      eE = -evB
      E = -vB
      V = -EL
      {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = +vBL e.m.f. setup within in the wire.
    2. The direction of the e.m.f. will be the polarity of the rod, which is positive at the east end and negative at the west end.
    3. As the electrons get accumulated at the west end it would mean that the west end is at negative potential. Which implies east end is at high potential (non zero potential).

question_html: Two identical loops, one of copper and another of aluminium are rotated with the same speed in the same magnetic field. In which case (i) the induced emf (ii) the induced current, will be more and why?
question_text: Two identical loops, one of copper and another of aluminium are rotated with the same speed in the same magnetic field. In which case (i) the induced emf (ii) the induced current, will be more and why?
explanation: Induced emf will be the same in both the loops because it does not depend on the nature of the material of the loops. Induced current will be more in copper loop because its resistance is less than that of the aluminium loop.
solution: Induced emf will be the same in both the loops because it does not depend on the nature of the material of the loops. Induced current will be more in copper loop because its resistance is less than that of the aluminium loop.
solution_html:
1. Induced emf will be the same in both the loops because it does not depend on the nature of the material of the loops.
  2. Induced current will be more in copper loop because its resistance is less than that of the aluminium loop.

question_text: A plot of magnetic flux, {tex}\phi {/tex} versus current (I) is shown in the figure for two inductors A and B. Which of the two has larger value of self-inductance?
explanation: Self-inductance of the inductor, {tex}L = \frac{\phi}{ I}{/tex} In the above graph, A is showing more steeper graph than B. Hence the slope of the given {tex}\phi {/tex} -I graph gives self-inductance of the coil. Inductor A have got greater slope than that of inductor B, therefore self-inductance of A is greater than self-inductance of B.

Self-inductance of the inductor, {tex}L = \frac{\phi}{ I}{/tex}
In the above graph, A is showing more steeper graph than B. Hence the slope of the given {tex}\phi {/tex}-I graph gives self-inductance of the coil. Inductor A have got greater slope than that of inductor B, therefore self-inductance of A is greater than self-inductance of B.

question_html:
A coil Q is connected to low voltage bulb B and placed near another coil P as shown in the figure. Give reasons to explain the following observations.

  1. The bulb B lights.
  2. Bulb gets dimmer if the coil Q is moved towards left.
question_text: A coil Q is connected to low voltage bulb B and placed near another coil P as shown in the figure. Give reasons to explain the following observations. The bulb B lights. Bulb gets dimmer if the coil Q is moved towards left.
explanation: Induced current, is responsible for the lighting of the bulb, which depends on change of flux. The bulb B lights due to induced current in coil Q because of change in magnetic flux linked with it on a consequence of continuous variation of magnitude of alternating current flowing in P. When coil Q moves towards left the rate of change of magnetic flux linked with Q decreases and so lesser current is induced in Q.
solution: Induced current, is responsible for the lighting of the bulb, which depends on change of flux. The bulb B lights due to induced current in coil Q because of change in magnetic flux linked with it on a consequence of continuous variation of magnitude of alternating current flowing in P. When coil Q moves towards left the rate of change of magnetic flux linked with Q decreases and so lesser current is induced in Q.
solution_html:
1. Induced current, is responsible for the lighting of the bulb, which depends on change of flux. The bulb B lights due to induced current in coil Q because of change in magnetic flux linked with it on a consequence of continuous variation of magnitude of alternating current flowing in P.

  2. When coil Q moves towards left the rate of change of magnetic flux linked with Q decreases and so lesser current is induced in Q.

question_html: A source of emf e is used to establish a current I through a coil of self-inductance L. Show that the work done by the source of build up the current {tex}I \text { is } \frac { 1 } { 2 } L I ^ { 2 }{/tex} .
question_text: A source of emf e is used to establish a current I through a coil of self-inductance L. Show that the work done by the source of build up the current {tex}I \text { is } \frac { 1 } { 2 } L I ^ { 2 }{/tex} .
explanation: Here we will use the concept of work energy theorem. Let, I current flows through the coil of self-inductance L at any instant t when rate of change of current in coil is dl/dt, {tex}\therefore {/tex} Induced emf, E = {tex}- L \frac { d I } { d t }{/tex} Magnitude, |E| = L {tex}\frac {dI}{dt}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Work done in establishing the current in small-time interval dt is given by {tex}d W = P d t = E I d t = \left( L \frac { d l } { d t } \right) I d t{/tex} {tex}d W = L I d I{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Total work done in increasing the current from zero to I. {tex}\therefore W = \int _ { 0 } ^ { I } L I d I = L \int _ { 0 } ^ { I } I d l = L \left[ \frac { I ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { I } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } L \left( I ^ { 2 } - 0 ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}W = \frac { 1 } { 2 } L I ^ { 2 }{/tex} This work is stored as the energy in the inductor.
solution: Here we will use the concept of work energy theorem. Let, I current flows through the coil of self-inductance L at any instant t when rate of change of current in coil is dl/dt, {tex}\therefore {/tex} Induced emf, E = {tex}- L \frac { d I } { d t }{/tex} Magnitude, |E| = L {tex}\frac {dI}{dt}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Work done in establishing the current in small-time interval dt is given by {tex}d W = P d t = E I d t = \left( L \frac { d l } { d t } \right) I d t{/tex} {tex}d W = L I d I{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Total work done in increasing the current from zero to I. {tex}\therefore W = \int _ { 0 } ^ { I } L I d I = L \int _ { 0 } ^ { I } I d l = L \left[ \frac { I ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { I } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } L \left( I ^ { 2 } - 0 ^ { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}W = \frac { 1 } { 2 } L I ^ { 2 }{/tex} This work is stored as the energy in the inductor.
solution_html:
Here we will use the concept of work energy theorem.
Let, I current flows through the coil of self-inductance L at any instant t when rate of change of current in coil is dl/dt,
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Induced emf, E = {tex}- L \frac { d I } { d t }{/tex}
Magnitude, |E| = L{tex}\frac {dI}{dt}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Work done in establishing the current in small-time interval dt is given by
{tex}d W = P d t = E I d t = \left( L \frac { d l } { d t } \right) I d t{/tex}
{tex}d W = L I d I{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Total work done in increasing the current from zero to I.
{tex}\therefore W = \int _ { 0 } ^ { I } L I d I = L \int _ { 0 } ^ { I } I d l = L \left[ \frac { I ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { I } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } L \left( I ^ { 2 } - 0 ^ { 2 } \right){/tex}
{tex}W = \frac { 1 } { 2 } L I ^ { 2 }{/tex}
This work is stored as the energy in the inductor.

question_html: Two loops of different shapes are moved into a region of uniform magnetic field in the directions marked by arrows as shown in the figure. What is the direction of the induced current in each loop?
question_text: Two loops of different shapes are moved into a region of uniform magnetic field in the directions marked by arrows as shown in the figure. What is the direction of the induced current in each loop?
explanation: The induced current always opposes the change in magnetic flux. Loop abc is entering the magnetic field, so magnetic flux linked with loop tends to increase, so current induced in loop abc is anti-clockwise to produce magnetic field upward to oppose the increase in flux. Loop defg is leaving the magnetic field; so flux linked with it tends to decrease, the induced current will be clockwise to produce magnetic field downward to oppose the decrease in magnetic flux.
solution: The induced current always opposes the change in magnetic flux. Loop abc is entering the magnetic field, so magnetic flux linked with loop tends to increase, so current induced in loop abc is anti-clockwise to produce magnetic field upward to oppose the increase in flux. Loop defg is leaving the magnetic field; so flux linked with it tends to decrease, the induced current will be clockwise to produce magnetic field downward to oppose the decrease in magnetic flux.
solution_html:
The induced current always opposes the change in magnetic flux. Loop abc is entering the magnetic field, so magnetic flux linked with loop tends to increase, so current induced in loop abc is anti-clockwise to produce magnetic field upward to oppose the increase in flux.
Loop defg is leaving the magnetic field; so flux linked with it tends to decrease, the induced current will be clockwise to produce magnetic field downward to oppose the decrease in magnetic flux.

The current induces in Cu coil is more than Al coil as Cu coil has less resistance than the Al coil and {tex}I \propto \frac{1}{R}{/tex} (for the same voltage).

question_html:
1. When primary coil P is moved towards secondary coil S (as shown in the figure below), the galvanometer shows momentary deflection. What can be done to have larger deflection in the galvanometer with the same battery?
  2. State the related law.
question_text: When primary coil P is moved towards secondary coil S (as shown in the figure below), the galvanometer shows momentary deflection. What can be done to have larger deflection in the galvanometer with the same battery? State the related law.
explanation: The primary coil P should be moved faster towards the secondary coil S. The related law is Faraday's laws of electromagnetic induction. The results of Faraday's experiments on electromagnetic induction are known as Faraday's laws of electromagnetic induction. These laws are stated as below: Whenever magnetic flux linked with a circuit (a loop of wire or a coil or an electric circuit in general) changes, induced e.m.f. is produced. The induced e.mf. lasts as long as the change in the magnetic flux continues. The magnitude of the induced e.mf. is directly proportional to the rate of change of the magnetic flux linked with the circuit.
solution: The primary coil P should be moved faster towards the secondary coil S. The related law is Faraday's laws of electromagnetic induction. The results of Faraday's experiments on electromagnetic induction are known as Faraday's laws of electromagnetic induction. These laws are stated as below: Whenever magnetic flux linked with a circuit (a loop of wire or a coil or an electric circuit in general) changes, induced e.m.f. is produced. The induced e.mf. lasts as long as the change in the magnetic flux continues. The magnitude of the induced e.mf. is directly proportional to the rate of change of the magnetic flux linked with the circuit.
solution_html:
1. The primary coil P should be moved faster towards the secondary coil S.
  2. The related law is Faraday's laws of electromagnetic induction.
    The results of Faraday's experiments on electromagnetic induction are known as Faraday's laws of electromagnetic induction. These laws are stated as below:
    1. Whenever magnetic flux linked with a circuit (a loop of wire or a coil or an electric circuit in general) changes, induced e.m.f. is produced.
    2. The induced e.mf. lasts as long as the change in the magnetic flux continues.
    3. The magnitude of the induced e.mf. is directly proportional to the rate of change of the magnetic flux linked with the circuit.

question_html:
A rectangular loop and a circular loop are moving out of a uniform magnetic field to a field free region with a constant velocity v as shown in a given figure.

Explain, in which loop do you expect the induced e.m.f. to be constant during the passage out of the region. The magnetic field is normal to the loops.
question_text: A rectangular loop and a circular loop are moving out of a uniform magnetic field to a field free region with a constant velocity v as shown in a given figure. Explain, in which loop do you expect the induced e.m.f. to be constant during the passage out of the region. The magnetic field is normal to the loops.
explanation: As the loops move out of the magnetic field, the rate at which the area of the loop decreases inside the field is constant in case of the rectangular loop, while it varies in the case of the circular loop. As a result, the rate of decrease of magnetic flux and hence the induced e.m.f. produced in the rectangular loop will be constant.
solution: As the loops move out of the magnetic field, the rate at which the area of the loop decreases inside the field is constant in case of the rectangular loop, while it varies in the case of the circular loop. As a result, the rate of decrease of magnetic flux and hence the induced e.m.f. produced in the rectangular loop will be constant.
solution_html: As the loops move out of the magnetic field, the rate at which the area of the loop decreases inside the field is constant in case of the rectangular loop, while it varies in the case of the circular loop. As a result, the rate of decrease of magnetic flux and hence the induced e.m.f. produced in the rectangular loop will be constant.

question_html: An artificial satellite with a metal surface is orbiting the earth around the equator. Will the earth's magnetism induce a current in it?
question_text: An artificial satellite with a metal surface is orbiting the earth around the equator. Will the earth's magnetism induce a current in it?
explanation: The satellite intercepts only the vertical component of the earth's magnetic field. But this component is zero at the equator. So, no emf is induced.
solution: The satellite intercepts only the vertical component of the earth's magnetic field. But this component is zero at the equator. So, no emf is induced.
solution_html: The satellite intercepts only the vertical component of the earth's magnetic field. But this component is zero at the equator. So, no emf is induced.

question_html:
A coil Q is connected to low voltage bulb B and placed near another coil P as shown in Figure. Give reasons to explain the following observations:

  1. The bulb 'B' lights.
  2. Bulb gets dimmer if the coil Q is moved towards left.
question_text: A coil Q is connected to low voltage bulb B and placed near another coil P as shown in Figure. Give reasons to explain the following observations: The bulb 'B' lights. Bulb gets dimmer if the coil Q is moved towards left.
explanation: The alternating current through coil P sets up an alternating magnetic flux around it. This varying flux passes through coil Q and sets up an induced current that lights up the bulb B. As the coil Q is moved towards the left, the rate of change of flux linked with the coil Q decreases. The induced current decreases and bulb B gets dimmer.
solution: The alternating current through coil P sets up an alternating magnetic flux around it. This varying flux passes through coil Q and sets up an induced current that lights up the bulb B. As the coil Q is moved towards the left, the rate of change of flux linked with the coil Q decreases. The induced current decreases and bulb B gets dimmer.
solution_html:
1. The alternating current through coil P sets up an alternating magnetic flux around it. This varying flux passes through coil Q and sets up an induced current that lights up the bulb B.
  2. As the coil Q is moved towards the left, the rate of change of flux linked with the coil Q decreases. The induced current decreases and bulb B gets dimmer.

subject: Physics
question_html: Define self-inductance and give its SI unit. Derive an expression for self-inductance of a long, air-cored solenoid of length l, radius r and having N number of turns.
question_text: Define self-inductance and give its SI unit. Derive an expression for self-inductance of a long, air-cored solenoid of length l, radius r and having N number of turns.
explanation: The self inductance of a coil is equal to the induced emf set up in the coil, when the current passing through it change at the unit rate. The SI unit of self inductance is henry. Self inductance of a long solenoid: Consider a long solenoid of length l number of turns N and radius. Suppose current I flows though it. Magnetic field set up in the coil is B {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{l}{/tex} But {tex}\phi=L i{/tex} {tex}\phi=\frac{N \times \mu_{0} N I}{l}=\frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} I A}{l}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Self inductance {tex}L=\frac{\phi}{I}=\frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{l}{/tex}
solution: The self inductance of a coil is equal to the induced emf set up in the coil, when the current passing through it change at the unit rate. The SI unit of self inductance is henry. Self inductance of a long solenoid: Consider a long solenoid of length l number of turns N and radius. Suppose current I flows though it. Magnetic field set up in the coil is B {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{l}{/tex} But {tex}\phi=L i{/tex} {tex}\phi=\frac{N \times \mu_{0} N I}{l}=\frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} I A}{l}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Self inductance {tex}L=\frac{\phi}{I}=\frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{l}{/tex}
solution_html:
The self inductance of a coil is equal to the induced emf set up in the coil, when the current passing through it change at the unit rate.
The SI unit of self inductance is henry.
Self inductance of a long solenoid:
Consider a long solenoid of length l number of turns N and radius. Suppose current I flows though it. Magnetic field set up in the coil is B {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{l}{/tex}
But {tex}\phi=L i{/tex}
{tex}\phi=\frac{N \times \mu_{0} N I}{l}=\frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} I A}{l}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Self inductance {tex}L=\frac{\phi}{I}=\frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{l}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Deduce an expression for the mutual inductance of two long coaxial solenoids but having different radii and different number of turns.
question_text: Deduce an expression for the mutual inductance of two long coaxial solenoids but having different radii and different number of turns.
explanation: Let coaxial solenoid P is wound over an another solenoid. Let l = length of both solenoid r 1 and r 2 = radii of P and S {tex}A _ { 2 } = \pi r_ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} [area of secondary coil, S] Magnetic induction in solenoid, P {tex}B _ { 1 } = \mu _ { 0 } n _ { 1 } I _ { 1 }{/tex} where, n 1 = {tex}\frac{N_1}{l}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Total flux linked with solenoid S, {tex}\phi _ { 2 } = B _ { 1 } A _ { 2 } N _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad {\phi _2} = {B_1}\left( {\pi r_2^2} \right){N_2} = \left( {{\mu _0}{n_1}{I_1}} \right)\pi r_2^2{N_2}{/tex} {tex}\phi _ { 2 } = \left( \mu _ { 0 } \frac { N _ { 1 } } { l } I _ { 1 } \right) \pi r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } N _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \phi _ { 2 } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi I _ { 1 } N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { l }{/tex} ...(i) But {tex}\phi _ { 2 } = M I _ { 1 }{/tex} were, M = coefficient of mutual induction {tex}\Rightarrow M I _ { 1 } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { l } I _ { 1 } \Rightarrow M = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { l }{/tex}
solution: Let coaxial solenoid P is wound over an another solenoid. Let l = length of both solenoid r 1 and r 2 = radii of P and S {tex}A _ { 2 } = \pi r_ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} [area of secondary coil, S] Magnetic induction in solenoid, P {tex}B _ { 1 } = \mu _ { 0 } n _ { 1 } I _ { 1 }{/tex} where, n 1 = {tex}\frac{N_1}{l}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Total flux linked with solenoid S, {tex}\phi _ { 2 } = B _ { 1 } A _ { 2 } N _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad {\phi _2} = {B_1}\left( {\pi r_2^2} \right){N_2} = \left( {{\mu _0}{n_1}{I_1}} \right)\pi r_2^2{N_2}{/tex} {tex}\phi _ { 2 } = \left( \mu _ { 0 } \frac { N _ { 1 } } { l } I _ { 1 } \right) \pi r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } N _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \phi _ { 2 } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi I _ { 1 } N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { l }{/tex} ...(i) But {tex}\phi _ { 2 } = M I _ { 1 }{/tex} were, M = coefficient of mutual induction {tex}\Rightarrow M I _ { 1 } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { l } I _ { 1 } \Rightarrow M = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { l }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let coaxial solenoid P is wound over an another solenoid.

Let l = length of both solenoid
r1 and r2 = radii of P and S
{tex}A _ { 2 } = \pi r_ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} [area of secondary coil, S]
Magnetic induction in solenoid, P
{tex}B _ { 1 } = \mu _ { 0 } n _ { 1 } I _ { 1 }{/tex}
where, n1  =  {tex}\frac{N_1}{l}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex}Total flux linked with solenoid S,
{tex}\phi _ { 2 } = B _ { 1 } A _ { 2 } N _ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad {\phi _2} = {B_1}\left( {\pi r_2^2} \right){N_2} = \left( {{\mu _0}{n_1}{I_1}} \right)\pi r_2^2{N_2}{/tex}
{tex}\phi _ { 2 } = \left( \mu _ { 0 } \frac { N _ { 1 } } { l } I _ { 1 } \right) \pi r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } N _ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \phi _ { 2 } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi I _ { 1 } N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { l }{/tex} ...(i)
But {tex}\phi _ { 2 } = M I _ { 1 }{/tex}
were, M = coefficient of mutual induction
{tex}\Rightarrow M I _ { 1 } = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { l } I _ { 1 } \Rightarrow M = \frac { \mu _ { 0 } \pi N _ { 1 } N _ { 2 } r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { l }{/tex}

question_html:
Figure shows a rectangular conducting loop PQRS in which arm RS of length I is movable. The loop is kept in a uniform magnetic field B directed downward perpendicular to the plane of the loop. The arm RS is moved with a uniform speed v.

Deduce an expression for

  1. the emf induced across the arm RS  
  2. the external force required to move the arm and
  3. the power dissipated as heat.
question_text: Figure shows a rectangular conducting loop PQRS in which arm RS of length I is movable. The loop is kept in a uniform magnetic field B directed downward perpendicular to the plane of the loop. The arm RS is moved with a uniform speed v. Deduce an expression for the emf induced across the arm RS the external force required to move the arm and the power dissipated as heat.
explanation: Let RS moves with speed v rightward and also RS is at distances x 1 and x 2 from PQ at instants t 1 and t 2 , respectively. Change in flux, {tex}d \phi = \phi _ { 2 } - \phi _ { 1 } = B l \left( x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } \right){/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} magnetic flux, {tex}\phi = \vec {B}. \vec {A} = BA cos 0^0 = Blx{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow {\text{d}}\phi {\text{ = Bldx}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}} = Bl\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = Blv\quad \left[ {\because v = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right]{/tex} If resistance of loop is R, then {tex}I = \frac { v B l } { R }{/tex} Magnetic force = {tex}BIl \;\text {sin } 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}= \left( \frac { v B l } { R } \right) B l = \frac { v B ^ { 2} l ^ { 2 } } { R }{/tex} Now, External force must be equal to magnetic force {tex}\therefore {/tex} External force {tex}= \frac { v B ^ { 2 } l ^ { 2 } } { R }{/tex} As, {tex}P = {I^2}R = {\left( {\frac{{vBl}}{R}} \right)^2} \times R = \frac{{{v^2}{B^2}{l^2}}}{{{R^2}}} \times R{/tex} {tex}\therefore P = \frac { v ^ { 2 } B ^ { 2 } l ^ { 2 } } { R }{/tex}
solution: Let RS moves with speed v rightward and also RS is at distances x 1 and x 2 from PQ at instants t 1 and t 2 , respectively. Change in flux, {tex}d \phi = \phi _ { 2 } - \phi _ { 1 } = B l \left( x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } \right){/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} magnetic flux, {tex}\phi = \vec {B}. \vec {A} = BA cos 0^0 = Blx{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow {\text{d}}\phi {\text{ = Bldx}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}} = Bl\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = Blv\quad \left[ {\because v = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right]{/tex} If resistance of loop is R, then {tex}I = \frac { v B l } { R }{/tex} Magnetic force = {tex}BIl \;\text {sin } 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}= \left( \frac { v B l } { R } \right) B l = \frac { v B ^ { 2} l ^ { 2 } } { R }{/tex} Now, External force must be equal to magnetic force {tex}\therefore {/tex} External force {tex}= \frac { v B ^ { 2 } l ^ { 2 } } { R }{/tex} As, {tex}P = {I^2}R = {\left( {\frac{{vBl}}{R}} \right)^2} \times R = \frac{{{v^2}{B^2}{l^2}}}{{{R^2}}} \times R{/tex} {tex}\therefore P = \frac { v ^ { 2 } B ^ { 2 } l ^ { 2 } } { R }{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Let RS moves with speed v rightward and also RS is at distances x1 and x2 from PQ at instants t1 and t2, respectively.
    Change in flux, {tex}d \phi = \phi _ { 2 } - \phi _ { 1 } = B l \left( x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } \right){/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} magnetic flux, {tex}\phi = \vec {B}. \vec {A} = BA cos 0^0 = Blx{/tex}]
    {tex}\Rightarrow {\text{d}}\phi {\text{ = Bldx}}{/tex}{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}} = Bl\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = Blv\quad \left[ {\because v = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right]{/tex}

    If resistance of loop is R, then {tex}I = \frac { v B l } { R }{/tex}

  2. Magnetic force = {tex}BIl \;\text {sin } 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
    {tex}= \left( \frac { v B l } { R } \right) B l = \frac { v B ^ { 2} l ^ { 2 } } { R }{/tex}
    Now, External force must be equal to magnetic force
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} External force {tex}= \frac { v B ^ { 2 } l ^ { 2 } } { R }{/tex}

  3. As, {tex}P = {I^2}R = {\left( {\frac{{vBl}}{R}} \right)^2} \times R = \frac{{{v^2}{B^2}{l^2}}}{{{R^2}}} \times R{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore P = \frac { v ^ { 2 } B ^ { 2 } l ^ { 2 } } { R }{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Figure given below shows an arrangement by which current flows through the bulb (X) connected with coil B, when a.c. is passed through coil A.
Explain the following observations:

  1. Bulb lights up.
  2. Bulb gets dimmer if the coil 'B' is moved upwards.
  3. If a copper sheet is inserted in the gap between the coils how the brightness of the bulb would change?
question_text: Figure given below shows an arrangement by which current flows through the bulb (X) connected with coil B, when a.c. is passed through coil A. Explain the following observations: Bulb lights up. Bulb gets dimmer if the coil 'B' is moved upwards. If a copper sheet is inserted in the gap between the coils how the brightness of the bulb would change?
explanation: Bulb lights up due to the induced current set up in coil B because of alternating current in coil A. Bulb gets dimmer when coil B is moved upwards because the flux linked with coil B decreases and induced current also decreases. When the copper sheet is inserted, eddy currents are set up in it which oppose the passage of magnetic flux. The induced emf in coil B decreases. This decreases the brightness of the bulb.
solution: Bulb lights up due to the induced current set up in coil B because of alternating current in coil A. Bulb gets dimmer when coil B is moved upwards because the flux linked with coil B decreases and induced current also decreases. When the copper sheet is inserted, eddy currents are set up in it which oppose the passage of magnetic flux. The induced emf in coil B decreases. This decreases the brightness of the bulb.
solution_html:
1. Bulb lights up due to the induced current set up in coil B because of alternating current in coil A.
  2. Bulb gets dimmer when coil B is moved upwards because the flux linked with coil B decreases and induced current also decreases.
  3. When the copper sheet is inserted, eddy currents are set up in it which oppose the passage of magnetic flux. The induced emf in coil B decreases. This decreases the brightness of the bulb.

subject: Physics
question_html: A jet plane is traveling west at 450 m/s. If the horizontal component of earth's magnetic field at that place is {tex}4 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex} tesla and the angle of dip is 30°, find the emf induced between the ends of wings having a span of 30 m.
question_text: A jet plane is traveling west at 450 m/s. If the horizontal component of earth's magnetic field at that place is {tex}4 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex} tesla and the angle of dip is 30°, find the emf induced between the ends of wings having a span of 30 m.
explanation: Here, l = 30 m, v = 450 ms -1 , {tex}\delta=30^{\circ}{/tex} , B H = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 tesla So, B V = B H tan {tex}\delta{/tex} {tex}=4 \times 10^{-4} \tan 30^{\circ}{/tex} {tex} =4 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt 3}{/tex} Now emf, {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = B V lv {tex}=4 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} {tex}\times {30 \times 450}{/tex} = 3.12 volt
solution: Here, l = 30 m, v = 450 ms -1 , {tex}\delta=30^{\circ}{/tex} , B H = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 tesla So, B V = B H tan {tex}\delta{/tex} {tex}=4 \times 10^{-4} \tan 30^{\circ}{/tex} {tex} =4 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt 3}{/tex} Now emf, {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = B V lv {tex}=4 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} {tex}\times {30 \times 450}{/tex} = 3.12 volt
solution_html:
Here, l = 30 m, v = 450 ms-1, {tex}\delta=30^{\circ}{/tex}, BH = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 tesla
So, BV = BH tan{tex}\delta{/tex}
{tex}=4 \times 10^{-4} \tan 30^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex} =4 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt 3}{/tex}
Now emf, {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = BVlv
{tex}=4 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}{tex}\times {30 \times 450}{/tex}
= 3.12 volt

question_html: A large circular coil, of radius R, and a small circular coil, of radius r are put in vicinity of each other. If the coefficient of mutual induction, for this pair, equals 1 mH, what would be the flux linked with the large coil when a current of 0.5 A flows through the small coil?
question_text: A large circular coil, of radius R, and a small circular coil, of radius r are put in vicinity of each other. If the coefficient of mutual induction, for this pair, equals 1 mH, what would be the flux linked with the large coil when a current of 0.5 A flows through the small coil?
explanation: Here, M = 1 mH {tex} = 1 \times {10^{ - 3}}H{/tex} and I = 0.5 A {tex}\therefore \phi = MI {/tex} {tex} = 1 \times {10^{ - 3}}\times 0.5{/tex} = {tex}5 \times {10^{ - 4}}Wb{/tex}
solution: Here, M = 1 mH {tex} = 1 \times {10^{ - 3}}H{/tex} and I = 0.5 A {tex}\therefore \phi = MI {/tex} {tex} = 1 \times {10^{ - 3}}\times 0.5{/tex} = {tex}5 \times {10^{ - 4}}Wb{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, M = 1 mH {tex} = 1 \times {10^{ - 3}}H{/tex}
and I = 0.5 A
{tex}\therefore \phi = MI {/tex} {tex} = 1 \times {10^{ - 3}}\times 0.5{/tex} = {tex}5 \times {10^{ - 4}}Wb{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: When current in a coil changes with time, how is the back emf induced in the coil related to it?
question_text: When current in a coil changes with time, how is the back emf induced in the coil related to it?
explanation: When the current changes, the flux through the loop of coil also changes. A back emf is induced proportional to the rate of change in flux. By Faraday's law, e = - dφ/dt The direction of back emf is such that it will try to oppose the change in current (Lenz' law). This follows from the conservation of energy.
solution: When the current changes, the flux through the loop of coil also changes. A back emf is induced proportional to the rate of change in flux. By Faraday's law, e = - dφ/dt The direction of back emf is such that it will try to oppose the change in current (Lenz' law). This follows from the conservation of energy.
solution_html:
When the current changes, the flux through the loop of coil also changes. A back emf is induced proportional to the rate of change in flux.
By Faraday's law,
e = - dφ/dt
The direction of back emf is such that it will try to oppose the change in current (Lenz' law). This follows from the conservation of energy.

subject: Physics
question_html: A metallic rod of length L is rotated at an angular speed co normal to a uniform magnetic field B. Derive expressions for the (i) emf induced in the rod (ii) current induced and (iii) heat dissipation if the resistance of the rod is R.
question_text: A metallic rod of length L is rotated at an angular speed co normal to a uniform magnetic field B. Derive expressions for the (i) emf induced in the rod (ii) current induced and (iii) heat dissipation if the resistance of the rod is R.
explanation: Suppose the rod completes one revolution in time T. Then change in flux = B {tex}\times{/tex} Area swept {tex}=B \times \pi L^{2}{/tex} Induced emf {tex}=\frac{\text { Change in flux }}{\text { Time }}{/tex} or {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{B \times \pi L^{2}}{T}=B \pi L^{2} f{/tex} {tex}\left[\because T=\frac{1}{f}\right]{/tex} As {tex}f=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi}{/tex} , therefore {tex}\varepsilon=B \pi L^{2} \cdot \frac{\omega}{2 \pi}=\frac{1}{2} B L^{2} \omega{/tex} Induced current, {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B L^{2} \omega}{R}{/tex} Heat dissipation in time t, {tex}Q=\frac{\varepsilon^{2} t}{R}=\frac{1}{4} \frac{B^{2} L^{4} \omega^{2} t}{R}{/tex}
solution: Suppose the rod completes one revolution in time T. Then change in flux = B {tex}\times{/tex} Area swept {tex}=B \times \pi L^{2}{/tex} Induced emf {tex}=\frac{\text { Change in flux }}{\text { Time }}{/tex} or {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{B \times \pi L^{2}}{T}=B \pi L^{2} f{/tex} {tex}\left[\because T=\frac{1}{f}\right]{/tex} As {tex}f=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi}{/tex} , therefore {tex}\varepsilon=B \pi L^{2} \cdot \frac{\omega}{2 \pi}=\frac{1}{2} B L^{2} \omega{/tex} Induced current, {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B L^{2} \omega}{R}{/tex} Heat dissipation in time t, {tex}Q=\frac{\varepsilon^{2} t}{R}=\frac{1}{4} \frac{B^{2} L^{4} \omega^{2} t}{R}{/tex}
solution_html:
Suppose the rod completes one revolution in time T. Then change in flux
= B {tex}\times{/tex} Area swept {tex}=B \times \pi L^{2}{/tex}
Induced emf {tex}=\frac{\text { Change in flux }}{\text { Time }}{/tex}

or {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{B \times \pi L^{2}}{T}=B \pi L^{2} f{/tex} {tex}\left[\because T=\frac{1}{f}\right]{/tex}
As {tex}f=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi}{/tex}, therefore
{tex}\varepsilon=B \pi L^{2} \cdot \frac{\omega}{2 \pi}=\frac{1}{2} B L^{2} \omega{/tex}
Induced current,
{tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B L^{2} \omega}{R}{/tex}
Heat dissipation in time t,
{tex}Q=\frac{\varepsilon^{2} t}{R}=\frac{1}{4} \frac{B^{2} L^{4} \omega^{2} t}{R}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
The circuit arrangement given in the figure shows that when an a.c. passes through coil A, the current starts flowing in coil B.

  1. Name the underlying principle involved.
  2. Mention two factors on which the current produced in the coil B depends.
question_text: The circuit arrangement given in the figure shows that when an a.c. passes through coil A, the current starts flowing in coil B. Name the underlying principle involved. Mention two factors on which the current produced in the coil B depends.
explanation: The phenomenon is called mutual induction. The current induced in coil B depends on the mutual inductance of two coils. the rate of change of current in coil A. the resistance of the coil B.
solution: The phenomenon is called mutual induction. The current induced in coil B depends on the mutual inductance of two coils. the rate of change of current in coil A. the resistance of the coil B.
solution_html:
1. The phenomenon is called mutual induction.
  2. The current induced in coil B depends on
    1. the mutual inductance of two coils.
    2. the rate of change of current in coil A.
    3. the resistance of the coil B.

subject: Physics
question_html: Obtain the expression for the mutual inductance of a pair of coaxial circular coils of radii r and R (R >> r) placed with their centres coinciding.
question_text: Obtain the expression for the mutual inductance of a pair of coaxial circular coils of radii r and R (R >> r) placed with their centres coinciding.
explanation: The magnetic field produced by current carrying larger coil C 1 in the vicinity of small coil C 2 is {tex}B_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2 R}{/tex} The magnetic flux linked with shorter coil C 2 is {tex}\phi_{2}=B_{1} A_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2 R} \pi r^{2}{/tex} Mutual Inductance {tex}M=\frac{\phi_{2}}{I_{1}}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi r^{2}}{2 R}{/tex} hency.
solution: The magnetic field produced by current carrying larger coil C 1 in the vicinity of small coil C 2 is {tex}B_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2 R}{/tex} The magnetic flux linked with shorter coil C 2 is {tex}\phi_{2}=B_{1} A_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2 R} \pi r^{2}{/tex} Mutual Inductance {tex}M=\frac{\phi_{2}}{I_{1}}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi r^{2}}{2 R}{/tex} hency.
solution_html:
The magnetic field produced by current carrying larger coil C1 in

the vicinity of small coil C2 is {tex}B_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2 R}{/tex}
The magnetic flux linked with shorter coil C2 is
{tex}\phi_{2}=B_{1} A_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2 R} \pi r^{2}{/tex}
Mutual Inductance {tex}M=\frac{\phi_{2}}{I_{1}}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi r^{2}}{2 R}{/tex} hency.

subject: Physics
question_html: Figure shows a conducting rod PQ in contact with metal rails RP and SQ which are 25 cm apart in a uniform magnetic field of flux density 0.4 T acting perpendicular to the plane of the paper. Ends R and S are connected through a 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance. What is the emf when the rod moves to the right with a velocity of 5 ms-1? What is the magnitude and direction of the current through 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance? If the rod moves to the left with the same speed, what will be the new current and its direction?
question_text: Figure shows a conducting rod PQ in contact with metal rails RP and SQ which are 25 cm apart in a uniform magnetic field of flux density 0.4 T acting perpendicular to the plane of the paper. Ends R and S are connected through a 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance. What is the emf when the rod moves to the right with a velocity of 5 ms -1 ? What is the magnitude and direction of the current through 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance? If the rod moves to the left with the same speed, what will be the new current and its direction?
explanation: Here B = 0.4T, v = 5 ms -1 , l = 25 cm = 0.25 m Induced emf, {tex}\varepsilon=B l v=0.4 \times 0.25 \times 5=0.5 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} Current {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{0.5 \mathrm{~V}}{5 \Omega}=0.1 \mathrm{~A}{/tex} Applying Fleming right hand rule, the induced current flows from Q to P, i.e., from the end R to S through the 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance. If the rod moves to the left with the same speed, then the current of 0.1 A will flow through 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance from the end S to R.
solution: Here B = 0.4T, v = 5 ms -1 , l = 25 cm = 0.25 m Induced emf, {tex}\varepsilon=B l v=0.4 \times 0.25 \times 5=0.5 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} Current {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{0.5 \mathrm{~V}}{5 \Omega}=0.1 \mathrm{~A}{/tex} Applying Fleming right hand rule, the induced current flows from Q to P, i.e., from the end R to S through the 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance. If the rod moves to the left with the same speed, then the current of 0.1 A will flow through 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance from the end S to R.
solution_html:
Here B = 0.4T, v = 5 ms-1, l = 25 cm = 0.25 m
Induced emf, {tex}\varepsilon=B l v=0.4 \times 0.25 \times 5=0.5 \mathrm{~V}{/tex}
Current {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{0.5 \mathrm{~V}}{5 \Omega}=0.1 \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
Applying Fleming right hand rule, the induced current flows from Q to P, i.e., from the end R to S through the 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance.
If the rod moves to the left with the same speed, then the current of 0.1 A will flow through 5{tex}\Omega{/tex} resistance from the end S to R.

subject: Physics
question_html:
How does the mutual inductance of a pair of coils change when:

  1. the distance between the coils is increased?
  2. the number of turns in each coil is decreased?
  3. a thin iron sheet is placed between the two coils, other factors remaining the same?

Justify your answer in each case.
question_text: How does the mutual inductance of a pair of coils change when: the distance between the coils is increased? the number of turns in each coil is decreased? a thin iron sheet is placed between the two coils, other factors remaining the same? Justify your answer in each case.
explanation: The mutual inductance of two coils, decreases when the distance between them is increased. This is because the flux passing from one coil to another decreases. Mutual inductance, {tex}M=\frac{\mu_{0} N_{1} N_{2} A}{l}{/tex} i.e., {tex}M \propto N_{1} N_{2}{/tex} Clearly, when the number of turns N 1 and N 2 in the two coils is decreased, the mutual inductance decreases. When an iron sheet is placed between the two coils the mutual inductance increases, because M {tex}\propto{/tex} permeability ( {tex}\mu{/tex} ).
solution: The mutual inductance of two coils, decreases when the distance between them is increased. This is because the flux passing from one coil to another decreases. Mutual inductance, {tex}M=\frac{\mu_{0} N_{1} N_{2} A}{l}{/tex} i.e., {tex}M \propto N_{1} N_{2}{/tex} Clearly, when the number of turns N 1 and N 2 in the two coils is decreased, the mutual inductance decreases. When an iron sheet is placed between the two coils the mutual inductance increases, because M {tex}\propto{/tex} permeability ( {tex}\mu{/tex} ).
solution_html:
1. The mutual inductance of two coils, decreases when the distance between them is increased. This is because the flux passing from one coil to another decreases.
  2. Mutual inductance, {tex}M=\frac{\mu_{0} N_{1} N_{2} A}{l}{/tex}
    i.e., {tex}M \propto N_{1} N_{2}{/tex}
    Clearly, when the number of turns N1 and N2 in the two coils is decreased, the mutual inductance decreases.
  3. When an iron sheet is placed between the two coils the mutual inductance increases, because
    M {tex}\propto{/tex} permeability ({tex}\mu{/tex}).

How is mutual inductance of a pair of coils affected when:

  1. separation between the coils is increased
  2. the number of turns of each coil is increased

question_text: How is mutual inductance of a pair of coils affected when: separation between the coils is increased the number of turns of each coil is increased a thin iron sheet is placed between the two coils, other factors remaining the same.
explanation: When separation between the coils is increased, magnetic flux linked with secondary coil decreases. Therefore, mutual inductance (M) of pair of coils decreases. When number of turns of each coil is increased, M increases because {tex}M = \frac{{{\mu _0}{N_1}{N_2}A}}{l}{/tex} As {tex}M \propto {\mu _r}{/tex} therefore, mutual inductance will increase on placing a thin iron sheet between the two coils.
solution: When separation between the coils is increased, magnetic flux linked with secondary coil decreases. Therefore, mutual inductance (M) of pair of coils decreases. When number of turns of each coil is increased, M increases because {tex}M = \frac{{{\mu _0}{N_1}{N_2}A}}{l}{/tex} As {tex}M \propto {\mu _r}{/tex} therefore, mutual inductance will increase on placing a thin iron sheet between the two coils.
solution_html:
1. When separation between the coils is increased, magnetic flux linked with secondary coil decreases. Therefore, mutual inductance (M) of pair of coils decreases.
  2. When number of turns of each coil is increased, M increases because
    {tex}M = \frac{{{\mu _0}{N_1}{N_2}A}}{l}{/tex}
  3. As {tex}M \propto {\mu _r}{/tex} therefore, mutual inductance will increase on placing a thin iron sheet between the two coils.

question_html: Name and define the unit used for measuring the coefficient of mutual inductance. State the relation of this unit with the units of magnetic flux and electric current.
question_text: Name and define the unit used for measuring the coefficient of mutual inductance. State the relation of this unit with the units of magnetic flux and electric current.
explanation: The SI unit of mutual inductance is henry. The mutual inductance of two coils is said to one henry if an induced emf of 1 volt is set in one coil when the current in the neighbouring coil changes at the rate of 1 A/s. As {tex}\phi=M I{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad M=\frac{\phi}{I}{/tex} 1 henry = 1 Wb A -1
solution: The SI unit of mutual inductance is henry. The mutual inductance of two coils is said to one henry if an induced emf of 1 volt is set in one coil when the current in the neighbouring coil changes at the rate of 1 A/s. As {tex}\phi=M I{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad M=\frac{\phi}{I}{/tex} 1 henry = 1 Wb A -1
solution_html:
The SI unit of mutual inductance is henry. The mutual inductance of two coils is said to one henry if an induced emf of 1 volt is set in one coil when the current in the neighbouring coil changes at the rate of 1 A/s.
As {tex}\phi=M I{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad M=\frac{\phi}{I}{/tex}
1 henry = 1 Wb A-1

question_html: Define the term self-inductance. Give its unit. Write an expression for the energy stored in an inductor when a steady current I is passed through it. Is this energy electric or magnetic?
question_text: Define the term self-inductance. Give its unit. Write an expression for the energy stored in an inductor when a steady current I is passed through it. Is this energy electric or magnetic?
explanation: The self-inductance of a coil is defined as the induced emf set up in the coil when the current through it changes at the unit rate. Henry (symbol H) is the SI derived unit of self-inductance. When a current I flows through an inductor of inductance L, the energy stored in it is = {tex}\frac12{/tex} IL 2 This energy is stored as magnetic energy.
solution: The self-inductance of a coil is defined as the induced emf set up in the coil when the current through it changes at the unit rate. Henry (symbol H) is the SI derived unit of self-inductance. When a current I flows through an inductor of inductance L, the energy stored in it is = {tex}\frac12{/tex} IL 2 This energy is stored as magnetic energy.
solution_html:
The self-inductance of a coil is defined as the induced emf set up in the coil when the current through it changes at the unit rate. Henry (symbol H) is the SI derived unit of self-inductance.
When a current I flows through an inductor of inductance L, the energy stored in it is = {tex}\frac12{/tex}IL2
This energy is stored as magnetic energy.

question_html: A solenoid with an iron core and a bulb are connected to a d.c. source. How does the brightness of the bulb change, when the iron core is removed from the solenoid?
question_text: A solenoid with an iron core and a bulb are connected to a d.c. source. How does the brightness of the bulb change, when the iron core is removed from the solenoid?
explanation: When the iron core is removed from the solenoid, the magnetic flux linked with the coil changes (decreases). As a result, induced e.m.f. is produced in the coil, which according to Lenz's law, opposes the removal of the iron core from the solenoid. The opposing induced e.m.f. so produced decreases the steady current in the solenoid. As lesser current flows through the solenoid, the brightness of the bulb decreases.
solution: When the iron core is removed from the solenoid, the magnetic flux linked with the coil changes (decreases). As a result, induced e.m.f. is produced in the coil, which according to Lenz's law, opposes the removal of the iron core from the solenoid. The opposing induced e.m.f. so produced decreases the steady current in the solenoid. As lesser current flows through the solenoid, the brightness of the bulb decreases.
solution_html: When the iron core is removed from the solenoid, the magnetic flux linked with the coil changes (decreases). As a result, induced e.m.f. is produced in the coil, which according to Lenz's law, opposes the removal of the iron core from the solenoid. The opposing induced e.m.f. so produced decreases the steady current in the solenoid. As lesser current flows through the solenoid, the brightness of the bulb decreases.

subject: Physics
question_html:
How does the self inductance of an air core coil change, when (i) the number of turns in the coil is decreased, (ii) an iron rod is introduced in the coil?
A copper coil L wound on a soft iron core and a lamp B are connected to a battery E through a tapping key K. When the key is closed, the lamp glows dimly. But when the key is suddenly opened, the lamp flashes for an instant to much greater brightness. Explain.
question_text: How does the self inductance of an air core coil change, when (i) the number of turns in the coil is decreased, (ii) an iron rod is introduced in the coil? A copper coil L wound on a soft iron core and a lamp B are connected to a battery E through a tapping key K. When the key is closed, the lamp glows dimly. But when the key is suddenly opened, the lamp flashes for an instant to much greater brightness. Explain.
explanation: Self-inductaqnce of an air-core solenoid, {tex}L=\frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{l}{/tex} When the number of turns $N$ in the coil is decreased, the self-inductance decreases. When iron core of relative permeability $\mu_{r}$ is introduced, self-inductance becomes {tex}L^{\prime}=\mu_{r} L{/tex} Self-inductance increases {tex}\mu_{r}{/tex} times. The resistance of copper is much smaller than that of the lamp. When the key is closed, most of the battery current flows through the copper coil and the remaining small current flows through the lamp. So the lamp glows dim. When the key is opened, very large induced emf is set up in the coil which tends to maintain the current through the lamp. Hence the lamp flashes for a moment to a much greater brightness.
solution: Self-inductaqnce of an air-core solenoid, {tex}L=\frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{l}{/tex} When the number of turns $N$ in the coil is decreased, the self-inductance decreases. When iron core of relative permeability $\mu_{r}$ is introduced, self-inductance becomes {tex}L^{\prime}=\mu_{r} L{/tex} Self-inductance increases {tex}\mu_{r}{/tex} times. The resistance of copper is much smaller than that of the lamp. When the key is closed, most of the battery current flows through the copper coil and the remaining small current flows through the lamp. So the lamp glows dim. When the key is opened, very large induced emf is set up in the coil which tends to maintain the current through the lamp. Hence the lamp flashes for a moment to a much greater brightness.
solution_html:
Self-inductaqnce of an air-core solenoid,
{tex}L=\frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{l}{/tex}

  1. When the number of turns $N$ in the coil is decreased, the self-inductance decreases.
  2. When iron core of relative permeability $\mu_{r}$ is introduced, self-inductance becomes
    {tex}L^{\prime}=\mu_{r} L{/tex}
    Self-inductance increases {tex}\mu_{r}{/tex} times.

The resistance of copper is much smaller than that of the lamp. When the key is closed, most of the battery current flows through the copper coil and the remaining small current flows through the lamp. So the lamp glows dim.
When the key is opened, very large induced emf is set up in the coil which tends to maintain the current through the lamp. Hence the lamp flashes for a moment to a much greater brightness.

question_text: A circular copper disc 10 cm in radius rotates at 20 {tex}\pi{/tex} rad/s about an axis through its centre and perpendicular to the disc. A uniform magnetic field of 0.2 T acts perpendicular to the disc. Calculate the potential difference developed between the axis of the disc and the rim. explanation: Here R = 10 cm = 0.10 m, {tex}\omega{/tex} = 20 {tex}\pi{/tex} rad s -1 , B = 0.2 T P.D. developed between the axis and the rim {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{1}{2} B R^{2} \omega=\frac{1}{2} \times 0.2 \times(0.10)^{2} 20 \pi{/tex} = 0.0628 V Induced current, {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{0.0628}{2}{/tex} = 0.0314 A
solution: Here R = 10 cm = 0.10 m, {tex}\omega{/tex} = 20 {tex}\pi{/tex} rad s -1 , B = 0.2 T P.D. developed between the axis and the rim {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{1}{2} B R^{2} \omega=\frac{1}{2} \times 0.2 \times(0.10)^{2} 20 \pi{/tex} = 0.0628 V Induced current, {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{0.0628}{2}{/tex} = 0.0314 A
solution_html:

question_html: A rectangular coil of N turns and the area of cross-section A, is held in a time-varying magnetic field given by B = B0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t, with the plane of the coil normal to the magnetic field. Deduce an expression for the emf induced in the coil.
question_text: A rectangular coil of N turns and the area of cross-section A, is held in a time-varying magnetic field given by B = B 0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t, with the plane of the coil normal to the magnetic field. Deduce an expression for the emf induced in the coil.
explanation: Here B = B 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \phi=N B A=N B_{0} A \sin \omega t{/tex} Induced emf will be {tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=-\frac{d}{d t}\left(N B_{0} A \sin \omega t\right){/tex} where {tex}\varepsilon_{0}=N B_{0} A \omega{/tex} = maximum value of induced emf.
solution: Here B = B 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \phi=N B A=N B_{0} A \sin \omega t{/tex} Induced emf will be {tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=-\frac{d}{d t}\left(N B_{0} A \sin \omega t\right){/tex} where {tex}\varepsilon_{0}=N B_{0} A \omega{/tex} = maximum value of induced emf.
solution_html:
Here B = B0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \phi=N B A=N B_{0} A \sin \omega t{/tex}
Induced emf will be
{tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=-\frac{d}{d t}\left(N B_{0} A \sin \omega t\right){/tex}
where {tex}\varepsilon_{0}=N B_{0} A \omega{/tex} = maximum value of induced emf.

question_text: In which of the cases shown in Figure, will the mutual inductance of two coils be (i) maximum and (ii) minimum, if the number of turns remains the same in each case?
explanation: Mutual inductance is maximum in case (a) because whole of the flux of primary is linked with the secondary. Mutual inductance is minimum in case (c) because the flux due to the primary linked with the secondary is zero when the axes of the two coils are perpendicular to each other.
solution: Mutual inductance is maximum in case (a) because whole of the flux of primary is linked with the secondary. Mutual inductance is minimum in case (c) because the flux due to the primary linked with the secondary is zero when the axes of the two coils are perpendicular to each other.

  2. Mutual inductance is minimum in case (c) because the flux due to the primary linked with the secondary is zero when the axes of the two coils are perpendicular to each other.

{tex}=5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{H}{/tex} = 5 mH

What is the instantaneous value of induced emf when the plane of the coil makes an angle of 60 o with the magnetic lines.
explanation: Consider a conducting coil of N turns and area S rotating with a uniform angular velocity ω about an axis in the plane of the coil, and perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of induction B. The frequency of rotation of the coil is, f = {tex}\frac{\omega}{2\pi}{/tex} Magnetic flux through the loop in the given position is given by: {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\vec{B} \cdot \vec{S}{/tex} = BS cos {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right){/tex} = BS sin ( {tex}\theta{/tex} ) We have, {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\theta{/tex} 0 + {tex}\omega{/tex} t Therefore, {tex}\phi{/tex} = BS sin ( {tex}\theta{/tex} 0 + {tex}\omega{/tex} ) Induced emf is given by, {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = -N {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} Pitting {tex}\phi{/tex} = BS sin ( {tex}\theta{/tex} 0 + {tex}\omega{/tex} t) {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = -N {tex}\frac{d\left(\phi=B S \sin \left(\theta_o+\omega t\right)\right)}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = -NBS {tex}\omega{/tex} cos ( {tex}\theta{/tex} 0 + {tex}\omega{/tex} t) Therefore, the e.m.f induced in coil rotating in uniform magnetic field is given by {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = −NBS {tex}\omega{/tex} cos( {tex}\theta{/tex} 0 + {tex}\omega{/tex} t). Where {tex}\theta{/tex} is angle made by area vector with the magnetic field at any particular instant of time. When the coil is rotated from 0 o , the emf keeps increasing until the coil is parallel to the field and then keeps decreasing until it turns 180 o . Then on further rotating the value of emf increases but the direction is reversed until it is again parallel to the field. On further rotating to 360 o it reaches zero. The instantaneous value of induced emf, E = E 0 sin (90 o − 60 o ) = E 0 sin 30 o = {tex}\frac{E_0}{2}{/tex}
solution: Consider a conducting coil of N turns and area S rotating with a uniform angular velocity ω about an axis in the plane of the coil, and perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of induction B. The frequency of rotation of the coil is, f = {tex}\frac{\omega}{2\pi}{/tex} Magnetic flux through the loop in the given position is given by: {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\vec{B} \cdot \vec{S}{/tex} = BS cos {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right){/tex} = BS sin ( {tex}\theta{/tex} ) We have, {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\theta{/tex} 0 + {tex}\omega{/tex} t Therefore, {tex}\phi{/tex} = BS sin ( {tex}\theta{/tex} 0 + {tex}\omega{/tex} ) Induced emf is given by, {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = -N {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} Pitting {tex}\phi{/tex} = BS sin ( {tex}\theta{/tex} 0 + {tex}\omega{/tex} t) {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = -N {tex}\frac{d\left(\phi=B S \sin \left(\theta_o+\omega t\right)\right)}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = -NBS {tex}\omega{/tex} cos ( {tex}\theta{/tex} 0 + {tex}\omega{/tex} t) Therefore, the e.m.f induced in coil rotating in uniform magnetic field is given by {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = −NBS {tex}\omega{/tex} cos( {tex}\theta{/tex} 0 + {tex}\omega{/tex} t). Where {tex}\theta{/tex} is angle made by area vector with the magnetic field at any particular instant of time. When the coil is rotated from 0 o , the emf keeps increasing until the coil is parallel to the field and then keeps decreasing until it turns 180 o . Then on further rotating the value of emf increases but the direction is reversed until it is again parallel to the field. On further rotating to 360 o it reaches zero. The instantaneous value of induced emf, E = E 0 sin (90 o − 60 o ) = E 0 sin 30 o = {tex}\frac{E_0}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Consider a conducting coil of N turns and area S rotating with a uniform angular velocity ω about an axis in the plane of the coil, and perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of induction B.

The frequency of rotation of the coil is, f = {tex}\frac{\omega}{2\pi}{/tex} Magnetic flux through the loop in the given position is given by:
{tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\vec{B} \cdot \vec{S}{/tex}
= BS cos {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right){/tex}
= BS sin ({tex}\theta{/tex})
We have, {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\theta{/tex}0 + {tex}\omega{/tex}t
Therefore, {tex}\phi{/tex} = BS sin ({tex}\theta{/tex}0 + {tex}\omega{/tex})
Induced emf is given by,
{tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = -N {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex}
Pitting {tex}\phi{/tex} = BS sin ({tex}\theta{/tex}0 + {tex}\omega{/tex}t)
{tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = -N{tex}\frac{d\left(\phi=B S \sin \left(\theta_o+\omega t\right)\right)}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = -NBS {tex}\omega{/tex}cos ({tex}\theta{/tex}0 + {tex}\omega{/tex}t)
Therefore, the e.m.f induced in coil rotating in uniform magnetic field is given by {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = −NBS{tex}\omega{/tex} cos({tex}\theta{/tex}0 + {tex}\omega{/tex}t).
Where {tex}\theta{/tex} is angle made by area vector with the magnetic field at any particular instant of time.
When the coil is rotated from 0o, the emf keeps increasing until the coil is parallel to the field and then keeps decreasing until it turns 180o. Then on further rotating the value of emf increases but the direction is reversed until it is again parallel to the field. On further rotating to 360o it reaches zero.
The instantaneous value of induced emf,
E = E0 sin (90o − 60o)
= E0 sin 30o = {tex}\frac{E_0}{2}{/tex}

question_html: A 70 turn coil with average diameter of 0.02 m is placed perpendicular to magnetic field of 9000 T. If the magnetic field is changed to 6000 T in 3 s, what is the magnitude of the induced emf?
question_text: A 70 turn coil with average diameter of 0.02 m is placed perpendicular to magnetic field of 9000 T. If the magnetic field is changed to 6000 T in 3 s, what is the magnitude of the induced emf?
explanation: Number of turns in the coil N = 70 The diameter of coil d = 0.02 m {tex}\therefore{/tex} The radius of coil r = {tex}\frac{d}{2}{/tex} = 0.01 m Initial magnetic field B 1 = 900 OT Final magnetic field B 2 = 600 OT Time duration t = 3s Emf induced in the coil, = -N {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 B 2 - B 1 = -70 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 0.012 {tex}\times{/tex} (6000 - 9000)3 = 22 V {tex}\therefore{/tex} Emf induced in the coil = 22
solution: Number of turns in the coil N = 70 The diameter of coil d = 0.02 m {tex}\therefore{/tex} The radius of coil r = {tex}\frac{d}{2}{/tex} = 0.01 m Initial magnetic field B 1 = 900 OT Final magnetic field B 2 = 600 OT Time duration t = 3s Emf induced in the coil, = -N {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 B 2 - B 1 = -70 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 0.012 {tex}\times{/tex} (6000 - 9000)3 = 22 V {tex}\therefore{/tex} Emf induced in the coil = 22
solution_html:
Number of turns in the coil N = 70
The diameter of coil d = 0.02 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The radius of coil r = {tex}\frac{d}{2}{/tex} = 0.01 m
Initial magnetic field B1 = 900 OT
Final magnetic field B2 = 600 OT
Time duration t = 3s
Emf induced in the coil, = -N{tex}\pi{/tex}r2B2 - B1
= -70 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 0.012 {tex}\times{/tex} (6000 - 9000)3 = 22 V
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Emf induced in the coil = 22
