subject: Physics
question_html: State Lenz's law. Prove that the charge induced is independent of time.
question_text: State Lenz's law. Prove that the charge induced is independent of time.
explanation: Relation between induced charge and change in magnetic flux. According to Faraday's law, the magnitude of induced emf, {tex}|\mathcal{E}|=\frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta t}{/tex} If R is the total resistance of the closed the induced current will be {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{\Delta q}{\Delta t}=\frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta t} \cdot \frac{1}{R}{/tex} Hence the charge induced in the circuit in time {tex}\Delta t{/tex} , {tex}\Delta q=\frac{\Delta \phi}{R}=\frac{\text { Net change in magnetic flux }}{\text { Resistance }}{/tex} Clearly, the induced charge depends on the net change in the magnetic flux and not on the time interval {tex}\Delta t{/tex} of the flux change. Thus the induced charge does not depend on the rate of change of magnetic flux.
solution: Relation between induced charge and change in magnetic flux. According to Faraday's law, the magnitude of induced emf, {tex}|\mathcal{E}|=\frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta t}{/tex} If R is the total resistance of the closed the induced current will be {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{\Delta q}{\Delta t}=\frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta t} \cdot \frac{1}{R}{/tex} Hence the charge induced in the circuit in time {tex}\Delta t{/tex} , {tex}\Delta q=\frac{\Delta \phi}{R}=\frac{\text { Net change in magnetic flux }}{\text { Resistance }}{/tex} Clearly, the induced charge depends on the net change in the magnetic flux and not on the time interval {tex}\Delta t{/tex} of the flux change. Thus the induced charge does not depend on the rate of change of magnetic flux.
solution_html:
Relation between induced charge and change in magnetic flux. According to Faraday's law, the magnitude of induced emf, {tex}|\mathcal{E}|=\frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta t}{/tex}
If R is the total resistance of the closed the induced current will be
{tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{\Delta q}{\Delta t}=\frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta t} \cdot \frac{1}{R}{/tex}
Hence the charge induced in the circuit in time {tex}\Delta t{/tex},
{tex}\Delta q=\frac{\Delta \phi}{R}=\frac{\text { Net change in magnetic flux }}{\text { Resistance }}{/tex}
Clearly, the induced charge depends on the net change in the magnetic flux and not on the time interval {tex}\Delta t{/tex} of the flux change. Thus the induced charge does not depend on the rate of change of magnetic flux.

subject: Physics
question_html: Distinguish between self-induction and mutual induction. Calculate self-inductance of a long solenoid of length l, number of turns N and radius r.
question_text: Distinguish between self-induction and mutual induction. Calculate self-inductance of a long solenoid of length l, number of turns N and radius r.
explanation: Self Inductance Mutual Inductance It is the phenomenon due to which a back emf is induced in a coil as a result of change in current flowing through it. It is the phenomenon due to which an induced emf is set up in a coil when the current flowing through a neighbouring coil changes. Induced emf {tex}\epsilon{/tex} = {tex}-L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} Induced emf {tex}\varepsilon_1=-M \frac{d I_2}{d t}{/tex} Self inductance of a coil depends on its dimensions. Mutual inductance of a pair of coils depends on their dimensions as well as relative orientation. Magnetic field inside solenoid = B = {tex}\mu_0 \frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{l}}{/tex} where, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{l}}{/tex} = number of turns per unit length Now, emf induced = E = NBA {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E = N {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\mu_0 \frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{i}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} R 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{~N}^2 \pi \mathrm{R}^2}{\mathrm{l}}{/tex} = Li where L = self inductance Hence, L = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 N^2 \pi R^2}{l}{/tex}
solution: Self Inductance Mutual Inductance It is the phenomenon due to which a back emf is induced in a coil as a result of change in current flowing through it. It is the phenomenon due to which an induced emf is set up in a coil when the current flowing through a neighbouring coil changes. Induced emf {tex}\epsilon{/tex} = {tex}-L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} Induced emf {tex}\varepsilon_1=-M \frac{d I_2}{d t}{/tex} Self inductance of a coil depends on its dimensions. Mutual inductance of a pair of coils depends on their dimensions as well as relative orientation. Magnetic field inside solenoid = B = {tex}\mu_0 \frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{l}}{/tex} where, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{l}}{/tex} = number of turns per unit length Now, emf induced = E = NBA {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E = N {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\mu_0 \frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{i}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} R 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{~N}^2 \pi \mathrm{R}^2}{\mathrm{l}}{/tex} = Li where L = self inductance Hence, L = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 N^2 \pi R^2}{l}{/tex}
solution_html:
Self Inductance Mutual Inductance
It is the phenomenon due to which a back emf is induced in a coil as a result of change in current flowing through it. It is the phenomenon due to which an induced emf is set up in a coil when the current flowing through a neighbouring coil changes.
Induced emf {tex}\epsilon{/tex} = {tex}-L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} Induced emf {tex}\varepsilon_1=-M \frac{d I_2}{d t}{/tex}
Self inductance of a coil depends on its dimensions. Mutual inductance of a pair of coils depends on their dimensions as well as relative orientation.

Magnetic field inside solenoid = B = {tex}\mu_0 \frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{l}}{/tex}
where, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{l}}{/tex} = number of turns per unit length
Now, emf induced = E = NBA
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E = N {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\mu_0 \frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{i}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex}R2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{~N}^2 \pi \mathrm{R}^2}{\mathrm{l}}{/tex} = Li
where L = self inductance
Hence, L = {tex}\frac{\mu_0 N^2 \pi R^2}{l}{/tex}
